row.reasoning:
The polypeptide is dominated by IPR051630 (Transcriptional Corepressor and Histone Demethylase family, residues 9–1410), which establishes membership in the lysine-specific demethylase 6 lineage that targets repressive histone marks. Its N-terminus contains IPR011990 (Tetratricopeptide-like helical domain superfamily, residues 67–444) with embedded IPR019734 (Tetratricopeptide repeat, residues 92–384). This tandem of TPR-like repeats forms an elongated solenoid that creates a versatile protein–protein interaction scaffold. Such scaffolds nucleate large transcriptional assemblies and provide docking sites for signal-activated transcription factors, thereby dictating which promoters are accessed.

The catalytic module is IPR003347 (JmjC domain, residues 1112–1275), the Fe(II)/2-oxoglutarate-dependent oxygenase fold that performs oxidative N-demethylation of methyl-lysine on histone tails. In this family, the chemistry corresponds to EC 1.14.11.68, removal of the trimethyl group from lysine 27 of histone H3, producing formaldehyde and succinate as reaction byproducts. Immediately adjacent structural supports include IPR048562 (Lysine-specific demethylase 6A/B-like, C-terminal helical domain, residues 1265–1320), which helps orient nucleosomal substrates and cofactors relative to the active site. The extreme C-terminus carries IPR046941 (Lysine-specific demethylase 6, GATA-like domain superfamily, residues 1330–1396) together with the lineage-specific IPR048560 (Lysine-specific demethylase 6A/B-like, GATA-like, residues 1337–1397). This Cys4 zinc-binding fold confers sequence- and context-dependent chromatin engagement at promoter regions. The ordered architecture—N-terminal TPR scaffold, central JmjC catalytic core, and C-terminal GATA-like chromatin anchor—causes selective occupancy of promoters and provides promoter-specific chromatin binding (GO:1990841) while positioning the active site to demethylate H3K27.

Catalytic removal of the H3K27me3/me2 repressive mark constitutes histone H3-K27 demethylation (GO:0071557). This epigenetic switch alleviates Polycomb-mediated repression and increases accessibility for the transcriptional machinery, yielding positive regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II (GO:0045944). The TPR scaffold enables recruitment by pathway-specific transcription factors and coactivators, allowing external cues to dictate target-gene choice. In hypoxia, association with hypoxia-inducible factors would direct demethylation at stress-responsive promoters, underpinning the cellular response to hypoxia (GO:0071456) and favoring survival programs that effect negative regulation of hypoxia-induced intrinsic apoptotic signaling pathway (GO:1903298). TGF-beta signaling components can use the same scaffold to retune promoters during the cellular response to transforming growth factor beta stimulus (GO:0071560). Nuclear receptor engagement explains the cellular response to vitamin D (GO:0071305), while vasoactive peptide pathways converge through promoter-bound transcription factors to generate cellular response to angiotensin (GO:1904385) and cellular response to endothelin (GO:1990859). Relief of H3K27-dependent repression on growth-associated genes provides a route to positive regulation of cell size (GO:0045793).

All steps of binding, catalysis, and regulation occur on nuclear chromatin, placing the protein in the nucleus (GO:0005634), more specifically at nuclear chromatin (GO:0000790). Mechanistically, the enzyme binds promoter-proximal nucleosomes via the C-terminal GATA-like domain and adjacent helices, recruits or is recruited by transcriptional regulators through the N-terminal TPR array, and uses the JmjC active site—with Fe(II), 2-oxoglutarate, and O2—to hydroxylate the ε-methyl group of H3K27me3, driving demethylation and transcriptional activation across signal-regulated gene programs.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A nuclear chromatin-anchored histone demethylase that targets promoter-proximal nucleosomes and removes the repressive trimethyl group from histone H3 lysine 27 via an Fe(II)/2‑oxoglutarate–dependent mechanism. An N-terminal tetratricopeptide repeat scaffold recruits signal-activated transcription factors and cofactors, while a C-terminal zinc-binding module secures promoter-specific chromatin engagement. By erasing H3K27 methylation, it lifts Polycomb repression to enhance RNA polymerase II transcription in response to hypoxia, TGF‑beta, vitamin D, angiotensin, and endothelin cues, promoting growth programs and limiting hypoxia-triggered apoptosis.

row.reasoning:
A long polypeptide (approx. 2.9 kDa in length) displays a domain architecture diagnostic of a chromatin-remodeling complex reader and recruiter. At the N-terminus, IPR038028 (Nucleosome-remodeling factor subunit BPTF family, residues 1–2910) defines the overall identity as the large targeting subunit of an ISWI-type nucleosome remodeling complex. Embedded within this framework, IPR018501 (DDT domain, residues 252–312) appears first, followed downstream by IPR028941 (WHIM2 domain, residues 468–534). The DDT–WHIM2 module is a hallmark of remodeler-targeting proteins; the DDT domain binds DNA/histone surfaces to dock on nucleosomes, while the WHIM2 domain cooperates with DDT to stabilize engagement of ISWI ATPases. Together, these domains cause the protein to recruit chromatin-remodeling activity to specific nucleosomal substrates.

A large central expanse (approximately residues 399–2799) is saturated with PHD-finger signatures: IPR011011 (Zinc finger, FYVE/PHD-type homologous superfamily, residues 399–2793), IPR013083 (Zinc finger, RING/FYVE/PHD-type homologous superfamily, residues 395–2799), IPR001965 (Zinc finger, PHD-type, residues 404–2791), IPR019787 (Zinc finger, PHD-finger, residues 402–2793), and IPR019786 (Zinc finger, PHD-type, conserved site, residues 405–2790). PHD fingers are precision readers of methyl-lysine states on histone H3 tails, especially H3K4me2/3. The multiplicity and breadth of these PHD-related signatures indicate one or more functional PHD modules embedded in a larger zinc-binding platform. This architecture directs the protein to methylated histone marks and endows it with selective, high-affinity histone-binding capacity.

At the C-terminus, IPR001487 (Bromodomain, residues 2800–2908) sits within IPR036427 (Bromodomain-like superfamily, residues 2800–2908) and carries IPR018359 (Bromodomain, conserved site, residues 2824–2881). Bromodomains bind acetyl-lysine marks on histone tails (for example on H4 or H3), providing an orthogonal recognition axis to the PHD module’s methyl-lysine readout. The juxtaposition of PHD-finger and bromodomain therefore enforces combinatorial recognition of methylated and acetylated histone states on the same nucleosome, transforming the protein into a logic gate that targets active or poised regulatory chromatin.

From this architecture, the molecular functions follow. The DDT–WHIM2 module plus reader domains cause chromatin binding (GO:0003682). The PHD array causes histone binding (GO:0042393) with specificity for methylated histone binding (GO:0035064). By recruiting and positioning an ISWI ATPase via the remodeler-recognition modules, the protein acts as a transcription coactivator (GO:0003713), not by catalyzing remodeling itself, but by docking the catalytic ISWI motor on appropriately marked nucleosomes to promote promoter/enhancer accessibility.

Such targeted remodeling is a core driver of developmental gene-expression programs. Combinatorial reading of H3K4 methylation and histone acetylation biases remodeling to regulatory regions of lineage-specifying genes, supporting roles in endoderm development (GO:0007492), the establishment of anterior/posterior pattern (GO:0009952), and embryonic placenta development (GO:0001892). These processes are sensitive to nucleosome positioning and enhancer-promoter configuration, which this reader–recruiter architecture directly modulates.

The domain composition mandates a nuclear residence, because all recognition modules are specialized for nucleosomal chromatin. Hence the protein localizes to the nucleus (GO:0005634), concentrated within the nucleoplasm (GO:0005654) at chromatin (GO:0000785), where it scans for compatible histone mark combinations.

Mechanistically, the DDT and WHIM2 domains engage nucleosomes and interface with ISWI ATPases, while the PHD finger binds H3K4me2/3 and the bromodomain binds acetylated histone tails. This multivalent binding stabilizes the complex on active or primed regulatory chromatin. Once docked, the associated ISWI subunit executes ATP-dependent nucleosome sliding/spacing, increasing accessibility of transcriptional start sites and enhancers to coactivators and the transcriptional machinery. In this context, the protein is expected to interact with ISWI ATPases such as SWI/SNF-related matrix-associated actin-dependent regulator of chromatin subfamily A members 1 and 5 (SMARCA1 and SMARCA5) via the DDT–WHIM2 module; with histone chaperones RBBP4 and RBBP7 that stabilize H3–H4 during remodeling; and directly with nucleosomal substrates containing histone H3 variants H3.1, H3.2, and H3.3 through its PHD/bromodomain readers. Additional remodeling cofactors, including Remodeling and spacing factor 1 and BPTF-associated chromatin complex component 1, likely assemble into a higher-order complex that tunes spacing outcomes, while Tyrosine-protein kinase BAZ1B, an ISWI partner, may modulate these interactions through phosphorylation-dependent signaling.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A nuclear chromatin reader and coactivator that targets an ISWI-type remodeling motor to specific nucleosomes by decoding histone marks. It uses a DDT–WHIM2 module to engage nucleosomes and interface with remodeler ATPases, PHD-finger modules to recognize methylated H3 tails, and a C‑terminal bromodomain to bind acetylated histones. This multivalent recognition stabilizes the remodeling complex on active or poised regulatory chromatin, promoting nucleosome sliding and spacing that enable transcriptional programs driving embryonic patterning, endoderm specification, and placental development.

row.protein_names: Solute carrier family 12 member 7 (Electroneutral potassium-chloride cotransporter 4) (K-Cl cotransporter 4)
row.protein_function: Mediates electroneutral potassium-chloride cotransport when activated by cell swelling (By similarity). May mediate K(+) uptake into Deiters' cells in the cochlea and contribute to K(+) recycling in the inner ear. Important for the survival of cochlear outer and inner hair cells and the maintenance of the organ of Corti. May be required for basolateral Cl(-) extrusion in the kidney and contribute to renal acidification (By similarity).
row.ppi_formatted:
- STE20/SPS1-related proline-alanine-rich protein kinase
- Serine/threonine-protein kinase WNK3
row.interpro_formatted:
- IPR000076: K/Cl co-transporter (family) [47-574]
- IPR004842: SLC12A transporter family (family) [46-1083]
- IPR004841: Amino acid permease/ SLC12A domain (domain) [124-695]
- IPR018491: SLC12A transporter, C-terminal (domain) [709-1083]
row.go_bp_leaf: GO:0006884 cell volume homeostasis
row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by SLC12A transporter signatures. IPR004842 (SLC12A transporter family, residues 46–1083) spans almost the entire polypeptide, marking it as a cation-chloride cotransporter built as a large multi-pass membrane protein. Within this framework, IPR000076 (K/Cl co-transporter family, residues 47–574) refines substrate specificity to the potassium–chloride branch of SLC12, embedding the determinants that coordinate K+ and Cl– in a coupled, electroneutral cycle. The membrane-embedded core is further defined by IPR004841 (Amino acid permease/ SLC12A domain, residues 124–695), which corresponds to the transmembrane bundle that forms the alternating-access ion translocation pathway. Downstream, IPR018491 (SLC12A transporter, C-terminal, residues 709–1083) denotes an extended cytosolic tail that functions as a regulatory platform for phosphorylation-dependent control and subunit assembly.

This ordered layout causes a specific molecular activity: the transmembrane core binds K+ and Cl– simultaneously and alternates access of the binding pocket across the membrane without net charge transfer, producing potassium:chloride symporter activity (GO:0015379). The large cytosolic N- and C-terminal regions surrounding the transport core provide sites for regulatory phosphorylation motifs and kinase docking sequences, enabling dynamic gating of transport in response to osmotic conditions.

Coupling K+ and Cl– flux in a 1:1 ratio provides a direct lever for osmotic balance. When cells swell, dephosphorylation of residues in the cytosolic tails typically activates the transporter, increasing KCl movement to drive water out and restore size. This places the protein squarely in cell volume homeostasis (GO:0006884). The same ion-moving mechanism naturally supports physiological roles such as K+ recycling across epithelial membranes in the inner ear and basolateral Cl– extrusion in kidney epithelia, both of which depend on electroneutral KCl translocation to shape transepithelial gradients without generating membrane depolarization.

The transmembrane-rich core (IPR004841 within the family-wide IPR004842 and K/Cl-specific IPR000076) necessitates residence in the plasma membrane as a multi-pass, integral component, where it can access extracellular and cytosolic ion pools. Thus the cellular component is best captured as integral component of membrane (GO:0016021) at the plasma membrane (GO:0005886).

The C-terminal regulatory domain (IPR018491) provides mechanistic context for interaction partners. STE20/SPS1-related proline-alanine-rich protein kinase (SPAK) is a canonical SLC12 regulator that docks via RFXV/I motifs on the cytosolic tails, enabling direct phosphorylation that suppresses K-Cl cotransport under isotonic conditions. Serine/threonine-protein kinase WNK3 acts upstream, sensing intracellular chloride and activating SPAK to maintain phosphorylation. Under cell swelling, reduced WNK signaling and heightened phosphatase action (e.g., PP1) dephosphorylate the tails, switching the transporter into an active state that mediates KCl movement for volume recovery. The transmembrane core executes alternating-access catalysis, while the cytosolic tails integrate kinase/phosphatase inputs to tune turnover rate, oligomer state, and membrane stability. In epithelia, scaffolds that concentrate SPAK/WNK at the basolateral membrane would further specialize the transporter for K+ recycling and Cl– extrusion required for organ-specific physiology.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A multi-pass plasma membrane cotransporter that moves K+ and Cl– together in an electroneutral 1:1 cycle. Its transmembrane core provides the alternating-access pathway, while extended cytosolic tails host regulatory phosphorylation sites that tune activity in response to osmotic signals. Activation during cell swelling enhances KCl flux to restore cell volume, and the same mechanism supports K+ recycling in the inner ear and basolateral Cl– extrusion in kidney epithelia. Kinase inputs from WNK–SPAK and counteracting phosphatases modulate its transport rate and membrane residency to match physiological demand.

row.subcellular_location: Cell outer membrane; Multi-pass membrane protein.

- IPR050298: Gram-negative bacterial outer membrane porin (family) [1-362]
row.reasoning:
A compact, outer-membrane beta-barrel architecture is evident from the InterPro signatures that blanket the sequence. The full-length IPR050298 (Gram-negative bacterial outer membrane porin, residues 1–362) identifies a classical porin scaffold destined for the bacterial cell envelope. Within this, IPR023614 (Porin domain superfamily, residues 22–362) and IPR033900 (Porin domain, Gram-negative type, residues 30–362) define the core beta-barrel that spans the outer membrane as multiple beta strands, a configuration consistent with the “multi-pass” designation for outer-membrane proteins. Two family-level classifiers, IPR001702 (Porin, Gram-negative type, residues 27–362) and IPR001897 (Porin, gammaproteobacterial, residues 22–289), specify the lineage and the porin fold in Salmonella-class Gammaproteobacteria. Embedded near the C-terminus is IPR013793 (Porin, Gram-negative type, conserved site, residues 314–330), a conserved motif corresponding to the inward-folded loop that forms the constriction zone (the “eyelet”) of the water-filled pore. The N- to mid-region family spans (IPR001897 to ~289) followed by the broader Gram-negative porin domain to 362 describe the ordered arrangement of an N-terminal signal-peptide–processed beta-barrel whose loop L3 forms the selectivity filter, while extracellular loops and periplasmic turns shape access and stability.

This domain architecture causes the protein to act as a passive channel. The beta-barrel produces an aqueous conduit; the conserved site at residues 314–330 forms the electrostatic constriction that sets size and charge selectivity. Such pores do not couple to energy sources and therefore mediate diffusion along concentration gradients. Consequently, the molecular function encompasses porin activity (GO:0015288) within the broader class of passive transmembrane transporter activity (GO:0022803). Trimerization and lateral packing with other outer-membrane proteins require multivalent contacts, aligning with protein binding (GO:0005515). The resulting flux of hydrophilic small molecules across the envelope situates the protein in transmembrane transport (GO:0055085) as the biological process, where its permeability channel supports nutrient influx and waste/antibiotic egress without direct substrate specificity.

Localization follows from the porin superfamily assignment and the multi-beta-strand barrel: these folds insert exclusively into the bacterial cell outer membrane. The signal-peptide–processed N-terminus and extensive beta-barrel domains culminating near residue 362 are hallmarks of integral components of the outer membrane, matching an integral component of membrane (GO:0016021) located at the cell outer membrane (GO:0009279).

Mechanistically, the protein likely forms a homotrimer in the outer membrane. Each monomer provides a ~16-stranded beta-barrel; loop L3 (314–330) dips into the lumen to create a narrow, electrostatically patterned eyelet that governs diffusion of small solutes. Extracellular loops confer subtle selectivity and may remodel under ionic strength or pH, slightly modulating conductance. This passive conduit thereby sets the baseline permeability of the Salmonella outer membrane.

The interaction landscape follows from its envelope role. Outer membrane protein A can act as a structural neighbor that ties the outer membrane to the peptidoglycan, stabilizing porin arrays and influencing channel density. Outer membrane protein TolC operates as a dedicated exit duct for efflux pumps; spatial partitioning between TolC channels and this porin would tune the balance between general diffusion and directed efflux, shaping antibiotic susceptibility. Outer membrane proteins W and X, as well as Ail and OmpX-like proteins, may co-cluster to form microdomains that regulate local curvature and porin insertion. Universal stress protein A likely coordinates stress-dependent remodeling of porin abundance, while Hfq and Ribonuclease E couple small RNA regulation and mRNA turnover to adjust channel levels under envelope stress. Lipid A biosynthesis acyltransferase tunes LPS acylation; altered LPS packing changes the dielectric and steric environment around the barrel, subtly affecting conductance and porin stability. Collectively, these interactions integrate structural, regulatory, and lipidomic controls to set outer-membrane permeability through this diffusion channel.

By this chain of inference—from the porin superfamily architecture (IPR023614, IPR033900) and Gram-negative/gammaproteobacterial family fingerprints (IPR001702, IPR001897, IPR050298) to the eyelet-defining conserved site (IPR013793)—the protein’s molecular function is a passive porin channel (GO:0015288; GO:0022803; GO:0005515), enabling transmembrane transport (GO:0055085) from its position as an integral component of the cell outer membrane (GO:0016021; GO:0009279).
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: An outer-membrane beta-barrel channel in Salmonella that assembles as a trimer to form water-filled pores permitting passive diffusion of small hydrophilic molecules across the envelope. A conserved constriction loop shapes size and charge selectivity, setting baseline permeability for nutrient uptake and waste or antibiotic flux. Its abundance and conductance are likely tuned by neighboring outer-membrane proteins, lipid A composition, and stress-responsive RNA-mediated regulation.

row.go_bp_leaf: GO:0048512 circadian behavior
row.reasoning:
The sequence features align with a class A G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) specialized for a neuromedin U (NMU)-like ligand. Three InterPro annotations overlap across the core of the protein: IPR000276 (G protein-coupled receptor, rhodopsin-like family, residues 59–354) encompasses the canonical class A GPCR scaffold; within it lies IPR017452 (GPCR, rhodopsin-like, 7TM domain, residues 74–346), which encodes the seven transmembrane helices and the conserved microswitch network (e.g., DRY at TM3 and NPxxY at TM7) that transmit ligand-induced conformational changes to intracellular G-protein coupling; superimposed on these is IPR005390 (Neuromedin U receptor family, residues 84–337), which specifies a peptide-recognition pocket tuned for neuromedin U–type neuropeptides. The ordered architecture—an extracellular N-terminus and loops shaping a peptide-binding vestibule, followed by a 7TM signal-transducing core—causes ligand-gated activation of heterotrimeric G proteins.

From this architecture, the molecular function is defined by two complementary activities. First, the family-specific IPR005390 signature constrains the ligand class to neuropeptides of the neuromedin U family, making the receptor a neuropeptide-responsive GPCR. This supports GO:0008188 neuropeptide receptor activity. Second, the rhodopsin-like 7TM core couples conformational change to G protein engagement, establishing GO:0004930 G protein-coupled receptor activity. The 7TM layout dictates a multi-pass membrane integration and an extracellular ligand-binding face.

The signaling consequences of neuromedin U GPCR activation are well aligned with modulators of rhythmic behaviors. In vertebrates, NMU signaling engages Gq/11 and/or Gi/o to control intracellular Ca2+ and cAMP, thereby tuning neuronal excitability and neuroendocrine outputs that shape daily activity rhythms. Thus, this receptor architecture mechanistically links neuropeptide detection to neuronal circuit modulation consistent with GO:0048512 circadian behavior. More generally, the chain of events—peptide binding, G protein engagement, and downstream second-messenger production—constitutes a canonical neuropeptide signaling pathway and a GPCR signaling cascade.

The structural logic of a 7TM receptor requires membrane residence. The transmembrane bundle encoded by IPR017452 positions the protein as a GO:0016021 integral component of membrane and, given its role in sensing extracellular neuropeptides and modulating behavior, specifically at the GO:0005886 plasma membrane. The experimentally observed “cell membrane; multi-pass” description matches this deduction and follows directly from the seven-helix core.

A mechanistic model emerges: the extracellular loops and N-terminus capture neuromedin U peptides; binding stabilizes an active-state 7TM conformation that exposes the intracellular cavity for heterotrimeric G proteins (likely Gαq/11 and/or Gαi/o with Gβγ). Activation triggers phospholipase C-β, IP3-mediated Ca2+ release, and/or inhibition of adenylyl cyclase, thereby adjusting neuronal firing and hormone release that entrain or modulate circadian locomotor and feeding behaviors. Desensitization proceeds via GRK phosphorylation and β-arrestin recruitment, leading to internalization and potential β-arrestin–mediated signaling.

Given this mechanism, plausible interaction partners include G protein subunits (GNAQ and GNA11 for Gq/11 coupling; GNAI2 as a potential Gi/o partner; GNB1 and GNG2 as common Gβγ components), the neuromedin U peptide ligand itself (NMU), desensitization machinery (GRK2, GRK6, ARRB1, ARRB2), and regulatory accelerators like RGS2 that tune signal duration in neurons governing circadian behavior in zebrafish.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A seven-transmembrane plasma-membrane receptor in zebrafish that detects neuromedin U–like neuropeptides at the extracellular surface and activates heterotrimeric G proteins inside the cell. This triggers second-messenger production, particularly Ca2+ mobilization via phospholipase C pathways and/or modulation of cAMP, to adjust neuronal excitability and neuroendocrine outputs that shape daily activity rhythms. Signaling is terminated by GRK- and β-arrestin–mediated desensitization and receptor endocytosis.

row.reasoning:
The polypeptide begins with IPR002861 (Reeler domain, residues 13–179) nested within IPR042307 (Reeler domain superfamily, residues 28–158). Reeler modules are extracytosolic binding folds that present structured surfaces for ligand or protein capture; their N-terminal placement implies a lumen-facing segment once the protein is inserted into a secretory-pathway membrane. This N-terminal block is encompassed by IPR051237 (Ferric-chelate Reductase and Defense family, residues 9–272), which functionally constrains the chemistry toward ferric-chelate reduction and positions the Reeler fold as part of a substrate-recognition apparatus for ferric complexes.

Immediately downstream, IPR005018 (DOMON domain, residues 216–331) appears. DOMON domains frequently bind redox-active ligands (e.g., heme or catecholic cofactors) and often act as electron-relay or substrate-docking platforms on the non-cytosolic side. Here, the adjacency of Reeler and DOMON creates a lumenal recognition-and-redox ensemble: Reeler shapes substrate capture, while DOMON helps poise the ferric chelate for electron acceptance.

The C-terminal half contains IPR006593 (Cytochrome b561/ferric reductase transmembrane, residues 335–534), a multi-pass membrane module that coordinates two b-type hemes via conserved histidines. This architecture conducts electrons from the cytosolic face to the lumenal face of the membrane. Multi-pass topology, together with the heme-based redox core, establishes a transmembrane electron-transfer pathway that terminates at the DOMON/Reeler platform to reduce ferric substrates bound in the lumenal compartment.

This ordered layout—lumen-facing capture modules (IPR002861 within IPR042307; both within IPR051237) leading into a cytochrome b561 redox conduit (IPR006593)—causally produces oxidoreductase activity on a metal substrate. The mechanistic outcome is reduction of Fe(III) to Fe(II), matching the molecular function formalized as GO:0016722 oxidoreductase activity, acting on metal ions. By generating Fe(II) within endosomes, the protein enables subsequent export to the cytosol by ferrous transporters (e.g., DMT1), thereby sustaining cellular iron supply; this places the protein squarely in GO:0006879 intracellular iron ion homeostasis.

Topology inferred from IPR006593 and the experimentally indicated multi-pass character demands a membrane-embedded state; hence GO:0016021 integral component of membrane. The chemistry—reducing ferric iron prior to its transport to the cytoplasm—locates the lumenal catalytic face in the endosomal system where transferrin-derived Fe(III) is released, pointing to GO:0010008 endosome membrane as the most specific cellular site.

Mechanistically, cytosolic ascorbate donates electrons to the cytosolic heme of the cytochrome b561 core, which then relays them across the bilayer to the lumenal heme. The DOMON/Reeler assembly captures ferric chelates and aligns them for electron transfer, yielding Fe(II) that is immediately available to ferrous transporters for export. This vectorial electron flow explains how the architecture enforces one-way ferric reduction at the endosome–cytosol interface.

The listed partners—mitochondrial ribosomal proteins uL23m, mL46, uL3m, uL16m, uL29m, uL22m, bL19m, uL13m, bL27m, and uS15m—suggest a physiological coupling between endosomal iron release and mitochondrial translation capacity. A plausible network is an iron-delivery metabolon at endosome–mitochondrion contact sites, where newly generated Fe(II) is preferentially funneled toward mitochondrial import pathways to support heme and iron–sulfur cofactor biogenesis that underpins mitochondrial ribosome assembly and function. Transient or adaptor-mediated associations with these ribosomal proteins would thus be expected when iron demand for mitochondrial protein synthesis is high. In parallel, direct collaboration with ferrous importers such as DMT1 (not listed here but mechanistically expected) would complete the reduction–transport handoff that maintains iron homeostasis in mouse cells.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A multi-pass endosomal membrane oxidoreductase in mouse that uses lumen-facing ligand-binding modules coupled to a cytochrome b561 redox core to reduce ferric iron to ferrous iron. Electrons flow from cytosolic donors across heme cofactors to a lumenal reaction site that captures ferric chelates, generating Fe2+ for export to the cytosol and sustaining cellular iron balance. By channeling reduced iron toward downstream transporters and likely to mitochondria via organelle contact sites, it supports iron-dependent biosynthesis and mitochondrial translation capacity.

row.protein_names: Pleiotrophin (PTN) (Heparin-binding brain mitogen) (HBBM) (Heparin-binding growth factor 8) (HBGF-8) (Heparin-binding growth-associated molecule) (HB-GAM) (Heparin-binding neutrophic factor) (HBNF) (Osteoblast-specific factor 1) (OSF-1)
row.protein_function: Secreted growth factor that mediates its signal through cell-surface proteoglycan and non-proteoglycan receptors (By similarity). Binds cell-surface proteoglycan receptor via their chondroitin sulfate (CS) groups (By similarity). Thereby regulates many processes like cell proliferation, cell survival, cell growth, cell differentiation and cell migration in several tissues namely neuron and bone. Also plays a role in synaptic plasticity and learning-related behavior by inhibiting long-term synaptic potentiation. Binds PTPRZ1, leading to neutralization of the negative charges of the CS chains of PTPRZ1, inducing PTPRZ1 clustering, thereby causing the dimerization and inactivation of its phosphatase activity leading to increased tyrosine phosphorylation of each of the PTPRZ1 substrates like ALK or AFAP1L2 in order to activate the PI3K-AKT pathway. Through PTPRZ1 binding controls oligodendrocyte precursor cell differentiation by enhancing the phosphorylation of AFAP1L2 in order to activate the PI3K-AKT pathway. Forms a complex with PTPRZ1 and integrin alpha-V/beta-3 (ITGAV:ITGB3) that stimulates endothelial cell migration through SRC dephosphorylation and activation that consequently leads to ITGB3 'Tyr-773' phosphorylation (By similarity). In adult hippocampus promotes dendritic arborization, spine development, and functional integration and connectivity of newborn granule neurons through ALK by activating AKT signaling pathway. Binds GPC2 and chondroitin sulfate proteoglycans (CSPGs) at the neuron surface, leading to abrogation of binding between PTPRS and CSPGs and neurite outgrowth promotion (By similarity). Binds SDC3 and mediates bone formation by recruiting and attaching osteoblasts/osteoblast precursors to the sites for new bone deposition (By similarity). Binds ALK and promotes cell survival and cell proliferation through MAPK pathway activation (By similarity). Inhibits proliferation and enhances differentiation of neural stem cells by inhibiting FGF2-induced fibroblast growth factor receptor signaling pathway. Mediates regulatory mechanisms in normal hemostasis and in hematopoietic regeneration and in maintaining the balance of myeloid and lymphoid regeneration. In addition may play a role in the female reproductive system, auditory response and the progesterone-induced decidualization pathway.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is diagnostic of the Pleiotrophin/Midkine family. A family-level signature IPR000762 occupies residues 12–164, indicating a secreted, basic growth factor organized as two compact domains stabilized by disulfides. The N-terminal module is delineated by IPR020089 (Pleiotrophin/Midkine, N-terminal domain, 34–95) and embedded within IPR037122 (N-terminal domain superfamily, 33–96). The C-terminal module is defined by IPR020090 (C-terminal domain, 96–158) within IPR038130 (C-terminal domain superfamily, 99–141). IPR020091 (disulphide-rich domain superfamily, 34–141) spans the core, accounting for multiple conserved cysteines that rigidify the fold for extracellular stability. A conserved basic patch captured by IPR020092 (heparin-binding growth factor, conserved site, 45–109) supplies the cationic surface necessary for high-affinity glycosaminoglycan engagement. This ordered two-domain, disulfide-rich architecture generates elongated basic grooves that bind sulfated polysaccharides.

Such a surface composition causes glycosaminoglycan binding and, specifically, heparin binding. By coupling to cell-surface chondroitin sulfate and heparan sulfate proteoglycans, this fold operates as an extracellular receptor ligand. It binds signaling receptors and co-receptors simultaneously, enabling receptor clustering or cross-bridging. These physical principles define molecular functions as growth factor activity, receptor ligand activity, signaling receptor binding, glycosaminoglycan binding, heparin binding, and, given the documented ternary complexes with αVβ3, integrin binding.

Secreted localization follows from the extracellularly stabilized, disulfide-rich organization and reliance on proteoglycan engagement. The protein therefore resides in the extracellular region and adheres to the extracellular matrix via glycosaminoglycans. Proteoglycan-rich synaptic niches, particularly the hippocampal Schaffer collateral–CA1 synapse, are natural sites for activity where chondroitin sulfate proteoglycans and receptor tyrosine phosphatases concentrate.

This ligand’s mode of action is to modulate receptor phosphorylation states by proteoglycan-dependent clustering. Binding to chondroitin sulfate chains on receptor-type protein tyrosine phosphatase zeta induces receptor clustering and phosphatase inactivation, which increases tyrosine phosphorylation of its substrates and activates downstream PI3K–AKT signaling. Direct binding to receptor tyrosine kinases such as ALK further amplifies trophic signaling through MAPK and AKT pathways. In endothelial contexts, assembly of a complex with PTPRZ1 and integrin αVβ3 promotes SRC activation and integrin phosphorylation, driving migration. Through these circuit-level effects, the ligand shapes multiple biological processes. In the nervous system, potentiation of AKT/MAPK while silencing phosphatase activity explains dendrite arborization (GO:0140059) and positive regulation of dendrite development (GO:1900006), contributes to learning (GO:0007612) and memory (GO:0007613), and enables negative regulation of long-term synaptic potentiation (GO:1900272) at the Schaffer collateral–CA1 synapse. Regulation of myelination (GO:0031641) follows from controlling oligodendrocyte precursor differentiation via proteoglycan-PTPR signaling. As a secreted mitogen and motogen, it supports tissue regeneration (GO:0042246), positive regulation of hepatocyte proliferation (GO:2000347), and regulation of hemopoiesis (GO:1903706). Its chemotactic influence on leukocytes, mediated by remodeling of the extracellular matrix and integrin signaling, explains positive regulation of leukocyte chemotaxis (GO:0002690) and leukocyte chemotaxis involved in inflammatory response (GO:0002232). By controlling trophic cues to stem and progenitor cells presented on proteoglycans, it mediates regulation of stem cell population maintenance (GO:2000036), positive regulation of stem cell differentiation (GO:2000738), and negative regulation of neuroblast proliferation (GO:0007406). In mineralized tissues, matrix-tethered signaling promotes bone mineralization (GO:0030282) and its positive regulation (GO:0030501). Its presence in reproductive tissues and ECM niches coherent with proteoglycan scaffolds supports roles in the estrous cycle (GO:0044849), oogenesis (GO:0048477), decidualization (GO:0046697), and response to auditory stimulus (GO:0010996).

This mechanistic framework predicts specific interaction partners. Receptor-type tyrosine-protein phosphatase zeta and beta are direct proteoglycan-modified receptors susceptible to ligand-induced clustering and inactivation. ALK tyrosine kinase receptor is an activating binding partner that transduces survival and growth signals. Syndecan-3 presents glycosaminoglycan chains to concentrate the ligand at the cell surface and couple it to cytoskeletal signaling. Midkine, a paralog with a homologous domain layout, likely co-occupies glycosaminoglycan sites and can form mixed assemblies affecting receptor choice. Hepatoma-derived growth factor, SPARC, and SPARC-like protein 1 are matricellular factors that co-habit the extracellular matrix and modulate integrin availability, thereby shaping ternary complex formation. Plexin-B2 offers an axon-guidance receptor platform in neuronal tissues for cross-talk with integrin/SRC signaling. Cystathionine gamma-lyase, by producing extracellular H2S in certain contexts, could post-translationally modulate receptor thiols and tune the efficacy of phosphatase inactivation, functionally linking metabolic status to ligand signaling.

In sum, the two-domain, disulfide-rich, glycosaminoglycan-binding architecture causes the protein to act as an extracellular growth factor and receptor ligand tethered to the extracellular matrix and synaptic proteoglycan scaffolds. By clustering phosphatases, engaging receptor tyrosine kinases, and partnering with integrins, it reprograms local phosphorylation states to drive dendrite development, learning and memory modulation, myelination, hematopoiesis, regeneration, bone mineralization, immune chemotaxis, stem cell regulation, and reproductive tissue remodeling, with a focal site of action at hippocampal Schaffer collateral–CA1 synapses.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A secreted, disulfide-stabilized, glycosaminoglycan-binding growth factor that anchors to the extracellular matrix and synaptic proteoglycan scaffolds to act as a receptor ligand. By binding chondroitin/heparan sulfate proteoglycans, clustering receptor-type protein tyrosine phosphatases, engaging receptor tyrosine kinases, and assembling ternary complexes with integrins, it elevates local tyrosine phosphorylation and activates AKT/MAPK pathways. This mechanism drives dendritic arborization and synaptic plasticity control in the hippocampus, regulates myelination and stem/progenitor cell behavior, supports hematopoietic balance and tissue regeneration, promotes bone mineralization, orchestrates leukocyte chemotaxis in inflammation, and contributes to reproductive and auditory system physiology.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by a single SIS-fold carbohydrate-phosphate isomerase module. The N- to C-terminal region is annotated as IPR050099 (SIS family GmhA and DiaA subfamilies, residues 7–194), IPR004515 (Phosphoheptose isomerase family, residues 3–192), IPR001347 (SIS domain, residues 29–198), and the encompassing IPR046348 (SIS domain superfamily, residues 6–193). In parallel, membership in IPR035461 (GmhA/DiaA family, residues 12–190) specifies the subfamily associated with phosphoheptose isomerization and, in some homologs, initiator-association functions. The near-complete overlap of these entries across 3–198 indicates a compact enzyme built around one SIS domain without additional catalytic appendages. This fold coordinates sugar phosphates via a basic pocket that anchors the phosphate and a constellation of polar side chains that promote proton transfer, driving aldose–ketose interconversion through a cis-enediol(ate) intermediate. In GmhA-like members, this architecture assembles as a homo-tetramer to shape the active pocket and enforce stereochemical outcome.

This domain arrangement causally confers D-sedoheptulose 7-phosphate isomerase chemistry. The SIS active site positions the C1–C2 atoms of sedoheptulose 7-phosphate for proton abstraction and re-protonation, reshuffling the carbonyl to yield D-glycero-D-manno-heptose 7-phosphate. The defined GmhA/DiaA-family pocket explains stringent substrate recognition and the lack of activity toward L-galacto-heptulose, L-galacto-heptulose 7-phosphate, or D-manno-heptulose: the phosphate clamp and stereospecific hydrogen-bond network select the D-glycero-D-manno configuration and reject mismatched ring geometries. This molecular logic directly matches GO:0008968 D-sedoheptulose 7-phosphate isomerase activity.

By generating D-glycero-D-manno-heptose 7-phosphate, the enzyme commits carbon flux into the heptose branch of nucleotide-sugar biosynthesis that feeds bacterial surface glycans. This places the protein squarely in GO:2001061 D-glycero-D-manno-heptose 7-phosphate biosynthetic process. In Campylobacter, these heptose donors are incorporated into surface polysaccharides, including capsular structures; therefore, the enzyme’s activity propagates to GO:0045227 capsule polysaccharide biosynthetic process by supplying the essential heptose precursor pool.

The SIS-only architecture lacks signal peptides, transmembrane helices, or secretion motifs, pointing to a soluble enzyme operating in the cytosol. Sugar-phosphate isomerases of this kind carry out central metabolic conversions before nucleotide activation and glycosyltransfer, which occur in the cytoplasm. Hence, the most specific localization is the GO:0005737 cytoplasm.

Mechanistically, the SIS fold binds sedoheptulose 7-phosphate via the phosphate-binding cradle, enforces ring opening, and uses a catalytic base/acid pair to form and collapse a cis-enediol(ate) intermediate with product-directed stereocontrol. Tetramerization completes the active site architecture and stabilizes transition states, ensuring high specificity for the D-glycero-D-manno product.

From this position at the pathway entrance, the enzyme likely functions within a metabolon that channels intermediates to downstream heptose-modifying enzymes. Immediate neighbors would include D-glycero-alpha-D-manno-heptose 7-phosphate kinase (phosphorylating the 1-position), D-glycero-alpha-D-manno-heptose-1,7-bisphosphate 7-phosphatase (removing the 7-phosphate), the bifunctional protein HldE (kinase/adenylyltransferase that activates heptose-1-P), and D-glycero-alpha-D-manno-heptose 1-phosphate guanylyltransferase (for GDP activation), as well as GDP-D-glycero-alpha-D-manno-heptose dehydrogenase that adjusts redox state en route to GDP-heptose donors. Downstream assembly with sugar transferase and methyltransferase family proteins would connect the nucleotide-heptose to capsule biosynthesis. The presence of dTDP-4-dehydrorhamnose 3,5-epimerase and HisB suggests broader glycan-biosynthetic and metabolic neighborhood effects within the cytoplasmic glycosylation hub. Finally, the GmhA/DiaA family linkage raises the possibility of transient association with the chromosomal replication initiator protein DnaA, allowing growth-state cues to modulate flux into envelope glycan biosynthesis.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A cytosolic sugar-phosphate isomerase that uses a SIS-fold active site to convert sedoheptulose 7-phosphate into the heptose-7-phosphate intermediate that seeds nucleotide-heptose biosynthesis. By stereoselectively generating the D-glycero-D-manno configuration through a cis-enediol mechanism, it commits flux into heptose donors used for capsular polysaccharide assembly. The enzyme likely operates within a cytoplasmic metabolon that includes heptose kinases, phosphatases, and nucleotide transferases, with potential regulatory coupling to replication initiation through a DiaA-like interface.

A coherent extracellular-to-cytosolic receptor architecture emerges from the domain order. The N-terminus displays the Leucine-rich repeat domain superfamily IPR032675 from residues 34–407, within which a discrete LRR repeat IPR001611 lies between 288–332. Immediately downstream sits a Malectin domain IPR021720 from 422–606. This arrangement creates an extended ectodomain capable of capturing macromolecular ligands: LRR motifs present solvent-exposed β-strands optimized for protein/peptide epitope binding, while the malectin fold forms a carbohydrate-recognition module for glycans or cell-wall-derived saccharides. A short segment after the malectin domain is poised to contain a single transmembrane helix, consistent with a single-pass type I topology in which the LRR–malectin ectodomain is apoplasmic and the kinase is cytosolic.

The cytosolic region is defined by the bilobal protein kinase core IPR000719 (687–988) overlapping the serine-threonine/tyrosine-protein kinase catalytic domain IPR001245 (691–979) and the protein kinase-like domain superfamily IPR011009 (664–982). An embedded active site signature IPR008271 (834–846) marks the catalytic center that coordinates ATP and the phosphoacceptor during transfer, enforcing serine/threonine-directed chemistry. Envelopment within the LRR Receptor-Like Ser/Thr Protein Kinase family IPR051824 (39–991) ties these modules into a canonical plant receptor-like kinase (RLK) scaffold: an extracellular recognition platform coupled through a single transmembrane segment to a cytosolic serine/threonine kinase.

This architecture causes receptor serine/threonine kinase activity by design. The LRR and malectin domains bind apoplastic ligands—peptides, glycoproteins, or polysaccharide fragments—stabilizing receptor complexes that transmit conformational change across the membrane. The cytosolic kinase then uses ATP to phosphorylate substrates on serine/threonine residues, implementing GO:0004702 receptor serine/threonine kinase activity, supported by ATP binding (GO:0005524) and carbohydrate binding (GO:0030246) mediated by the malectin fold.

Signal initiation at the cell surface propagates into a phosphorylation cascade, fulfilling protein phosphorylation (GO:0006468) and the broader transmembrane receptor protein serine/threonine kinase signaling pathway (GO:0007178). The presence of a malectin domain in the ectodomain steers ligand preference toward glycans or cell-wall–derived patterns (e.g., β-1,3-glucans), which aligns with defensive roles in plant innate immunity, specifically defense response to fungus (GO:0050832), because β-glucans are abundant fungal cell-wall components.

Cellular localization follows from topology and interaction context. The single-pass type I build places the receptor at the plasma membrane, consistent with GO:0005886 plasma membrane. Enrichment at cell-cell junctions is implied by the LRR–malectin RLK’s role in surveilling the apoplast and by known partitioning of immune receptors into nanodomains; the GO:0009506 plasmodesma term is consistent with a subpopulation functioning at plasmodesmal neck regions to regulate intercellular connectivity during stress. Downstream signaling targets transcriptional programs, explaining detectable signaling in or toward the GO:0005634 nucleus.

Interaction candidates help refine mechanism. Tetraspanin-3 and Tetraspanin-16 scaffold plasma-membrane nanodomains and plasmodesmata microdomains; their engagement would cluster the receptor into signaling platforms that boost ligand sensitivity and control access to plasmodesmata. Glucan endo-1,3-β-glucosidase 10 remodels β-1,3-glucans in the apoplast; association with this enzyme would modulate the availability or form of glucan-derived elicitors sensed by the malectin-containing ectodomain, tuning defense activation and plasmodesmal closure via callose dynamics. A UDP-glycosyltransferase superfamily protein could alter glycosylation states of ligands or co-receptors, refining receptor affinity and signaling duration. AT3g15480/MJK13_14 likely acts as a substrate or adaptor phosphorylated by the cytosolic kinase to relay signals to cytoplasmic and nuclear effectors.

Together, the LRR–malectin ectodomain binds glycan-rich or glycosylated patterns at the apoplast, tetraspanins assemble the receptor into PM/plasmodesmal signaling hubs, and the cytosolic serine/threonine kinase domain converts ligand recognition into phosphorylation cascades that culminate in defense transcription and dynamic regulation of plasmodesmata permeability.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A single-pass cell-surface receptor kinase in Arabidopsis whose extracellular leucine-rich repeats and malectin fold bind peptide and glycan cues at the apoplast. Ligand engagement within tetraspanin-organized membrane microdomains activates the cytosolic serine/threonine kinase, driving phosphorylation cascades that adjust defense gene expression and regulate plasmodesmal connectivity, particularly in response to fungal cell-wall–derived glucans.

row.reasoning:
The polypeptide is defined by IPR010335 (Mesothelin family, residues 1–624), a precursor architecture that generates two products: an N-terminal soluble ligand and a C-terminal glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI)-anchored cell-surface glycoprotein. Embedded within this, IPR026664 (Stereocilin-related family, residues 40–598) denotes a long, extracellular adhesive-type region that forms an extended binding surface. The N-to-C layout—signal-bearing N-terminus, a large extracellular binding region (40–598), and a C-terminal segment competent for GPI addition—directs a biosynthetic route through the early secretory pathway with remodeling in the Golgi apparatus, followed by either (i) GPI anchoring to the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane or (ii) proteolytic release of the N-terminal moiety to the extracellular milieu. This domain architecture conditions the molecule for protein–protein interactions rather than catalysis, matching GO:0005515 protein binding as the molecular function: the elongated, glycosylated ectodomain provides a multivalent platform to engage other cell-surface and secreted proteins.

From this binding capacity, two distinct biological roles follow. First, the GPI-anchored form concentrates in membrane microdomains and promotes cell–cell and cell–matrix adhesion by docking to complementary extracellular partners; this activity operationalizes GO:0007155 cell adhesion. Second, the soluble N-terminal fragment functions as a paracrine factor that enhances megakaryocytic colony formation, placing the protein within GO:0030219 megakaryocyte differentiation. The GPI anchor enforces an outer-leaflet orientation without a cytosolic tail, so signaling arises indirectly by clustering co-receptors and organizing nanoscale complexes rather than by direct intracellular signaling.

The secreted fragment resides in the extracellular space, consistent with GO:0005615 extracellular space. The membrane-tethered form partitions to the plasma membrane as an anchored component (GO:0031225) of the cell surface (GO:0005886 plasma membrane), and the maturation steps (signal peptide cleavage, GPI attachment, glycan remodeling) require passage through the Golgi apparatus (GO:0005794 Golgi apparatus).

Given this mechanism, plausible partners are high-molecular-weight mucins and raft-associated receptors. Direct adhesive ligation to Mucin 16 explains heterotypic binding that strengthens cell attachment; association with Mucin-1 may modulate the viscoelastic glycocalyx. Co-clustering with Uroplakin-3b and Prostate stem cell antigen—both GPI-anchored—would nucleate raft microdomains that tune signaling thresholds. Engagement of Epidermal growth factor receptor suggests that mesothelin-driven clustering can potentiate ligand-dependent EGFR signaling. Contacts with T-cell surface glycoprotein CD3 zeta chain and TNFRSF9 (4-1BB) point to immunological synapse modulation by reorganizing receptor topography at the interface. Glutamate carboxypeptidase 2 in the same microenvironment could reshape the pericellular peptide landscape, indirectly influencing receptor occupancy. Overall, the stereocilin-related ectodomain generates adhesion and co-receptor scaffolding, while the secreted fragment acts as a megakaryocyte-potentiating cue.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A secreted–membrane dual-function glycoprotein processed in the Golgi that yields a GPI-anchored cell-surface form for adhesion and co-receptor clustering, and a soluble N-terminal factor that potentiates megakaryocyte development. The elongated extracellular region mediates high-affinity protein binding to mucins and other surface receptors, organizing raft microdomains that modulate signaling (including EGFR) at the plasma membrane, while the released fragment acts in the extracellular space to enhance megakaryocyte colony formation.

row.go_bp_leaf: GO:0030198 extracellular matrix organization
row.reasoning:
The polypeptide begins with IPR039861 (Interphotoreceptor matrix proteoglycan family, residues 1–429), a lineage-defining signature that specifies a retina-specialized proteoglycan whose N-terminal region typically contains Ser-Gly dipeptides for glycosaminoglycan attachment and basic clusters that engage sulfated polysaccharides. Embedded within and extending beyond this region is IPR000082 (SEA domain, residues 235–693), corroborated by IPR036364 (SEA domain superfamily, residues 238–686). SEA modules are classical mucin-like domains that undergo autoproteolytic or regulated proteolytic processing and carry dense O-glycosylation, conferring protease resistance and promoting release or rearrangement of ectodomains within extracellular matrices. The ordered architecture—an N-terminal family-specific, GAG-anchoring segment followed by an extended SEA module—creates a secreted, highly glycosylated scaffold optimized to bind anionic glycosaminoglycans and to assemble into insoluble extracellular networks.

This domain layout lacks catalytic motifs, steering the interpretation toward a non-enzymatic, adhesive and structural role. The family-defining N-terminus provides polyvalent binding sites for chondroitin sulfate and heparan sulfate, as well as affinity to hyaluronan; the SEA domain contributes both mechanical resilience and proteolytic processing that stabilizes or modulates matrix incorporation. Collectively, this architecture causes direct glycosaminoglycan binding—captured by glycosaminoglycan binding (GO:0005539), heparin binding (GO:0008201), and hyaluronic acid binding (GO:0005540)—and confers a load-bearing role consistent with extracellular matrix structural constituent (GO:0005201).

By crosslinking hyaluronan backbones with sulfated proteoglycans, the protein builds and stabilizes the dense extracellular network between photoreceptor cells and the retinal pigment epithelium. This activity implements extracellular matrix organization (GO:0030198) by generating a persistent, insoluble scaffold that constrains diffusion, presents binding surfaces, and maintains tissue architecture specific to the visual system.

The secretion of this proteoglycan into the matrix compartment surrounding the photoreceptor outer segments, together with evidence of synthesis in the inner segment and deposition around the ciliary outer segment, places the protein in the interphotoreceptor matrix (GO:0033165). As a secreted matrix constituent, it also resides in the extracellular matrix (GO:0031012) and extracellular region (GO:0005576). The SEA-driven processing supports accumulation and retention within this extracellular niche while allowing remodeling.

Mechanistically, the protein likely polymerizes a macromolecular mesh by multivalent GAG engagement and by forming noncovalent complexes after SEA-domain processing. Within this scaffold, it can dock soluble retinoid carrier proteins, thereby shaping retinoid trafficking pathways across the interphotoreceptor space. The most direct partner is Retinol-binding protein 3, which would be retained within the matrix through polyvalent interactions. Additional partners are expected to include other interphotoreceptor proteoglycans (e.g., IMPG2) and large matrix components such as perlecan and versican, potentially stabilized by hyaluronan link proteins, together producing a composite lattice that positions ligands and regulates diffusion in the photoreceptor milieu.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A secreted, highly glycosylated extracellular scaffold that binds hyaluronan and sulfated glycosaminoglycans to assemble a resilient matrix surrounding photoreceptor cilia and outer segments. Proteolytic processing within a mucin-like module stabilizes its incorporation into the insoluble meshwork, where it crosslinks polysaccharides, organizes the intercellular space, and provides docking sites for soluble ligands that support photoreceptor function.

row.go_bp_leaf: GO:0009651 response to salt stress
row.reasoning:
Two InterPro signatures define the architecture. An N-terminal IPR025279 (Stress response protein NST1 family) spans residues 6–160 and sits entirely within a much larger IPR051195 (Fungal stress response NST1 family) that extends from residues 13–1134, almost the full length of the polypeptide. This nested arrangement indicates a large, fungal-specific scaffold in which a conserved N-terminal module nucleates interactions while the extended surrounding region provides additional low-specificity binding surface rather than enzymatic chemistry. The absence of catalytic or transmembrane domains and the prevalence of a family signature associated with stress regulators point toward a non-enzymatic, multivalent binder. This architecture directly produces a capability for broad protein association—formalized as GO:0005515 protein binding—because extended, likely disordered segments enable simultaneous recruitment of multiple partners.

In Saccharomyces cerevisiae, stress-response scaffolds use such multivalency to assemble regulatory complexes that tune osmoadaptation. Assembling inhibitory complexes in basal conditions would dampen the expression and trafficking of salt-tolerance effectors, thereby lowering the threshold for salt sensitivity. This causal role belongs to the process defined as GO:0009651 response to salt stress, specifically as a negative modulator of tolerance. The partner repertoire reinforces this function: engagement with Protein CAF40 positions the scaffold to connect to the CCR4–NOT deadenylase, promoting mRNA silencing of stress-response genes; association with General negative regulator of transcription subunit 3 adds a transcriptional repression arm that further reduces production of tolerance-promoting proteins. Contacts with AP-2 complex subunit mu and Tubulin beta chain provide a cytoskeleton-coupled endocytic route to remove membrane transporters or signaling receptors that would otherwise enhance survival under high salt. The two uncharacterized proteins YNL089C and YKL075C plausibly serve as adaptors that stabilize or target these assemblies to specific cortical sites. The NST1 core likely pairs with an accessory factor such as MSL1 to build the complete inhibitory hub.

Because the entire architecture is soluble, lacks signal peptides and transmembranes, and functionally cooperates with cytosolic repressors and cytoskeleton/endocytic machinery, the operational locale is the cytoplasm, i.e., GO:0005737 cytoplasm. Mechanistically, the protein acts as a cytoplasmic organizer: its N-terminal conserved module seeds complex formation, its extended regions dock repressors and endocytic adaptors, and the assembled hub concurrently represses mRNA output and accelerates internalization of tolerance-related proteins. Upon salt stress, this hub would be expected to remodel—potentially via phosphorylation—reducing its inhibitory hold so that osmoprotective programs can proceed. In the absence of such remodeling, its constitutive assembly with MSL1, CAF40, the transcriptional repressor, AP-2 mu, and tubulin would causally decrease salt tolerance.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A large cytoplasmic scaffold in baker’s yeast that assembles inhibitory complexes controlling osmotic adaptation. By recruiting mRNA-silencing and transcriptional repression factors together with endocytic and cytoskeletal components, it lowers the abundance and surface persistence of proteins that promote survival in high salt. Partnering with an accessory factor, it coordinates these modules to dampen salt-tolerance pathways under basal conditions and likely remodels under stress to release this restraint.

row.go_bp_leaf: GO:0007165 signal transduction
row.reasoning:
An N-terminal family signature, IPR017047 (Cripto family, residues 6–188), spans almost the entire ectodomain, marking this protein as an EGF-CFC scaffold specialized for growth-factor-mediated signaling at the cell surface. Within this envelope lie two hallmark modules in ordered succession: a compact IPR000742 EGF-like domain (residues 95–106) followed immediately by IPR019011 CFC (Cripto/FRL-1/Cryptic) domain (residues 115–149). This juxtaposition creates a disulfide-rich, extracellular binding surface (EGF-like) physically coupled to a CFC module that is known to engage receptor ectodomains. Such an architecture causes the protein to function as an extracellular co-receptor: the EGF-like fold provides a ligand- or receptor-contacting interface, while the CFC domain mediates docking to receptor serine/threonine kinases, enabling assembly of an active signaling complex rather than catalyzing intracellular reactions.

Because the functional core is entirely extracellular and lacks cytosolic catalytic motifs, the protein must operate at the cell surface. The experimentally observed localization to the cell membrane aligns with the architectural expectation for an EGF-CFC co-receptor presented on the plasma membrane, consistent with GO:0005886 plasma membrane. Acting at the surface, the EGF-like/CFC tandem promotes receptor clustering and stabilizes ligand-receptor cooperativity, which is the essence of coreceptor activity. This yields molecular functions formalized as GO:0015026 coreceptor activity, underpinned by GO:0005102 signaling receptor binding through the CFC-contacted receptor ectodomains, and GO:0019838 growth factor binding through the EGF-like fold.

By enforcing productive receptor-ligand complex formation, the protein initiates intracellular signaling cascades that change gene expression. This behavior constitutes GO:0007165 signal transduction. Within the EGF-CFC family, the geometry of the EGF-like and CFC modules is tuned to engage TGF-beta family pathways, notably the Nodal axis: the CFC domain binds type I/II receptor ectodomains while the EGF-like fold coordinates the Nodal-class ligand, together triggering receptor activation and downstream SMAD-mediated transcription. Such activation in early embryonic contexts directs epiblast cell fate choices and promotes mesoderm specification.

Mechanistically, the protein most plausibly operates as a membrane-tethered extracellular co-receptor that captures a Nodal-class ligand via the EGF-like module and simultaneously engages type I (ALK4/ALK7) and type II (ActRIIA/ActRIIB) receptor ectodomains via the CFC domain. This bimodal tethering nucleates a signaling complex at the plasma membrane, enabling receptor trans-phosphorylation and SMAD2/3 activation. Likely interaction partners therefore include the NODAL growth factor, the type I receptor ACVR1B (ALK4) and its type II counterparts ACVR2A and ACVR2B; GDF3 can serve as a ligand partner in Nodal-like assemblies, while LEFTY proteins could act as competitive antagonists that bind the same extracellular interfaces to dampen the pathway.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A cell-surface, membrane-tethered co-receptor that uses an extracellular EGF-like module adjacent to a CFC module to capture Nodal-class growth factors and engage their receptor ectodomains. By nucleating the receptor–ligand complex at the plasma membrane, it triggers downstream SMAD signaling that guides early embryonic cell-fate decisions, particularly those leading toward mesoderm formation.

row.protein_function: The major shell protein of the carboxysome, a polyhedral inclusion where RuBisCO (ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase, ccbL-ccbS) is sequestered. Assembles into hexamers which make sheets that form the facets of the polyhedral carboxysome. The shell probably limits the diffusion of CO(2) into and out of the carboxysome (Probable). Molecular modeling shows the central pore of this protein is selectively permeable to anions such as HCO(3) rather than CO(2) or O(2) (Probable). There are estimated to be 2970 CsoS1A/CsoS1C proteins per carboxysome (the proteins differ by only 1 residue) (Ref.5).

row.reasoning:
The polypeptide is almost entirely covered by bacterial microcompartment (BMC) shell signatures. The core fold is defined by IPR000249 (Bacterial microcompartment domain, residues 7–84), refined by IPR044872 (CcmK/CsoS1, BMC domain, residues 8–93) and embedded within IPR037233 (CcmK-like superfamily, residues 6–98) and IPR050575 (Bacterial microcompartment shell family, residues 8–96). A conserved-site motif, IPR020808 (Bacterial microcompartments protein, conserved site, residues 25–44), marks pore-lining residues at the center of the canonical BMC-H hexamer. This architecture causes each subunit to assemble into hexamers with a single axial pore; the hexamers tessellate into planar sheets that make the facets of a polyhedral shell. The conserved-site placement at 25–44 is positioned to tune the electrostatics and diameter of the pore, which explains selective permeability to anions such as HCO3− while disfavoring neutral gases like CO2 or O2.

This domain layout is characteristic of the CcmK/CsoS1 family that forms the major tiles of carboxysome shells in alpha-type bacterial microcompartments. In Halothiobacillus neapolitanus, an alpha-carboxysome houses RuBisCO, and a shell built from these hexamers establishes a semi-permeable barrier. The positive potential around the central pore, encoded by the conserved site, admits bicarbonate while impeding CO2 efflux and O2 influx. By elevating the internal CO2 concentration and suppressing O2 entry, the shell enhances the carboxylation efficiency of RuBisCO that drives the reductive pentose-phosphate (Calvin–Benson–Bassham) cycle. The extensive coverage of BMC shell domains and the small size of the subunit point to a high copy number; for a standard alpha-carboxysome geometry, this class of facet-forming hexamers typically contributes on the order of thousands of subunits, consistent with an estimated ~2970 copies per organelle distributed between nearly identical paralogs that differ by only a single residue (CsoS1A/CsoS1C).

Thus, the immediate molecular function is structural: a constituent of the carboxysome shell (GO:0043886). That scaffolding function causally supports two biological outputs. First, it enables carboxysome assembly and organization by forming the flat facets that enclose the lumen. Second, by enforcing an anion-selective permeability regime, it supports carbon fixation through the reductive pentose-phosphate cycle (GO:0019253) by concentrating CO2 around RuBisCO. The cellular location that follows from this BMC-H architecture is the carboxysome (GO:0031470), a protein-based prokaryotic microcompartment residing in the cytosol.

Mechanistically, the subunit hexamerizes to create sheets that laterally co-polymerize with close paralogs such as CsoS1B, CsoS1C, and CsoS1D to tune pore properties across the lattice. The sheets meet at fivefold vertices formed by small pentameric peptides (carboxysome peptide A and B), which cap the shell and close curvature. On the luminal face, repetitive, acidic segments of the scaffolding protein CsoS2 dock to the hexamer surfaces, tethering RuBisCO assemblies to the shell. Together, these interactions build the alpha-carboxysome enclosure that gates HCO3− influx, restricts CO2/O2 flux, and thereby boosts RuBisCO carboxylation in H. neapolitanus.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A major hexamer-forming facet protein of the alpha-carboxysome shell in Halothiobacillus neapolitanus. By assembling into tessellating sheets with a positively charged central pore, it creates an anion-selective barrier that favors bicarbonate passage while discouraging CO2 and O2 exchange. This selective permeability concentrates CO2 around encapsulated RuBisCO to enhance carboxylation during the Calvin–Benson–Bassham cycle. Thousands of copies co-polymerize with nearly identical paralogs to build the shell facets, interface with pentameric vertex proteins, and engage the internal scaffold to organize the lumenal enzyme cargo.

row.protein_function: PPIase that acts as a histone chaperone. Histone proline isomerase that increases the rate of cis-trans isomerization at 'Pro-17' (H3P16), 'Pro-31' (H3P30) and 'Pro-39 (H3P38) on the histone H3 N-terminal tail. H3P16 and H3P30 are the major proline targets with little activity shown against H3P38. H3P38 isomerization influences SET2-mediated H3K36 methylation thereby regulating gene expression.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is bipartite. The N-terminus (residues 10–164) harbors IPR041232 (Nucleoplasmin-like domain), an acidic histone-chaperone module that binds H3–H4 and facilitates nucleosome assembly and histone tail exposure. The entire protein falls within IPR023566 (Peptidyl-prolyl cis-trans isomerase Fpr3/Fpr4-like family, residues 2–392), specifying a nuclear FKBP clade that targets chromatin substrates. The C-terminal third contains IPR046357 (Peptidyl-prolyl cis-trans isomerase domain superfamily, residues 276–392) with a well-defined catalytic core refined as IPR001179 (FKBP-type peptidyl-prolyl cis-trans isomerase domain, residues 302–392). This ordering—an N-terminal nucleoplasmin-like histone-binding scaffold feeding into a C-terminal FKBP catalytic pocket—creates a mechanism in which histone capture and positioning by the chaperone domain delivers specific proline residues in histone tails to the FKBP active site for cis-trans isomerization (EC 5.2.1.8).

This FKBP pocket catalyzes peptidyl-prolyl bond rotation, establishing GO:0003755 peptidyl-prolyl cis-trans isomerase activity as the core molecular function. The hydrophobic cavity of FKBP domains also accommodates macrolide ligands (e.g., FK506/rapamycin-like scaffolds), so the architecture intrinsically supports GO:0005527 macrolide binding; macrolide occupancy would occlude the active site and modulate catalysis. The nucleoplasmin-like scaffold and FKBP surface grooves together provide multivalent contacts with histones and assembly factors, accounting for GO:0005515 protein binding.

Coupling a histone chaperone module to an FKBP catalytic site focuses the chemistry onto histone tails. The N-terminal H3 tail contains multiple prolines; positioning by the nucleoplasmin-like domain enables efficient isomerization at Pro-16 and Pro-30 as principal targets, with lower turnover at Pro-38 adjacent to Lys-36. This implements GO:0000412 histone peptidyl-prolyl isomerization. Because the isomeric state flanking Lys-36 dictates how the SET domain methyltransferase SET2 engages the tail, catalysis at H3 Pro-38 reduces productive SET2 access and thereby down-modulates H3K36 methylation, matching GO:0000415 negative regulation of histone H3-K36 methylation. Independently, the nucleoplasmin-like domain promotes deposition and exchange of H3–H4, supporting GO:0006334 nucleosome assembly; proline isomerization further accelerates assembly by relieving kinetic barriers in tail conformations.

The combination of histone-tail remodeling with a nucleoplasmin-like scaffold necessitates a chromatin-centric locale, fulfilling GO:0000785 chromatin. The interaction milieu refines this to the nucleolus (GO:0005730 nucleolus): associations with 25S rRNA and nucleolar ribosome-assembly factors such as Ribosome biogenesis protein RPF2, ERB1, Nucleolar complex protein 2, Periodic tryptophan protein 1, Nucleolar protein 58, LOC1, and H/ACA RNP subunit CBF5 place the protein on pre-60S and snoRNP maturation pathways. A plausible role is to chaperone H3 on rDNA chromatin and flip specific H3 prolines to tune chromatin states at ribosomal DNA, thereby coordinating nucleosome assembly with ribosome biogenesis. Direct engagement with Histone H3 is expected from the chaperone–isomerase architecture, and transient contacts with other peptidyl-prolyl cis-trans isomerases would fine-tune proline-remodeling flux across nucleolar substrates.

Mechanistically, the N-terminal nucleoplasmin-like domain concentrates the enzyme on nucleosomal substrates and pre-ribosomal chromatin. The FKBP catalytic site performs rapid cis-trans interconversion of H3 Pro-16 and Pro-30, and to a lesser degree Pro-38, with the latter altering SET2 access and attenuating H3K36 methylation. Macrolide binding would competitively inhibit this activity. Through interactions with RPF2, ERB1, NOC2, PWP1, NOP58, LOC1, CBF5, and 25S rRNA, the enzyme likely couples histone-tail isomerization to nucleolar chromatin organization during 60S subunit assembly, while its broad protein-binding surfaces maintain dynamic complexes on chromatin.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A nuclear histone-focused peptidyl-prolyl isomerase that uses an N-terminal nucleoplasmin-like chaperone module to capture H3–H4 and present the H3 tail to a C-terminal FKBP catalytic pocket. By flipping specific prolines on the H3 tail—primarily at positions near 16 and 30, and to a lesser extent near 38—it accelerates nucleosome assembly and modulates the local chromatin state. Isomerization near Lys36 limits access by the H3K36 methyltransferase, dampening this mark and thereby tuning gene expression, particularly on nucleolar chromatin linked to ribosome biogenesis. The FKBP pocket also binds macrolides, which would inhibit the catalytic cycle.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by IPR024854 (Kinectin family, residues 1–1356), a hallmark of long coiled-coil scaffolds that anchor in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) via a single C‑terminal transmembrane segment while projecting a large N‑terminal cytosolic tail. Embedded near the N‑terminus is IPR007794 (Ribosome receptor lysine/proline rich domain, residues 29–164). This basic, low-complexity, Lys/Pro-rich segment is characteristic of ribosome- and rRNA-engaging tethers on the cytosolic face of the ER. In a type II orientation, this domain is positioned in the cytosol where it can contact ribosomes, mRNA, translation factors, and cytoskeletal motors, while a short luminal tail accounts for ER lumen exposure.

This architecture causally encodes two capacities. First, the coiled-coil family core in IPR024854 forms an elongated, kinesin-binding platform. By presenting recognition surfaces to kinesin light and heavy chains, it acts as a receptor that recruits kinesin-1 to ER and vesicle membranes, thereby enabling microtubule-powered motility of cargo. Second, the Lys/Pro-rich IPR007794 domain generates avid electrostatic contacts with rRNA and ribosome-associated mRNPs, rationalizing GO:0003723 RNA binding. The same extended cytosolic scaffold can contact adhesion machinery; accumulation at integrin-based adhesion complexes positions it to engage cadherin cytoplasmic tails or cadherin-associated adaptors at the cortex, consistent with GO:0045296 cadherin binding.

From these molecular activities, the biological roles follow. By docking kinesin-1 onto ER-derived vesicles, the protein drives microtubule-based vesicle transport and supports directional trafficking from the ER toward downstream stations, aligning with processes such as endoplasmic reticulum to Golgi vesicle-mediated transport and the broader category of microtubule-based vesicle transport. Its presence at adhesion assemblies and ability to bind cadherin-related components place it in the regulation of cell adhesion, coordinating cargo delivery and membrane remodeling with adhesion dynamics. The Lys/Pro-rich ribosome-receptor property implies it can couple translating ribosomes and their mRNAs to motile ER domains, promoting mRNA localization and co-translational targeting to membranes.

The membrane topology constrains localization. A single-pass type II membrane configuration embeds the C-terminus in the ER membrane with a short luminal tail, specifying GO:0005789 endoplasmic reticulum membrane and GO:0005788 endoplasmic reticulum lumen. Because the motor-recruiting scaffold is on the cytosolic face, ER-derived vesicles carrying this receptor can be trafficked to the cell periphery and concentrate at adhesion sites near the plasma membrane, providing a route to the GO:0005886 plasma membrane context through vesicle docking and ER–plasma membrane contact regions.

A mechanistic model emerges: the ER-anchored scaffold captures kinesin-1 (Kinesin light chain 1 and Kinesin-1 heavy chain) via its cytosolic coiled-coils to propel ER-derived vesicles along microtubules. Through its Lys/Pro-rich domain and associations with Elongation factor 1-delta and Elongation factor 1-gamma, it binds translating ribosomes and their mRNAs, linking motility to localized protein synthesis on ER membranes. Interactions with small GTPases Transforming protein RhoA and Rho-related GTP-binding protein RhoG tune motor engagement and steer vesicles toward adhesion-rich cortical zones, where cadherin binding helps coordinate junctional turnover with delivery of membrane and adhesion components. Association with Cerebellar degeneration-related protein 2 may further modulate cargo specificity or neuronal trafficking contexts. Together, these interactions integrate translation, microtubule transport, and adhesion remodeling in an ER-anchored vesicle trafficking module.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: An ER-anchored, single-pass type II membrane adaptor that projects a long cytosolic coiled-coil to recruit kinesin-1 and drive microtubule-based movement of ER-derived vesicles. A lysine/proline-rich segment engages ribosomes and mRNA via translation factors, coupling co-translational cargo production to vesicle motility. At the cell periphery it concentrates near adhesion assemblies, where contacts with cadherin-associated components and Rho-family GTPases coordinate membrane delivery and adhesion remodeling at plasma membrane–proximal sites.
