row.protein_names: Protein ABHD1 (EC 3.1.1.-) (Alpha/beta hydrolase domain-containing protein 1) (Abhydrolase domain-containing protein 1) (Lung alpha/beta hydrolase 1)
row.protein_function: Not known
row.subcellular_location: Membrane {ECO:0000305}; Single-pass type II membrane protein {ECO:0000305}.

- IPR000073: Alpha/beta hydrolase fold-1 (domain) [123-364]
row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by alpha/beta hydrolase features that span nearly the entire polypeptide and coalesce into a canonical serine hydrolase core. The AB hydrolase 4 family signature (IPR012020, residues 2–396) envelopes the sequence, within which the AB hydrolase superfamily, subfamily 4 (IPR050960, residues 38–386) and the alpha/beta hydrolase fold superfamily (IPR029058, residues 82–365) converge on the central catalytic scaffold. The fold-1 core (IPR000073, residues 123–364) specifies the β-sheet/α-helix topology that positions the nucleophile at the “nucleophile elbow,” typically a GXSXG serine motif. The AB hydrolase 4 conserved site (IPR000952, residues 288–329) maps to the catalytic acid/histidine region, completing the Ser–His–Asp triad. This architecture enforces carboxylic-ester hydrolysis chemistry (EC 3.1.1.–), most plausibly on lipid substrates due to the extensive ABHD lineage associations with membrane lipases.

Topology dictates where catalysis occurs. The polypeptide is a single-pass type II membrane protein, which places a short N-terminal tail in the cytosol and the large C-terminal domain in a non-cytosolic compartment. The domain layout—starting only after ~residue 38—implies a transmembrane signal anchor near the N-terminus, positioning the catalytic alpha/beta hydrolase module (residues 123–364 with the conserved site at 288–329) on the luminal/extracellular side. Such a configuration targets substrates in the luminal leaflet of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) or along the secretory pathway. The presence of a cytosolic N-tail alongside a catalytically competent luminal domain also rationalizes general protein-binding capacity (GO:0005515), as the short cytosolic segment can engage cytosolic machinery while the C-terminal domain executes hydrolysis in the lumen.

Catalytically, a serine nucleophile activated by the histidine/aspartate dyad (encoded within IPR000073 and IPR000952) will cleave ester bonds in glycerophospholipids or lysophospholipids. In the ER, such reactions remodel the luminal leaflet, generating lysolipids or altering acyl composition. This modification increases membrane curvature stress, a physical cue that promotes vesicle budding. The observed association with the guanine nucleotide-exchange factor SEC12, a type II ER membrane protein at ER exit sites, anchors the enzyme to COPII initiation zones. The cytosolic N-tail can dock near SEC12’s cytosolic GEF domain, while the luminal catalytic head locally adjusts luminal leaflet composition. This coupling provides a direct path from molecular function (serine/carboxylic-ester hydrolase; lipase) to biological process: phospholipid and broader lipid metabolic remodeling, deployed spatiotemporally to support ER-to-Golgi vesicle formation.

Subcellularly, the single-pass type II architecture and the SEC12 linkage converge on the endoplasmic reticulum membrane as the primary residence. Functionally active residues lie in the ER lumen, consistent with luminal access to substrates. Taken together, the evidence supports integral membrane localization (integral component of membrane) at the ER membrane, with the catalytic domain projecting into the ER lumen.

Mechanistic hypothesis: At ER exit sites, the enzyme binds SEC12 through its cytosolic tail (protein binding), positioning its luminal alpha/beta hydrolase head to hydrolyze phospholipid esters in the luminal leaflet. By transiently generating lysophospholipids or adjusting acyl chains, it promotes positive curvature, thereby facilitating COPII budding initiated by SAR1 activation and SEC23/SEC24 coat assembly. Partners likely include SEC12 (observed), SAR1A/SAR1B (activated at the same sites), and inner coat components such as SEC23/SEC24 that assemble concomitantly.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A single-pass type II membrane serine hydrolase in human cells whose catalytic domain projects into the endoplasmic reticulum lumen, where it hydrolyzes ester bonds in luminal leaflet lipids to remodel phospholipid composition. By tuning local membrane curvature at ER exit sites, it likely supports COPII vesicle formation. A short cytosolic N-terminal tail mediates protein binding, positioning the enzyme via association with the SEC12 GEF, while the luminal alpha/beta hydrolase core executes lipid hydrolysis.

row.protein_function: Transcription factor. Together with FMA and SPCH, regulates the stomata formation. Required for the differentiation of stomatal guard cells, by promoting successive asymmetric cell divisions and the formation of guard mother cells. Promotes the conversion of the leaf epidermis into stomata.

row.reasoning:
The sequence begins with a compact DNA-binding module: IPR011598 (Myc-type, basic helix-loop-helix (bHLH) domain, residues 1–55) sits at the N-terminus and is encompassed by IPR036638 (Helix-loop-helix DNA-binding domain superfamily, residues 1–67). This architecture generates a basic region for contacting specific base patterns in cis-regulatory DNA and a helix-loop-helix scaffold for obligate dimerization. Such bHLH dimers recognize E-box-like motifs in regulatory regions and thereby impose transcriptional programs. Extending beyond the core, IPR044283 (Transcription factor FAMA/SPEECHLESS/MUTE-like family, residues 1–192) marks a plant-specific lineage of bHLH transcription factors that gates transitions within the stomatal lineage. The ordered layout—an N-terminal DNA-binding/dimerization engine embedded within a stomatal-lineage-specific regulatory family—causes sequence-specific occupancy of promoters/enhancers and recruitment of transcriptional machinery, which defines transcription cis-regulatory region binding (GO:0000976) as the molecular function.

A bHLH engine in this family is specialized for building the stomatal complex. The family signature (IPR044283) confines activity to epidermal lineage commitment and the successive asymmetric divisions that precede guard mother cell formation. By switching on genes that both allow one final round of cell cycle entry and suppress further asymmetric divisions, this factor compels the conversion of meristemoids into guard mother cells and then to guard cells. These causal roles map directly onto stomatal complex development (GO:0010374) and the broader epidermal cell differentiation (GO:0009913).

Transcriptional control over nuclear DNA requires nuclear residence. The basic region often doubles as, or is juxtaposed to, nuclear localization determinants, and bHLH dimers act on chromatin-bound cis-regulatory elements. Therefore, the protein’s operating site is the nucleus (GO:0005634).

Mechanistically, bHLH proteins act as dimers. ICE1 and SCREAM2 are compatible bHLH partners; heterodimerization with these factors would tune E-box recognition and transcriptional potency, enabling precise activation of guard mother cell genes and repression of continued asymmetric division programs. External positional cues are relayed by secreted peptides EPIDERMAL PATTERNING FACTOR 1 and 2 and EPF-like 9; these are matured by the subtilase SBT1.2 and perceived by LRR receptor kinases ERL1 and ERL2, triggering the MAPKKK YODA cascade. This signaling axis modulates transcriptional thresholds in the stomatal lineage and likely gates when the bHLH dimer can drive the cis-regulatory program. The polarity scaffold BREAKING OF ASYMMETRY IN THE STOMATAL LINEAGE coordinates asymmetric divisions that precede the bHLH-driven transition; once the transcription factor engages, it reprograms the cell to a guard mother cell fate, integrating receptor-mediated inhibition/permission cues to enforce proper spacing and restrict overproliferation. In sum, the domain architecture ensures DNA binding and dimerization, the stomatal-lineage family mark directs the process specificity, and the interaction network supplies positional information that the transcription factor converts into a decisive nuclear program for stomatal differentiation.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A nuclear basic helix–loop–helix transcription factor in Arabidopsis that dimerizes to bind cis-regulatory DNA and execute a stomatal-lineage program. It integrates positional peptide–receptor and MAP kinase signals to terminate asymmetric divisions, promote guard mother cell formation, and drive guard cell differentiation, thereby converting regions of the leaf epidermis into functional stomata with correct spacing and number.

row.protein_function: Core component of nucleosome which plays a central role in DNA double strand break (DSB) repair. Nucleosomes wrap and compact DNA into chromatin, limiting DNA accessibility to the cellular machineries which require DNA as a template. Histones thereby play a central role in transcription regulation, DNA repair, DNA replication and chromosomal stability. DNA accessibility is regulated via a complex set of post-translational modifications of histones, also called histone code, and nucleosome remodeling.

row.reasoning:
The sequence is dominated by a canonical histone architecture. IPR009072 (Histone-fold, homologous_superfamily, residues 2–131) spans essentially the full core, providing the helix–strand–helix “handshake” scaffold that drives obligate heterodimerization with an H2B partner and DNA binding along the nucleosomal superhelical path. Nested within it, IPR002119 (Histone H2A family, residues 4–124) specifies the H2A lineage, refining interaction geometry for the H2A–H2B dimer and defining the characteristic acidic surface used by many chromatin factors. The embedded IPR007125 (Histone H2A/H2B/H3 domain, residues 14–90) pinpoints the conserved histone-fold subdomain that contacts both DNA and the H2B handshake partner. Downstream, IPR032454 (Histone H2A, C-terminal domain, residues 93–126) constitutes the docking segment that interlocks with the H3–H4 tetramer at the nucleosome dyad and carries the damage-responsive tail that can be phosphorylated. IPR032458 (Histone H2A conserved site, residues 23–29) highlights an N-terminal motif in the tail where lysines are positioned for regulatory acetylation and other modifications. This ordered layout—histone fold for dimerization and DNA wrapping, a lineage-specific C-terminus for docking and signaling, and tail motifs for post-translational modification—creates a modular platform whose surfaces are engineered for selective protein recognition.

These structural modules cause two primary molecular functions. First, the extensive solvent-exposed surfaces of the histone fold and adjacent tails enable multivalent protein binding (GO:0005515), including to chaperones, remodelers, and effector domains. Second, by coupling modification-sensitive patches (e.g., phosphorylation at the H2A C-terminal tail and acetylation at the N-terminus) to recruitment of repair and remodeling factors, the protein acts as a chromatin-protein adaptor (GO:0140463), physically bridging nucleosomal DNA to checkpoint mediators and repair scaffolds.

From these molecular activities, several biological processes follow. The nucleosome formed by H2A–H2B dimers docked onto an H3–H4 tetramer compacts DNA and provides the substrate that condensin and related factors act upon, driving mitotic chromosome condensation (GO:0007076). Proper nucleosome architecture in pericentric domains supports cohesion and kinetochore function, promoting faithful homologous chromosome segregation (GO:0045143). The H2A C-terminal domain is primed for DNA-damage-induced phosphorylation, which creates a docking site for BRCT-containing checkpoint mediators; this initiates and amplifies mitotic G2 DNA damage checkpoint signaling (GO:0007095). The same phospho-mark and associated adaptor function concentrate repair assemblies at DNA lesions, enabling double-strand break repair (GO:0006302), especially by recruiting recognition and scaffolding factors to damaged chromatin.

The histone-fold architecture inherently targets the nucleus (GO:0005634) and deposits into nucleosomal arrays along chromosomes. In fission yeast, strong demands on chromatin integrity localize this H2A to heterochromatin-rich territories, including pericentric heterochromatin (GO:0005721), rDNA heterochromatin (GO:0033553), the mating-type region heterochromatin (GO:0031934), and the chromosome subtelomeric region (GO:0099115). In these compartments, the nucleosome provided by H2A supports transcriptional silencing, mechanical rigidity for segregation, and a protected substrate for repair-factor docking.

A mechanistic model emerges: the histone fold (IPR009072/IPR007125/IPR002119) forms a stable H2A–H2B dimer that binds DNA and docks via the H2A C-terminal domain (IPR032454) onto H3–H4 to complete the nucleosome. The N-terminal conserved site (IPR032458) undergoes acetylation to tune chromatin accessibility, while DNA damage induces phosphorylation at the H2A C-terminus to generate an affinity tag for BRCT modules. This tag recruits checkpoint mediators and scaffolds, converting local DNA breaks into signaling foci and repair hubs. Chaperones and remodelers then cycle the H2A–H2B dimer to remodel or reassemble nucleosomes as repair proceeds.

The interaction landscape follows directly. The H2A–H2B handshake dictates a constitutive partnership with Histone H2B-alpha. The H2A C-terminal docking with the H3–H4 tetramer explains association with Histone H4 and both canonical Histone H3.1/H3.2 and variant Histone H3.3. Exchange with Histone H2A.Z occurs at boundaries and promoters where variant replacement regulates chromatin dynamics. DNA repair protein crb2 and BRCT-containing protein 1 are recruited by the phosphorylated H2A tail during damage signaling, embodying the adaptor role. Histone acetyltransferase mst1 targets the H2A/H2B tails to loosen chromatin and facilitate repair-associated remodeling. FACT complex subunit spt16 binds the H2A–H2B dimer to disassemble and re-deposit nucleosomes during transcription and repair. Co-residence and potential isoform mixing with Histone H2A-alpha reflect hemicomplex interchange within nucleosomes across the genome, including heterochromatin domains.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A canonical nucleosomal core subunit in fission yeast that dimerizes with H2B and docks onto the H3–H4 tetramer to wrap DNA, creating regulatory surfaces that recruit chromatin enzymes, remodelers, and DNA damage effectors. Modification of its N- and C-terminal tails modulates chromatin accessibility and converts DNA lesions into signaling platforms that activate the G2 checkpoint and assemble repair machinery. It is integral to compacting chromosomes for mitosis and sustaining heterochromatin at pericentric, subtelomeric, rDNA, and mating-type regions within the nucleus.

row.reasoning:
The sequence is dominated by two overlapping InterPro family signatures characteristic of MATE transporters. IPR045069 (Multidrug and toxic compound extrusion family, eukaryotic, residues 36–470) spans nearly the full polypeptide, and within it lies IPR002528 (Multi antimicrobial extrusion protein family, residues 46–442). This nested arrangement defines the canonical MATE architecture: a compact, multi-pass membrane transporter formed by two pseudosymmetric six-helix bundles that create a central, ion-coupled substrate translocation pathway. The multi-pass membrane annotation reinforces that the polypeptide folds into a 12-helix transporter core. In MATE proteins, conserved acidic residues in the transmembrane core coordinate a coupling ion (typically H+ in plants), and alternating-access conformational changes exchange protons for cationic organic substrates or specialized anions (e.g., citrate), producing net extrusion.

This domain architecture causally encodes secondary active transport, specifically drug and xenobiotic efflux through antiport. The ion-binding cavity created by the MATE fold supports antiporter activity (GO:0015297), and the reliance on an electrochemical gradient establishes secondary active transmembrane transporter activity (GO:0015291). The substrate spectrum of eukaryotic MATEs centers on drug-like organic cations and specialized metabolites; therefore, the direct molecular activity resolves to drug transmembrane transporter activity (GO:0015238).

The transport function drives higher-order physiology by clearing cytotoxic compounds and balancing metabolite pools. By extruding xenobiotics across a membrane, the protein mediates xenobiotic transport (GO:0042908) and drug transport (GO:0015893). Because the movement occurs across a lipid bilayer, it participates in transmembrane transport (GO:0055085). The ultimate biological role is protection from harmful chemicals, aligning with response to toxic substance (GO:0009636) as a stress-adaptation process.

Localization follows from both topology and function. A MATE fold necessitates embedding in a membrane, consistent with an integral component of membrane (GO:0016021). The specific cellular site can be deduced by reconciling detoxification logic with nuclear-associated annotations. Efflux at the nuclear envelope would protect the nucleoplasm from accumulation of toxic cations and regulate nuclear metabolite composition. The nuclear envelope is a specialized membrane that is ontologically part of the nucleus; thus, a nuclear-associated localization (GO:0005634) coheres mechanistically. Positioning in the nuclear envelope (GO:0005635) allows antiport between the nucleoplasm and the perinuclear/ER lumen using the proton gradient across the inner nuclear membrane.

This mechanistic setting suggests distinct interaction partners. A second detoxification transporter, Protein DETOXIFICATION 42, could form a functional array with complementary substrate specificity or provide redundancy in the same membrane. Patatin-like protein 1, a phospholipase, can remodel local phospholipids and release signaling lysophospholipids or fatty acids, tuning the bilayer properties and proton coupling efficiency of the transporter; lipid modulation often gates the alternating-access transitions of multi-pass transporters. The uncharacterized F18B13.24 protein may serve as an accessory factor that stabilizes the transporter in the nuclear envelope or senses substrates to recruit the efflux module.

Putting these pieces together, the eukaryotic MATE fold spanning residues 36–470 establishes a proton-coupled antiporter that exports cationic xenobiotics and specialized metabolites from the nucleoplasm across the nuclear envelope. This activity effectuates detoxification and preserves nuclear homeostasis by limiting the exposure of chromatin and regulatory enzymes to harmful compounds.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A multi-pass membrane transporter in Arabidopsis that uses a proton-driven antiport mechanism to extrude drug-like xenobiotics and specialized metabolites. By situating at the nuclear envelope, it likely limits accumulation of toxic cations in the nucleoplasm and shapes nuclear metabolite pools, thereby supporting cellular detoxification and stress tolerance. Lipid remodeling by a patatin-like phospholipase may tune its activity, and partnering transporters may broaden substrate coverage at the same membrane.

- IPR052647: Zinc finger CCCH-type domain-containing protein (family) [1-974]
row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is defined by two InterPro signatures. IPR052647 (Zinc finger CCCH-type domain-containing protein, family) spans essentially the entire polypeptide (residues 1–974), indicating a large member of the CCCH-type zinc finger protein family that typically combines a small RNA-contacting module with extensive low-complexity regions that scaffold ribonucleoprotein (RNP) assemblies. Nested within this, IPR041367 (E3 ligase, CCCH-type zinc finger, domain) occupies residues 252–267. This compact C3H1 zinc-chelating fold is classically used to grip single-stranded, U-rich RNA tracts. In several E3-ligase-associated proteins the same CCCH fold functions as the RNA-recognition element, whereas the ubiquitin-transfer chemistry resides in a separate catalytic domain that is not evident here. The architecture therefore points to an RNA-selector module embedded in a predominantly non-catalytic scaffold that binds additional proteins.

A CCCH zinc finger directly confers RNA binding. Because this protein is large and lacks signatures of enzymatic cores or transmembrane segments, the most direct molecular functions are RNA binding (with a preference for single-stranded and mRNA contexts) and broad protein binding mediated by flanking disordered regions that assemble RNP effectors. In human cells, such CCCH-bearing scaffolds typically act on nascent and nuclear-transiting transcripts.

Nuclear speckles are organizing centers for pre-mRNA processing, quality control, and RNP remodeling. A CCCH-driven RNA binding activity that is embedded in a non-enzymatic scaffold fits a role in selecting transcripts and recruiting processing or surveillance complexes in this compartment. This places the protein in mRNA processing and nuclear RNA surveillance, with consequences for RNA catabolic pathways and the regulation of mRNA stability when aberrant or unstable transcripts are encountered.

The same reasoning that links CCCH-mediated RNA recognition to speck-associated RNP assembly also supports localization to nuclear speckles (GO:0016607) and, more broadly, the nucleoplasm. Mechanistically, the CCCH zinc finger can read U-rich or otherwise exposed single-stranded regions on nascent or incompletely processed RNAs; the large, low-complexity body of the protein then nucleates interactions with cap-binding and surveillance factors to hand off faulty RNAs to 3′–5′ decay machines. Because some CCCH proteins interface with ubiquitin ligases, this scaffold may additionally recruit E3 enzymes to modify RNP components without being an E3 catalyst itself.

From this model, likely interaction partners include nuclear RNA-surveillance and processing factors concentrated in speckles: the cap-binding and adaptor proteins (NCBP1/CBP80, NCBP2/CBP20, ARS2/SRRT), the NEXT co-adaptors (RBM7, ZCCHC8) and the MTR4/SKIV2L2 helicase, as well as core nuclear exosome subunits (e.g., EXOSC3, EXOSC10) and abundant speck-resident splicing regulators (SRSF1, SRSF2, U2AF2). Together these partners would enable selective capture and routing of problematic RNAs to nuclear decay.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A large human nuclear RNA-binding scaffold that concentrates in nuclear speckles, where a CCCH zinc finger selects exposed, U-rich single-stranded segments on nascent or unprocessed transcripts. The remainder of the protein likely recruits RNA-processing and surveillance factors to remodel ribonucleoprotein particles and direct aberrant RNAs toward nuclear decay, thereby shaping mRNA processing and stability. It may also interface with ubiquitination machinery through binding rather than catalysis to license turnover of specific RNPs.

row.protein_names: RNase E specificity factor CsrD (Regulator of CsrB and CsrC decay CsrD)
row.protein_function: Serves as a specificity factor required for RNase E-mediated decay of the small global regulatory RNAs CsrB and CsrC, it is probably not a nuclease. Nor does its activity involve c-di-GMP, despite its domain composition. Positively modulates motility gene expression, is also required for curli expression.

row.reasoning:
An N-to-C architectural readout shows a membrane-anchored sensory/signaling protein with a periplasmic detector and a cytosolic GGDEF–EAL tandem. The N-terminal region includes IPR033423 (Gammaproteobacterial periplasmic sensor domain GAPES4, residues 34–130), a periplasm-facing module that typically captures envelope cues and drives conformational switching across the bilayer. The polypeptide is annotated across residues 2–634 by IPR050706 (Cyclic di-GMP phosphodiesterase-like family), which frames the entire protein as a PDE-like signaling scaffold. Immediately downstream on the cytosolic side lies IPR043128 (Reverse transcriptase/Diguanylate cyclase domain superfamily, residues 207–386), containing IPR000160 (GGDEF domain, residues 213–387) embedded within IPR029787 (Nucleotide cyclase superfamily, residues 230–387). This GGDEF module is followed contiguously by IPR035919 (EAL domain superfamily, residues 396–644) and the catalytic core IPR001633 (EAL domain, residues 395–644). The domain order—periplasmic sensor → membrane span(s) → cytosolic GGDEF → cytosolic EAL—is the hallmark of bacterial transmembrane signaling enzymes that regulate c-di-GMP; however, the joint presence of both GGDEF and EAL domains in one chain frequently corresponds to catalytic attenuation and repurposing for assembly and substrate selection.

The GGDEF and EAL folds impose obligate dimerization to align their active-site-like surfaces, even when catalysis is disabled. This architecture therefore natively drives homomer formation, supporting GO:0042802 identical protein binding as the operative molecular function. In such tandems, the EAL scaffold supplies broad nucleic-acid/protein docking grooves and divalent-ion coordination sites, while the GGDEF shoulder provides additional interaction surfaces and allosteric cavities. These features, deployed next to the membrane, create a platform to capture specific RNA substrates and recruit endoribonucleases. Consequently, the protein acts upstream of RNA decay without cutting RNA itself, fulfilling the role formalized as GO:0051252 regulation of RNA metabolic process. By steering particular small noncoding RNAs into an RNase E-centered degradosome, it directly contributes to their turnover, which is the essence of GO:0034661 ncRNA catabolic process.

The periplasmic GAPES4 detector suggests that envelope-derived signals toggle the cytosolic scaffold between permissive and non-permissive states for RNA handoff to RNase E. Multi-pass membrane topology places the complex at the cell boundary, in line with the annotated localization to GO:0005886 plasma membrane. Because the GGDEF–EAL tandem organizes multivalent interfaces for both homomerization and partner recruitment, the protein functions as a node within a larger assembly, consistent with residence in a GO:0032991 protein-containing complex.

A mechanistic hypothesis follows from this chain of inference. The periplasmic GAPES4 domain senses changes in the envelope that propagate through the transmembrane core and reconfigure the cytosolic GGDEF–EAL tandem into a dimeric capture device (GO:0042802). The EAL surface binds the sRNAs that titrate the carbon storage regulator (CsrB/C) and simultaneously docks RNase E, promoting selective endonucleolytic cleavage of these sRNAs (GO:0034661), thereby rewiring the availability of the Csr regulator and broadly tuning RNA fate (GO:0051252). This membrane-tethered specificity factor likely assembles with RNase E at the inner surface of the plasma membrane (GO:0005886; GO:0032991).

From this vantage point, partner logic becomes clear. Direct association with Ribonuclease E is expected to execute the RNA decay step, while contact with the Carbon storage regulator integrates substrate recognition and timing. Coupling to the two-component system components BarA (histidine kinase) and UvrY (response regulator) would synchronize sRNA production (transcriptional control) with sRNA removal (decay), ensuring robust homeostasis of the Csr circuit. Finally, the presence of GGDEF/EAL-like surfaces rationalizes assembly with diguanylate cyclases (DgcT, CdgI, DgcI, DgcJ) and phosphodiesterases (PdeN, PdeI): not to catalyze or sense c-di-GMP directly, but to scaffold against these enzymes so that motility and curli programs—processes governed by the Csr and c-di-GMP networks—are co-regulated via shared proximity at the membrane hub.

This integrated model explains how a membrane, multi-pass GGDEF–EAL scaffold with a periplasmic sensor (IPR033423; IPR000160 within IPR043128 and IPR029787; IPR001633 within IPR035919; IPR050706 across the length) achieves GO:0042802 identical protein binding, drives GO:0051252 regulation of RNA metabolic process and GO:0034661 ncRNA catabolic process, and operates within GO:0005886 plasma membrane as part of a GO:0032991 protein-containing complex. It thereby promotes the selective decay of specific small RNAs that, through the Csr regulon, modulate motility and curli expression.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A multi-pass inner-membrane signaling scaffold that uses a periplasmic sensor and a cytosolic GGDEF–EAL tandem to dimerize and assemble a membrane-associated RNA decay module. By docking specific small regulatory RNAs and recruiting RNase E, it promotes selective turnover of these transcripts, thereby tuning the activity of the carbon storage regulator and reshaping global programs such as motility and curli production. Its GGDEF/EAL architecture functions primarily as a protein–protein and RNA–protein assembly surface rather than a c-di-GMP catalyst.

row.protein_names: Bifunctional diterpene synthase, chloroplastic (Copalyl diphosphate synthase) (EC 5.5.1.12) (Miltiradiene synthase) (SmMDS) (EC 4.2.3.131) (Terpene synthase 4) (SmTPS4)
row.protein_function: Bifunctional diterpene cyclase that catalyzes the successive two-step type-B (protonation-initiated cyclization) and type-A (ionization-initiated cyclization) reactions of geranylgeranyl diphosphate (GGDP) producing successively (+)-copalyl diphosphate and miltiradiene.

row.reasoning:
A two-domain terpene cyclase architecture emerges from the InterPro map. IPR050148 (Terpene synthase-like family, residues 104–858) spans the catalytic core, with an N-terminal α-helical module and a C-terminal metal-binding module. The N-terminal half is captured by IPR008930 (Terpenoid cyclases/protein prenyltransferase alpha-alpha toroid homologous superfamily, 106–512) and further specified by IPR001906 (Terpene synthase, N-terminal domain, 284–489) and its broader scaffold IPR036965 (Terpene synthase, N-terminal domain superfamily, 107–536). This ensemble defines the class II terpene cyclase fold that stabilizes cationic intermediates in a protonation-initiated cyclization. In diterpene systems, this fold typically carries a catalytic acidic dyad (for example, a DxH motif) that donates a proton to the C=C bond of geranylgeranyl diphosphate (GGPP), bending the chain into a labdane framework and yielding the bicyclic diphosphate intermediate (+)-copalyl diphosphate. Such chemistry corresponds to copalyl diphosphate synthase activity, aligning with GO:0050559.

The C-terminal half is defined by IPR005630 (Terpene synthase, metal-binding domain, 566–804) and encompassed by IPR008949 (Isoprenoid synthase domain superfamily, 528–867). This module carries the canonical class I motifs—an aspartate-rich DDxxD and the NSE/DTE triad—that chelate Mg2+ and enforce ionization of the diphosphate group on the previously formed labdane intermediate. Ionization triggers a cascade of carbocation rearrangements and deprotonation to form the tricyclic hydrocarbon miltiradiene. Thus the domain order encodes a sequential type-B (protonation-initiated) reaction in the N-terminal domain followed by a type-A (ionization-initiated, Mg2+-dependent) reaction in the C-terminal domain, producing first (+)-copalyl diphosphate and then miltiradiene.

The two-step logic of this architecture maps directly to biological processes. The first, N-terminal step constructs 5alpha,9alpha,10beta-labda-8(20),13-dien-15-yl diphosphate—formalizing GO:1901949 5alpha,9alpha,10beta-labda-8(20),13-dien-15-yl diphosphate biosynthetic process. The second, C-terminal step consumes that same diphosphate intermediate by ionization and rearrangement, corresponding to GO:1901948 5alpha,9alpha,10beta-labda-8(20),13-dien-15-yl diphosphate catabolic process. Together they accomplish GO:1901946 miltiradiene biosynthetic process, the net outcome of the bifunctional cyclase.

Residue coverage implies an additional targeting segment preceding residue ~104. Plant terpene synthases with such N-terminal extensions commonly carry plastid transit peptides, which, once cleaved, leave the conserved core beginning near residue 100. Given that GGPP for diterpene formation is generated by the plastidial MEP pathway and that soluble class II/ I diterpene cyclases function in the chloroplast stroma, the protein is most parsimoniously placed in the chloroplast, i.e., GO:0009507 chloroplast.

Mechanistically, the enzyme likely forms a local metabolon with a plastidial geranylgeranyl diphosphate synthase to channel GGPP directly into the class II active site, minimizing diffusion and preserving carbocationic fidelity. The C-terminal class I site then binds the intermediate via a shared tunnel or product-release/product-capture interface, where Mg2+ coordinated by DDxxD and NSE motifs catalyzes diphosphate ionization and ring closure to miltiradiene. Downstream, the hydrophobic product probably partitions to plastid–ER contact sites for oxidation by ER-anchored cytochrome P450s. Thus plausible interaction partners include a plastidial GGPPS (and its small subunit), MEP-pathway suppliers (DXS/DXR) that raise local GGPP flux, stromal chaperonins (CPN60/CPN20) that fold the large alpha-helical enzyme, and ER-resident CYP76-type monooxygenases with their NADPH–cytochrome P450 reductase that process miltiradiene into downstream specialized metabolites. Transient engagement with the TOC/TIC import apparatus would occur during chloroplast targeting of the precursor polypeptide.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A chloroplast-localized, two-domain diterpene cyclase that channels geranylgeranyl diphosphate through a sequential mechanism: its N-terminal module protonates the substrate to form the bicyclic copalyl diphosphate intermediate, and its C-terminal, metal-dependent module ionizes and cyclizes that intermediate to produce miltiradiene. Operating in the plastid stroma where GGPP is supplied by the MEP pathway, it likely forms a substrate-channeling complex with upstream prenyltransferases and hands its hydrophobic product off toward ER-localized oxidases at plastid–ER contact sites.

row.protein_function: Histones H1 are necessary for the condensation of nucleosome chains into higher-order structures (Probable). Probably does not act as global transcriptional repressor. Acting in concert with chromobox protein homologs hpl-1 and hpl-2, involved in reproduction, somatic gonad development, male tail development, and vulval cell fate decisions; perhaps as a result of modulating expression of Hox genes mab-5 and egl-5. Plays a role in linking epigenetic regulation with the innate immune response.
row.subcellular_location: Nucleus {ECO:0000269|PubMed:22083954}. Chromosome {ECO:0000269|PubMed:22083954, ECO:0000305|PubMed:3202838}. Cytoplasm {ECO:0000269|PubMed:22083954}. Note=Cytoplasmic localization is observed after infection with Gram-positive bacterium B.thuringiensis. {ECO:0000269|PubMed:22083954}.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is diagnostic of a canonical linker histone. A broad family signature, IPR005819 (Linker histone H1/H5, family) spans residues 24–205, indicating a full-length H1-like protein with the expected tripartite design: short N-terminal segment, central globular domain, and an extended, Lys-rich C-terminal tail. Embedded within this, residues 35–113 comprise IPR005818 (Linker histone H1/H5, domain H15), the hallmark globular “H15” domain. Two overlapping superfamily signatures, IPR036388 (Winged helix-like DNA-binding domain superfamily, residues 32–126) and IPR036390 (Winged helix DNA-binding domain superfamily, residues 35–126), further define the H15 core as a winged-helix fold specialized to clamp the nucleosomal dyad and contact both strands of linker DNA.

This architecture causes tight nucleosome bridging: the H15 globular fold positions on the dyad while the basic C-terminal tail neutralizes linker DNA and promotes internucleosomal contacts, condensing arrays into higher-order fibers. Such compaction limits access of transcriptional machinery, establishing the molecular basis for GO:0010629 negative regulation of gene expression. The winged-helix core also creates a nucleosome-contextual binding surface that senses histone tail states. Although it lacks a canonical aromatic cage of a standalone reader, H1’s dyad engagement allows cooperative recognition of modified nucleosomes through adaptor chromobox proteins. In Caenorhabditis elegans, association with chromobox proteins (e.g., hpl-1) can tether the H1 globular domain to nucleosomes bearing H3K27me3, yielding effective GO:0061628 H3K27me3 modified histone binding at the chromatin fiber.

The consequence of this reader-assisted anchoring is region-selective compaction rather than indiscriminate repression, aligning with a role in shaping gene expression programs rather than acting as a universal silencer. In developmental contexts, compaction at Hox loci (e.g., mab-5 and egl-5) would enforce correct patterning of the somatic gonad, male tail, and vulval lineages by delimiting accessible chromatin neighborhoods; this provides a mechanistic bridge from molecular function to organismal development.

The same chromatin physics explains cellular localization. The winged-helix H15 and Lys-rich tail necessarily bind DNA in the nucleus, placing the protein in GO:0005634 nucleus. Enrichment at regions requiring architectural rigidity, such as repetitive chromatin, supports GO:0000781 chromosome, telomeric region involvement as H1 stabilizes long linker segments at telomeres and buffers end-associated chromatin. Dynamic regulation by phosphorylation weakens tail–DNA affinity, enabling rapid eviction. During infection by Gram-positive Bacillus thuringiensis, such eviction and nuclear export would account for a stress-induced presence in GO:0005737 cytoplasm. This redistribution plausibly links epigenetic state to innate immune activation: release from chromatin de-represses immune genes while cytoplasmic H1 can engage signaling adaptors.

A coherent mechanistic model emerges. In the resting state, the H15 domain (IPR005818 within IPR005819; reinforced by IPR036388/IPR036390) binds the nucleosomal dyad and compacts linker DNA, with cooperative tethering to H3K27me3-marked regions (GO:0061628) via chromobox proteins to impose local repression (GO:0010629). At telomeres, the same mechanism organizes terminal chromatin (GO:0000781). Upon bacterial challenge, post-translational modification reduces chromatin affinity, mobilizing the protein into the cytoplasm (GO:0005737), which both frees promoters of immune genes and allows cytoplasmic interactions that propagate defense signaling.

The hypothesized interaction network follows from this model. Direct engagement with nucleosomes predicts contacts with Histone H3.3 type 2 and Histone H2A.V, variants that mark dynamic and stress-responsive chromatin, where H1 turnover is high. Chromobox protein homolog hpl-1 can bridge H1 to methyl-marked nucleosomes, enabling modified histone binding. Exchange among H1 variants suggests heterotypic or competitive interactions with Histone H1.3 during variant swapping on linker DNA. Nuclear coupling to transcription-splicing interfaces can bring the protein into proximity with the Pre-mRNA-splicing factor CWC22 homolog when compaction gates co-transcriptional spliceosome assembly. After infection-driven export, association with Protein unc-119, a cytosolic trafficking adaptor, could scaffold H1 in immune-related signaling complexes, completing the link between epigenetic remodeling and innate immune responses.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A linker histone in C. elegans that uses a winged-helix globular core to bind the nucleosomal dyad and its Lys-rich tail to bridge linker DNA, compacting chromatin into higher-order structures. Through cooperation with chromobox readers, it preferentially anchors on trimethylated H3K27 nucleosomes to enforce local repression of developmentally regulated loci, including Hox genes, rather than acting as a universal silencer. It resides predominantly on nuclear chromosomes, including telomeric regions, but mobilizes to the cytoplasm during bacterial infection, thereby coupling chromatin decompaction with innate immune signaling.

row.protein_names: Probable UDP-sugar transporter protein SLC35A4 (Solute carrier family 35 member A4)
row.protein_function: Mediates the transport of CDP-ribitol. Does not exhibit CMP-sialic acid, UDP-galactose and UDP-N-acetylglucosamine transport activity.
row.subcellular_location: Golgi apparatus membrane {ECO:0000269|PubMed:28167211, ECO:0000269|PubMed:38580077}; Multi-pass membrane protein {ECO:0000255}.
row.interpro_formatted:
- IPR037185: Multidrug transporter EmrE superfamily (homologous_superfamily) [63-159]
- IPR007271: Nucleotide-sugar transporter (family) [3-324]
row.reasoning:
The sequence begins with IPR007271 (Nucleotide-sugar transporter family, residues 3–324), which spans essentially the full polypeptide, marking it as a canonical SLC35-type nucleotide-sugar transporter. Embedded within this, the central region aligns to IPR037185 (Multidrug transporter EmrE superfamily, residues 63–159). This nested architecture places an EmrE-like core within the broader nucleotide-sugar transporter fold. In transporters of this class, multiple transmembrane helices pack to create a deeply buried, electrostatically positive cavity that recognizes the pyrophosphate group of nucleotide sugars and coordinates base-specific contacts; alternating access is driven by conformational exchange that couples import of a nucleotide sugar to export of a corresponding nucleotide monophosphate. The EmrE-like signature suggests a compact antiporter engine that gates access from cytosolic to luminal sides, while the encompassing SLC35/NST family signature dictates substrate class and Golgi residence.

A Golgi-resident, multi-pass topology follows from this architecture. Nucleotide-sugar transporters form integral, multi-pass membrane proteins that concentrate in the Golgi apparatus membrane to supply the lumen with activated sugars required for glycan assembly. The presence of the IPR007271 family anchor and the EmrE-like core therefore causes Golgi localization and positions the transporter within the glycosylation machinery. This supports the cellular component formalized as GO:0000139 Golgi membrane, and more generally an integral component of membrane identity.

Functionally, an NST/EmrE-like antiporter catalyzes nucleotide-sugar transmembrane transporter activity. The cavity’s cationic residues and shape complementarity enable high-affinity recognition of CDP-linked donors and pairing with an export pathway for CMP after glycosyltransferase reactions in the lumen. The transporter’s selectivity profile—mediating CDP-ribitol transport while lacking activity toward CMP-sialic acid, UDP-galactose, and UDP-N-acetylglucosamine—implies that the pocket distinguishes the cytidine base and the flexible polyol backbone of ribitol, likely rejecting bulky, ringed hexoses and sialylated donors whose steric and charge distribution do not match the recognition geometry. This architecture therefore specifies nucleotide-sugar transmembrane transporter activity (e.g., GO:0015780), and the polypeptide’s participation in stable assemblies at the Golgi rim rationalizes GO:0005515 protein binding through formation of transport–enzyme metabolons and retention complexes.

Supplying CDP-ribitol into the Golgi directly fuels protein glycosylation pathways that require ribitol-phosphate installation during dystroglycan matriglycan maturation. In biological process terms, the transporter drives transmembrane transport that is functionally upstream of protein glycosylation, enabling correct formation of glycan structures essential for cell–matrix interactions. Thus, the causal chain runs from an EmrE-like antiport engine embedded in an SLC35 scaffold (sequence-level) to nucleotide-sugar transmembrane transport (molecular function), which in turn sustains protein glycosylation in the Golgi (biological process), at the Golgi membrane (cellular component).

Mechanistically, the transporter likely operates as a dimeric or higher-order antiporter, exchanging cytosolic CDP-ribitol for luminal CMP produced by ribitol-phosphate transferases. On the cytosolic face, it can transiently associate with the CDP-ribitol–synthesizing enzyme to channel substrate. On the luminal side, it is poised next to glycosyltransferases that install ribitol phosphate and extend matriglycan, creating a vectorial substrate pipeline. These protein–protein contacts explain the protein binding capability and provide a route to specificity through substrate channeling and local enrichment.

From this logic, plausible interaction partners include: the cytosolic CDP-ribitol synthase (CRPPA/ISPD) delivering substrate to the transporter; luminal ribitol-phosphate transferases (FKTN and FKRP) that immediately consume CDP-ribitol; upstream and downstream O-mannose pathway enzymes (B4GAT1, RXYLT1/TMEM5, and LARGE1) forming a glycosylation metabolon; SLC35-family transporters (e.g., SLC35A1 or SLC35A2) that co-assemble for Golgi retention and coordinated donor balance; and tethering/trafficking modules such as COG complex subunits that help maintain the transporter within the Golgi ribbon.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A multi-pass Golgi membrane antiporter that imports CDP‑ribitol into the lumen in exchange for nucleotide monophosphate, thereby supplying the donor required for ribitol‑phosphate addition during glycan assembly. Its EmrE-like core embedded in a nucleotide‑sugar transporter scaffold creates a selective, electrostatically tuned cavity that favors cytidine‑linked ribitol over other nucleotide sugars. By operating adjacent to glycosyltransferases, it likely forms a substrate‑channeling hub that couples import to immediate consumption during protein glycosylation.

row.protein_names: Glutathione hydrolase 5 proenzyme (EC 3.4.19.13) (Gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase-related enzyme) (GGT-rel) (Gamma-glutamyltransferase 5) (GGT 5) (EC 2.3.2.2) (Gamma-glutamyltransferase-like activity 1) (Gamma-glutamyltranspeptidase 5) (Leukotriene-C4 hydrolase) (EC 3.4.19.14) [Cleaved into: Glutathione hydrolase 5 heavy chain; Glutathione hydrolase 5 light chain]
row.protein_function: Cleaves the gamma-glutamyl peptide bond of glutathione and glutathione-S-conjugate such as leukotriene C4. Does not cleaves gamma-glutamyl compounds such as gamma-glutamyl leucine. May also catalyze a transpeptidation reaction in addition to the hydrolysis reaction, transferring the gamma-glutamyl moiety to an acceptor amino acid to form a new gamma-glutamyl compound. Acts as a negative regulator of geranylgeranyl glutathione bioactivity by cleaving off its gamma-glutamyl group, playing a role in adaptive immune responses.
row.subcellular_location: Membrane {ECO:0000250|UniProtKB:Q9Z2A9}; Single-pass type II membrane protein {ECO:0000250|UniProtKB:Q9Z2A9}.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by the gamma‑glutamyltranspeptidase lineage. IPR000101 (Gamma-glutamyltranspeptidase family, residues 1–586) spans the enzyme’s core and marks it as a proenzyme that undergoes intramolecular processing into heavy and light chains typical of this family. Embedded within, IPR029055 (Nucleophile aminohydrolases, N‑terminal, residues 27–586) defines the N‑terminal nucleophile (Ntn) hydrolase fold that generates its own catalytic N‑terminus by autocatalysis. The positions of IPR043138 (Gamma‑glutamyltranspeptidase, large subunit, residues 270–379) and IPR043137 (Gamma‑glutamyltranspeptidase, small subunit, C‑terminal domain, residues 384–586) resolve the two-chain architecture: an N‑proximal large (heavy) subunit that helps shape the binding pocket, followed by the small (light) subunit that begins near the conserved catalytic region. IPR055262 (Gamma‑glutamyltranspeptidase, conserved site, residues 388–412) pinpoints the autocatalytic cleavage site that liberates the light chain’s N‑terminal threonine; this residue becomes the nucleophile that forms a covalent γ‑glutamyl‑enzyme intermediate. Together, these features specify the Ntn mechanism: the processed Thr at the light-chain N‑terminus attacks the γ‑glutamyl carbonyl of substrates to drive either hydrolysis (water as acceptor) or transpeptidation (an amino acid or peptide as acceptor).

The family-wide coverage and Ntn signature impose a chemistry that cleaves the γ‑glutamyl bond of glutathione and certain glutathione‑S‑conjugates. This directly supports GO:0036374 glutathione hydrolase activity, where the enzyme hydrolyzes the γ‑Glu–Cys linkage of glutathione or analogous conjugates. The same catalytic architecture accommodates leukotriene C4, a glutathione conjugate of an eicosanoid; removal of its γ‑glutamyl group yields leukotriene D4, consistent with GO:0002951 leukotriene‑C(4) hydrolase. Because the Ntn mechanism proceeds through a covalent acyl‑enzyme intermediate that can be resolved by amino acids instead of water, the active site inherently supports a transpeptidation route that forms new γ‑glutamyl linkages. This bond‑forming capability aligns with GO:0000048 peptidyltransferase activity in the sense of catalyzing formation of peptide-like linkages, here via γ‑glutamyl transfer to acceptor amino acids.

These molecular activities feed into defined metabolic pathways. By cleaving glutathione and its conjugates, the enzyme drives GO:0006751 glutathione catabolic process and contributes to the γ‑glutamyl cycle, which salvages amino acids—placing the enzyme within GO:0006520 amino acid metabolic process. Hydrolysis of leukotriene C4 to leukotriene D4 positions the reaction step within GO:1901750 leukotriene D4 biosynthetic process. Because leukotrienes derive from arachidonic acid, this step is embedded in GO:0006631 fatty acid metabolic process. The peptide-bond hydrolysis aspect qualifies under GO:0006508 proteolysis.

The topology implied by a single-pass type II membrane protein places a short N‑terminal cytosolic tail and a transmembrane anchor near the extreme N‑terminus, with the entire Ntn catalytic module (residues 27–586) projecting extracellularly. Such orientation situates the active site at the cell surface to process extracellular glutathione‑S‑conjugates and cysteinyl‑leukotrienes. This supports GO:0005886 plasma membrane as the principal cellular component.

Mechanistically, the proenzyme self‑activates by cleaving within the IPR055262 conserved segment to generate the light chain with an N‑terminal Thr nucleophile. Substrate binding within the pocket formed by the heavy chain (IPR043138) and light chain (IPR043137) positions the γ‑glutamyl group for nucleophilic attack, creating a γ‑glutamyl‑enzyme intermediate. Resolution by water yields hydrolysis products (e.g., cysteinylglycine from glutathione, leukotriene D4 from leukotriene C4); resolution by an external amino acid yields a transpeptidation product (new γ‑glutamyl compounds). The enzyme’s extracellular location and pocket geometry explain a preference for bulky glutathione‑S‑conjugates (such as leukotriene C4 or prenylated glutathione adducts) over small γ‑glutamyl dipeptides like γ‑Glu‑Leu.

In the pericellular space, this enzyme likely assembles into a metabolon that channels substrates and products across the glutathione and leukotriene pathways. Association with Aminopeptidase N and Cytosol aminopeptidase would enable sequential removal of the glycine from leukotriene D4 to form leukotriene E4, completing cysteinyl‑leukotriene catabolism at the cell surface or immediately downstream. Collaboration with Gamma‑glutamylcyclotransferase and 5‑oxoprolinase would recycle liberated γ‑glutamyl residues through the γ‑glutamyl cycle, while Glutathione synthetase resynthesizes glutathione from recovered components. Functional coupling with Leukotriene C4 synthase ensures substrate supply from the arachidonate pathway. Co-clustering with other family members—Glutathione hydrolase 1 proenzyme, Glutathione hydrolase 6, and Glutathione hydrolase 7—could broaden substrate range or regulate local activity. Interaction with Guanine nucleotide‑binding protein subunit beta‑5 suggests a link to GPCR signaling systems triggered by cysteinyl‑leukotrienes, enabling feedback control; by cleaving the γ‑glutamyl group from geranylgeranyl‑glutathione and related adducts, the enzyme likely dampens pro‑inflammatory signaling inputs during adaptive immune responses.

Consequently, the domain architecture mandates a membrane‑tethered Ntn hydrolase/transpeptidase that processes extracellular glutathione‑S‑conjugates. This causes glutathione hydrolase and leukotriene C4 hydrolase activities, which in turn drive glutathione turnover, amino acid salvage, and specific steps of eicosanoid remodeling at the plasma membrane, with optional transpeptidation generating γ‑glutamyl derivatives when suitable acceptors are present.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A type II membrane ectoenzyme that self-activates into heavy and light chains and uses an N-terminal threonine nucleophile to cleave the gamma‑glutamyl bond of glutathione and bulky glutathione‑S‑conjugates such as leukotriene C4 at the cell surface. By forming a covalent gamma‑glutamyl–enzyme intermediate that is resolved by water or by amino acid acceptors, it hydrolyzes glutathione conjugates to support amino acid salvage and can perform transpeptidation to generate new gamma‑glutamyl compounds. Its leukotriene C4 hydrolase step produces leukotriene D4 within the eicosanoid pathway, and cleavage of prenylated glutathione adducts modulates immune signaling. The enzyme likely functions within a plasma‑membrane metabolon with peptidases and glutathione‑cycle enzymes to coordinate substrate processing and signal termination.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by a canonical thioredoxin fold. A compact IPR013766 Thioredoxin domain spans residues 56–194, and within this core lie two reinforcing signatures: IPR036249 Thioredoxin-like superfamily from 89–193 and IPR005746 Thioredoxin family from 97–191. Centered inside the catalytic core sits IPR017937 Thioredoxin, conserved site at 110–128, which marks the active motif that houses the redox Cys pair that performs thiol–disulfide exchange. The domain begins only after an N-terminal extension (residues 1–55) that precedes the fold, an architecture frequently used in mammals to harbor a cleavable presequence that directs import into the mitochondrial matrix. In Sus scrofa, the presence of this N-terminal segment before a mature thioredoxin domain strongly points to a mitochondrial targeting presequence, followed by processing to expose the active CGPC-like motif inside the folded core.

The thioredoxin fold enforces protein disulfide oxidoreductase chemistry. The conserved site (110–128) positions the N-terminal cysteine for nucleophilic attack on a substrate disulfide, forming a transient mixed disulfide intermediate. The C-terminal cysteine in the motif then resolves this intermediate, releasing a reduced substrate and oxidizing the thioredoxin active site. Restoration of the active dithiol state is achieved by a dedicated FAD/NADPH-dependent thioredoxin reductase. This catalytic cycle requires transient, specific contacts with substrate proteins to form and resolve mixed disulfides, which directly implies protein binding (GO:0005515) as an enabling molecular function alongside protein disulfide oxidoreductase activity. The layered presence of IPR013766, IPR005746, and IPR036249 establishes that this chemistry is not incidental but the organizing principle of the protein.

From this chemistry, the biological roles follow. In a mammalian mitochondrion, a thioredoxin catalyzes reduction of disulfide bonds and reverses cysteine oxidation on matrix proteins, thereby maintaining cell redox homeostasis (GO:0045454) and supporting oxidation–reduction balance (GO:0055114). By continuously supplying electrons to mitochondrial peroxiredoxins, it contributes to the cellular response to oxidative stress (GO:0034599), lowering hydrogen peroxide levels and limiting oxidative injury. Its ability to reduce improperly formed disulfides also promotes protein folding and repair (GO:0006457) within the organelle, preventing aggregation of oxidatively damaged proteins.

The N-terminal extension preceding the thioredoxin domain indicates post-translational import into the organelle, and the lack of transmembrane signatures argues for a soluble compartment. Given how mammalian thioredoxin systems are organized, the most plausible location is the mitochondrial matrix (GO:0005759) within the broader mitochondrion (GO:0005739). In this compartment, the protein can encounter its reductase, antioxidant clients, and oxidized targets in close proximity.

A mechanistic model emerges: an N-terminal presequence directs import into the mitochondrial matrix, where the thioredoxin fold cycles between dithiol and disulfide states. The protein binds substrates via the conserved active-site motif (110–128), executes thiol–disulfide exchange to reduce client disulfides, and is in turn re-reduced by a mitochondrial thioredoxin reductase using NADPH. Likely partners follow from this circuitry. The upstream electron donor is the mitochondrial thioredoxin reductase, while downstream primary clients include mitochondrial peroxiredoxins that detoxify peroxides and methionine sulfoxide reductases that repair oxidized methionine residues. Additional transient associations with oxidized matrix enzymes would occur as dictated by stress conditions. Even though no explicit subcellular location or interaction partners are stated, the domain architecture and N-terminal extension in a mammalian context causally point to this mitochondrial redox maintenance module.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A soluble mitochondrial thiol–disulfide oxidoreductase in pig that uses a conserved redox-active cysteine pair to bind and reduce disulfide bonds in client proteins, thereby maintaining the organelle’s redox balance, fueling peroxide detoxification through peroxiredoxins, and facilitating repair/refolding of oxidatively damaged proteins. It likely receives electrons from a mitochondrial thioredoxin reductase and operates in the matrix through transient mixed-disulfide interactions with its substrates.

row.reasoning:
A compact redox fold dominates the sequence. IPR036249 (Thioredoxin-like superfamily, residues 22–156) covers the core of the protein and contains within it IPR013766 (Thioredoxin domain, residues 58–154). This nested arrangement is typical of a canonical thioredoxin architecture: a central βαβαββα topology harboring a Cys-based active site that cycles between a reduced dithiol and an oxidized disulfide. The location of the explicit thioredoxin domain toward the C-terminal half, embedded within the broader thioredoxin-like superfamily envelope, implies a small, soluble redox module whose catalytic cysteines are presented on a stable scaffold. There is no indication of transmembrane helices, so the molecule is most likely peripherally associated with membranes rather than embedded within them.

The thioredoxin fold dictates chemistry: it catalyzes reversible thiol–disulfide exchange. This architecture causes protein disulfide oxidoreductase activity (GO:0015035), enabling the protein to reduce or rearrange disulfide bonds in target polypeptides. In mitochondria, such chemistry typically operates on proteins exposed to the intermembrane space to set or reset disulfide states that govern complex assembly and stability.

The interaction landscape points to the mitochondrial cristae biogenesis hub. Association with MICOS complex subunit MIC10 and the assignment to the MICOS complex (GO:0061617) indicate that this thioredoxin-like protein acts at cristae junctions on the inner membrane. Redox control at MICOS is mechanistically reasonable: the cristae-shaping subunits undergo conformational and oligomeric transitions that are sensitive to cysteine oxidation, so a thioredoxin domain protein can directly tune their disulfide status to promote correct assembly. This links its molecular function to mitochondrial organization (GO:0007005), with a likely emphasis on cristae architecture. The partnership with a rhodanese domain-containing protein (a sulfurtransferase that forms and resolves persulfide intermediates) suggests a coupled sulfur/redox relay: the thioredoxin domain can resolve mixed disulfides or persulfide adducts generated during sulfur transfer, contributing to cell redox homeostasis (GO:0045454) while ensuring MICOS subunits remain in the functional redox state. Association with a generic transmembrane protein is consistent with inner-membrane anchoring, while the link to mitochondrial fission process protein 1 suggests coordination between cristae junction maintenance and membrane dynamics during division.

Together, the domain architecture compels a redox enzyme interpretation, the partner set anchors it at the inner mitochondrial membrane where MICOS resides, and the most coherent mechanistic model is a peripherally associated intermembrane-space–facing thioredoxin-like oxidoreductase that transiently forms mixed disulfides with MICOS components (e.g., MIC10) to chaperone their assembly and stabilize cristae junctions, while collaborating with a rhodanese sulfurtransferase to manage reactive sulfur species near the complex.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A small mitochondrial redox regulator that uses a thioredoxin fold to form and resolve transient disulfide bonds on proteins at cristae junctions. By tuning the redox state of MICOS subunits such as MIC10, it promotes proper assembly and stability of the cristae-shaping machinery at the inner membrane, likely coordinating with a sulfurtransferase and membrane partners to couple cristae organization with broader mitochondrial dynamics.

- IPR015943: WD40/YVTN repeat-like-containing domain superfamily (homologous_superfamily) [614-1012]

row.reasoning:
A single, continuous family signature dominates the sequence: IPR042505 (Cytoplasmic dynein 2 intermediate chain 1 family) spans residues 6–1058, essentially the entire polypeptide. This immediately sets the global identity: a noncatalytic subunit of the dynein-2 motor that powers retrograde transport along axonemal microtubules. Superimposed upon this family scaffold, the C-terminal half is densely packed with WD repeat architecture: IPR015943 (WD40/YVTN repeat-like-containing domain superfamily, 614–1012), IPR036322 (WD40-repeat-containing domain superfamily, 640–988), and the core repeat annotation IPR001680 (WD40 repeat, 686–983). The ordered nesting of these WD40 signatures in the distal region is characteristic of a seven-bladed beta-propeller. Such propellers create a rigid, solvent-exposed platform that enforces specificity in protein–protein recognition through shallow grooves and loops; they do not catalyze reactions but instead act as multivalent adaptors.

This architecture dictates molecular function. The dynein-2 intermediate chain family framework (IPR042505) positions the protein within a cytoskeletal motor complex, while the C-terminal WD40 propeller (IPR015943/IPR036322/IPR001680) provides the binding surface that recruits dynein light chains and cargo adaptors. The N-terminal portion of the family region (before the WD cluster) typically anchors to the dynein heavy-chain tail and light intermediate chain, enabling the intermediate chain to bridge the motor core to regulatory and cargo-binding modules. Consequently, the primary molecular activity is protein binding (GO:0005515), with a specific bias toward cytoskeletal protein binding (GO:0008092). The beta-propeller’s role as a connector that organizes multiple partners also fulfills molecular adaptor activity (GO:0060090).

From these molecular activities, the biological process follows. When an intermediate chain fastens light chains and cargo adaptors to the dynein-2 heavy chain, it constructs a functional retrograde intraflagellar transport machine. That assembly supports movement along microtubules at the ciliary axoneme, satisfying microtubule-based movement (GO:0007018). By enabling retrograde trafficking of IFT trains, the protein underpins the turnover and distribution of axonemal components, a prerequisite for building and maintaining the organelle, i.e., cilium organization (GO:0044782). Because this coupling moves protein complexes along defined tracks between ciliary tip and base, it contributes to intracellular protein transport (GO:0006886).

The cellular context is encoded both by motor identity and by an explicit localization label. Dynein-2 operates at the base of cilia and within the axoneme, assembling and loading at the pericentriolar region. This aligns with the centrosomal location (GO:0005813). As a stable constituent of a motor, it resides in the dynein complex (GO:0030286) and functions within the cytoplasm (GO:0005737). Given its role in ciliary transport, it is also expected in the cilium (GO:0005929), including the axoneme (GO:0005930).

Mechanistically, the N-terminal region within the dynein-2 intermediate chain family domain couples to the dynein-2 heavy chain and the light intermediate chain, while the C-terminal WD40 beta-propeller forms a hub for light chains (e.g., LC8/DYNLL, Tctex-type, LC7/Roadblock) and intraflagellar transport adaptors. At the centrosome and ciliary base, this assembly docks onto IFT trains, converting ATP-driven conformational changes from the heavy chain into productive, retrograde carriage of ciliary cargo. In this model, likely interaction partners include the dynein-2 heavy chain (DYNC2H1), the dynein-2 light intermediate chain (DYNC2LI1), dynein light chains (DYNLL1/2, DYNLRB1/2, DYNLT1/3), and IFT-B components (such as IFT88, IFT57, IFT52, and IFT172) that form the trains to which cargo is attached. Together these contacts generate a cohesive motor–adaptor platform that sustains cilium homeostasis.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A cytosolic scaffold subunit of the dynein-2 motor that uses a C‑terminal WD‑repeat beta‑propeller to assemble light chains and cargo adaptors onto the dynein core. By bridging the motor to intraflagellar transport trains at the centrosome and within the cilium, it enables retrograde movement along axonemal microtubules, thereby supporting cilium maintenance and the distribution of ciliary components.

row.reasoning:
A compact N-terminal module, IPR057541 (Phosphofurin acidic cluster sorting protein 1/2, N-terminal C2 domain, residues 68–149), sits at the front of a much larger platform, IPR019381 (Phosphofurin acidic cluster sorting protein 1/2, C-terminal, residues 68–857). C2 domains create a Ca2+-responsive, anionic-phospholipid–binding surface on the cytosolic face of membranes. That targeting cue positions the polypeptide where vesicles form or fuse, and it provides a switchable membrane anchor whose affinity increases with local Ca2+. The extensive C-terminal PACS1/2-specific region is characteristic of cargo-recognition scaffolds that read phosphorylated acidic-cluster motifs on client proteins and present those clients to coat/adaptor complexes. This arrangement—an N-terminal C2 membrane-targeting module feeding into a long C-terminal cargo-recognition/scaffold region—drives an adaptor mechanism: the protein binds membranes in a Ca2+-dependent manner while simultaneously engaging cargo and coat machinery.

From this architecture, the immediate molecular activities follow. The C2 domain causes calcium-dependent phospholipid binding (GO:0005544), and the extended scaffold necessitates multivalent protein binding (GO:0005515) to capture cargo and partners. By bridging cargo proteins to coat complexes at bud sites, the scaffold carries out clathrin adaptor activity (GO:0035615), enabling sorting of acidic-cluster–containing cargos into specific transport carriers.

Coupling Ca2+-sensitive membrane binding with cargo selection naturally channels traffic between endosomes and the trans-Golgi network, a route used by proteases and receptors bearing acidic-cluster signals. Thus the primary process is vesicle-mediated transport (GO:0016192), with a specific role in retrograde transport from endosome to Golgi (GO:0042147). The same modular logic also extends to organelle-directed protein movement: a C2-anchored scaffold can hand off select clients to the mitochondrial surface, supporting protein targeting to mitochondrion (GO:0006626).

The mechanistic need for a cytosolic, membrane-proximal scaffold places the protein on the cytosolic side of organelles where coat assembly occurs (cytosol, GO:0005829; trans-Golgi network, GO:0005802; endosome, GO:0005768). The explicit involvement in delivering or regulating factors at the mitochondrial surface is consistent with presence at the mitochondrion (GO:0005739), where cargo transfer can influence mitochondrial function and signaling.

Putting these elements together, a working model emerges: the C2 domain docks the protein onto acidic membranes in a Ca2+-modulated fashion; the C-terminal PACS scaffold recognizes phosphorylated acidic-cluster motifs on cargo and simultaneously binds coat adaptors. At the trans-Golgi network and endosomes, it recruits AP and clathrin modules to package cargos such as furin and mannose-6-phosphate receptors into retrograde carriers. At the mitochondrial surface, it can transiently engage import-associated or apoptotic regulators to direct client delivery. Likely partners therefore include clathrin (CLTC), adaptor complexes (AP-1 subunits AP1G1/AP1M1), GGA adaptors (GGA1/GGA3), and the retromer core (VPS35/VPS26A) for endosome–Golgi traffic; kinases such as casein kinase 2 (CSNK2A1) that generate phosphorylated acidic clusters on cargo; canonical acidic-cluster cargos (FURIN, IGF2R/CI-MPR); and mitochondrial-surface factors (TOMM20) or BH3-only clients (BID) whose delivery modulates mitochondrial state.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A cytosolic, Ca2+-regulated membrane adaptor that reads phosphorylated acidic-cluster motifs on client proteins and couples them to coat machinery to drive vesicle sorting. By docking to anionic membranes through its C2 module and using a large C-terminal scaffold to bind cargo and adaptors, it promotes endosome–to–trans-Golgi retrograde transport and can also direct selected clients to the mitochondrial surface, thereby linking trafficking cues to mitochondrial function.

row.protein_function: Protein mannosyltransferase (PMT) involved in hyphal growth and drug sensitivity. Transfers mannose from Dol-P-mannose to Ser or Thr residues on proteins. PMT1, PMT2 and PMT4 account for most of the protein-O-glycosylation activity, while PMT5 and PMT6 may specifically modulate a much narrower spectrum of target proteins. Accounts for the O-glycosylation of AXL2, responsible for bud site selection, as well as of the SEC20 t-SNARE component. O-glycosylation of SEC20 is essential for its stability. Required for biofilm formation.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is characteristic of a lipid-linked sugar–dependent glycosyltransferase embedded in the secretory pathway membrane. The N-terminal half (residues 53–298) carries IPR003342 (ArnT-like, N-terminal domain), a hallmark of GT-C–type enzymes that use polyisoprenyl phosphate–activated sugars. This domain organizes early transmembrane segments that position the lipid-linked donor within the membrane and establish the topology of the catalytic cavity toward the lumen. The central luminal loop (residues 325–523) bears IPR016093 (MIR motif) nested within IPR036300 (Mir domain superfamily), a peptide-recognition module known to bind polypeptide segments and to modulate glycosyltransferase activity. Its placement between transmembrane bundles causes the lumenal exposure of a substrate-capturing surface that engages nascent secretory proteins as they enter the ER. The C-terminal region (residues 545–748) contains IPR032421 (Protein O-mannosyl-transferase, C-terminal four TM domain), the signature transmembrane block that shapes the active site pocket and the dolichyl-phosphate–mannose (DPM) donor-binding channel. The overall protein is classified by IPR027005 (O-mannosyl-transferase-like, residues 33–748), which consolidates the assignment to the PMT family. The ordered arrangement—ArnT-like TM core → MIR-containing lumenal loop → PMT-specific C-terminal TM bundle—creates a lumen-facing catalytic cavity that couples DPM binding to transfer of mannose onto Ser/Thr of polypeptide substrates. This architecture gives rise to mannosyl donor transfer chemistry, i.e., GO:0000030 mannosyltransferase activity.

Because the MIR loop captures unfolded or partially folded secretory proteins as they translocate, the enzyme executes O-mannosyl attachment co- or early post-translationally, establishing GO:0035269 protein O-linked mannosylation as the primary process. O-mannosylation stabilizes and matures cell-surface and cell wall proteins; by enhancing folding and shielding protease-accessible segments, it directly enacts GO:0045861 negative regulation of proteolysis. The modified clients populate the cell wall and plasma membrane, where they control morphogenesis and adhesion. Consequently, the enzyme’s activity drives GO:0031505 fungal-type cell wall organization and underpins adhesive community behaviors captured by GO:0044011 single-species biofilm formation on inanimate substrate. In Candida, morphogenetic transitions at neutral pH demand robust surface signaling and adhesion; by ensuring the stability and function of these pH-responsive effectors, the enzyme mediates GO:0036244 cellular response to neutral pH and fuels GO:0036178 filamentous growth of a population of unicellular organisms in response to neutral pH.

Topologically, the GT-C scaffold with multiple transmembrane helices and a lumenal MIR loop dictates integration into the endoplasmic reticulum membrane, where DPM arrives after flipping from the cytosolic leaflet and where secretory polypeptides are first accessible. Thus, the protein operates as an integral component of membrane at the endoplasmic reticulum. The downstream consequences of its modifications are realized at the plasma membrane and cell wall; some PMT family members have been detected in later secretory compartments, so a pool at the plasma membrane is plausible and consistent with membrane-associated functional outputs.

Mechanistically, the enzyme likely couples donor supply to catalysis via physical proximity to DPM biosynthetic regulators. Association with a Dolichol phosphate-mannose biosynthesis regulatory protein would channel DPM into the catalytic site, maximizing transfer efficiency. The MIR loop captures substrates such as the bud-site selection factor Axl2 and the t-SNARE Sec20; mannosylation of Sec20 prevents its degradation, explaining the negative regulation of proteolysis. After glycosylation, properly modified cargo engage ER export receptors such as Erv29p, suggesting transient coordination with Erv29p to ensure efficient ER-to-Golgi trafficking of glycoproteins. Through this network, the enzyme secures the cell-surface proteome required for cell wall architecture, biofilm formation, and neutral-pH-induced filamentation.

This chain of inference—from ArnT-like and PMT-specific transmembranes flanking a MIR recognition loop (IPR003342, IPR016093/IPR036300, IPR032421; within the O-mannosyl-transferase-like family IPR027005)—causally yields mannosyltransferase activity (GO:0000030), drives protein O-linked mannosylation (GO:0035269), stabilizes proteins to reduce proteolysis (GO:0045861), and thereby organizes the fungal cell wall (GO:0031505), promotes biofilm formation (GO:0044011), and enables neutral pH responses and filamentation (GO:0036244, GO:0036178). The multi-pass topology places the enzyme in the endoplasmic reticulum membrane and functionally links it to the plasma membrane proteome (GO:0005886).
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A multi-pass glycosyltransferase embedded in the endoplasmic reticulum that uses dolichyl-phosphate–mannose to O-mannosylate serine and threonine residues on nascent secretory proteins. A lumen-exposed recognition loop recruits substrates as they enter the ER, and a transmembrane catalytic core transfers mannose to stabilize and mature client proteins. By securing the stability of key surface and trafficking factors, this enzyme shapes cell wall architecture, promotes biofilm formation, and enables filamentous growth at neutral pH; its activity protects clients from proteolysis and ensures efficient export through early secretory cargo receptors, with functional consequences manifested at the plasma membrane.

row.protein_function: Structural component of lens fiber gap junctions. Gap junctions are dodecameric channels that connect the cytoplasm of adjoining cells. They are formed by the docking of two hexameric hemichannels, one from each cell membrane. Small molecules and ions diffuse from one cell to a neighboring cell via the central pore.
