row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by connexin signatures. IPR000500 (Connexin family, residues 1–338) defines a four-pass membrane pore-forming scaffold that oligomerizes into hexameric hemichannels. The N-terminal assembly and pore-lining region are specified by IPR013092 (Connexin, N-terminal domain, residues 3–233) within IPR038359 (Connexin, N-terminal domain superfamily, residues 2–242), which is the gating element that lines the inner vestibule and confers voltage/pH sensitivity. Embedded within this region is IPR017990 (Connexin, conserved site, residues 54–203), marking the hallmark motifs essential for channel assembly and selectivity. The extracellular docking apparatus is delineated by IPR019570 (Connexin, cysteine-rich domain, residues 165–231), whose conserved cysteines form disulfide-stabilized extracellular loops that drive head-to-head docking of two hemichannels to create a continuous intercellular conduit. The cytosolic regulatory tail is captured by IPR034634 (Connexin, C-terminal homologous superfamily, residues 268–417), a modulatory region that tunes gating and partner binding. IPR002262 (Gap junction alpha-3 protein, Cx46 family, residues 180–403) narrows the subtype to the lens-dominant alpha connexin class that assembles robust intercellular channels in fiber cells.

This ordered layout causes the polypeptide to assemble into a hexameric hemichannel in the plasma membrane and, via disulfide-locked extracellular loops, to dock with an opposing hemichannel at cell–cell contacts, forming a dodecameric gap junction pore. The resulting conduit enables passive diffusion of ions and small metabolites between neighboring cells, which corresponds to gap junction channel activity (GO:0005243) and passive transmembrane transporter activity (GO:0022803) as molecular functions. By enabling direct cytoplasmic continuity, these channels drive cell–cell signaling (GO:0007267) and intercellular transport (GO:0046903). In the lens, sustained coupling among avascular fiber cells supports transparency and homeostasis, aligning with lens development in camera-type eye (GO:0002088).

The multi-pass architecture and docking loops position the protein at the plasma membrane and at specialized cell–cell junctions; thus, it resides in the plasma membrane (GO:0005886), where it concentrates in gap junction plaques (GO:0005921). As a connexin, it is an integral component of membrane (GO:0016021).

Mechanistically, six subunits oligomerize into a connexon that inserts into the plasma membrane; the cysteine-rich extracellular loops mediate disulfide-stabilized docking with a partner connexon from an adjacent cell, creating an aqueous pore gated by the N-terminal funnel and modulated by the C-terminal tail. In the lens epithelium and fiber cells, this connexon can form heteromeric hemichannels or heterotypic intercellular channels with other connexins. Therefore, plausible interaction partners include Gap junction gamma-1, alpha-8, alpha-5, beta-2, alpha-4, alpha-1, and beta-1 proteins for co-assembly or heterotypic docking. Structural and homeostatic coupling to lens cytoplasm suggests indirect or crowding-modulated associations with Beta-crystallin B1, Beta-crystallin B2, and Gamma-crystallin S that influence channel stability and lens transparency.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A multi-pass membrane channel that assembles as a hexameric hemichannel and docks head-to-head across adjacent cells to form gap junction pores in lens fiber cells. Its N-terminal funnel gates the pore, conserved extracellular cysteines lock intercellular docking, and the cytosolic tail tunes gating and interactions. The channel provides passive, direct cytoplasmic continuity for ions and small metabolites, supporting intercellular signaling and homeostatic coupling essential for lens development and transparency at cell–cell junctions in the plasma membrane.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by a conserved NIPSNAP module. An N-terminal family signature IPR051557 (NipSnap domain-containing protein, residues 36–284) spans most of the polypeptide and houses a C-terminal IPR012577 (NIPSNAP domain, residues 185–282). These signatures sit within IPR011008 (Dimeric alpha-beta barrel superfamily, residues 67–284), indicating the fold is a soluble α/β barrel that tends to dimerize. Dimeric α/β barrels typically create solvent-accessible pockets at subunit interfaces; in NIPSNAP-like proteins this pocket is enriched for polar and aromatic residues that stabilize small, protonatable ligands such as biogenic amines. This architecture therefore enforces two baseline capacities: small-molecule binding via the barrel pocket and polyvalent protein binding via exposed loops and termini around the barrel.

Amino-binding pockets of this sort are well-suited to engage neurotransmitters—a chemically coherent class of small cationic or aromatic compounds—supporting a direct ligand-binding activity that matches the concept formalized as GO:0042165 neurotransmitter binding. The broad surface area and loop flexibility of the dimeric barrel, together with short linear motifs commonly present in NIPSNAP family members, also favor transient docking of cytosolic partners, consistent with GO:0005515 protein binding. In NIPSNAP homologs, such motifs often contact ATG8-family proteins; this is echoed here by the observed partners Microtubule-associated protein 1 light chain 3 beta (LC3B) and the autophagy adaptors Tax1-binding protein 1 (TAX1BP1) and Calcium-binding and coiled-coil domain-containing protein 2 (CALCOCO2/NDP52).

The lack of predicted transmembrane helices, combined with frequent mitochondrial targeting in this family, points to a soluble organellar residence. A matrix-localized dimer that peripherally associates with membranes can contact or sense the mitochondrial inner membrane, aligning with the concept of GO:0005743 mitochondrial inner membrane as an interaction surface while primarily residing in the mitochondrial matrix (subcellular location). Neurons position mitochondria beneath the synaptic membrane to sustain transmission; a matrix protein enriched in synaptic mitochondria will co-fractionate with synaptic compartments, explaining an association with the GO:0097060 synaptic membrane environment without being an intrinsic synaptic membrane component.

Mechanistically, the NIPSNAP fold places a neurotransmitter/amine-sensing pocket into the mitochondrial matrix where it can report local neurotransmission-linked metabolic states. Under mitochondrial stress in active neuronal terminals, conformational changes or damage-induced exposure enable the NIPSNAP surface to be recognized from the cytosol, where it can recruit LC3B directly and scaffold TAX1BP1 and CALCOCO2. This assembly nucleates selective autophagosome formation around compromised mitochondria—mitophagy (GO:0000422)—preserving organelle quality at synapses. In sensory neurons, such mitochondrial quality control modulates excitability and transmitter release, thereby influencing nociceptive circuits and contributing to the higher-level outcome described by GO:0019233 sensory perception of pain.

In summary, an N-terminally extended, C-terminal NIPSNAP domain embedded in a dimeric α/β barrel creates a small-molecule-binding pocket for neurotransmitters and an adaptable protein-binding surface. In neuronal mitochondria positioned at synapses, this architecture couples local ligand sensing to recruitment of LC3B, TAX1BP1, and CALCOCO2, triggering mitophagy at the synaptic vicinity and shaping pain-related neuronal function.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A dimeric, soluble mitochondrial protein that resides in the matrix and engages the inner membrane, where its barrel-shaped fold harbors a pocket for small amine ligands typical of neurotransmitters and presents flexible surfaces for partner docking. In synaptic mitochondria, this ligand-sensing scaffold recruits LC3-family proteins and autophagy adaptors to assemble mitophagy machinery around damaged organelles, thereby maintaining mitochondrial quality at nerve terminals and indirectly tuning nociceptive signaling.

row.reasoning:
The sequence is dominated by the transthyretin/hydroxyisourate hydrolase fold. A compact core spans residues 22–150, captured both as IPR023416 (Transthyretin/hydroxyisourate hydrolase domain) and its umbrella IPR036817 (Transthyretin/hydroxyisourate hydrolase domain superfamily), indicating a conserved β-sandwich module known to dimerize and assemble into homotetramers. Nested within this core is IPR000895 (Transthyretin/hydroxyisourate hydrolase family, residues 34–122), which distinguishes the vertebrate transthyretin lineage from catalytically active uricolytic enzymes: in transthyretins, catalytic residues are absent, and the fold is repurposed to bind iodinated thyroid hormones. Two short signatures specify function and assembly: IPR023418 (Transthyretin, thyroxine binding site, residues 38–53) marks the hormone-binding channel formed at the dimer–dimer interface, while IPR023419 (Transthyretin, conserved site, residues 128–140) anchors quaternary structure and stabilizes the tetramer.

This architecture causes two molecular activities. First, the presence of the defined iodothyronine-binding stretch (IPR023418 at 38–53) creates a hydrophobic, iodide-accommodating cavity that binds thyroid hormones, establishing GO:0070324 thyroid hormone binding. Amphibian transthyretins are known to favor triiodothyronine (T3) over thyroxine (T4), a preference that can be rationalized by the cavity geometry implied by the transthyretin family signature (IPR000895) embedded within the tetrameric channel. Second, oligomerization is an intrinsic requirement for forming the binding tunnel; the conserved site (IPR023419, 128–140) helps drive assembly into a dimer of dimers, directly entailing GO:0042802 identical protein binding.

Once a high-affinity T3-binding tetramer exists, its physiological role follows: the protein acts as a soluble carrier that captures thyroid hormone and ferries it through body fluids, fitting GO:0070327 thyroid hormone transport. The secretion-competent fold and the need to distribute hormone systemically imply an extracellular itinerary. A secreted tetramer operating in plasma and cerebrospinal fluid aligns with GO:0005576 extracellular region. In amphibians, this supports delivery of T3 to the choroid plexus and developing brain, where precise T3 levels orchestrate metamorphic gene programs.

Mechanistically, the protein likely forms a stable homotetramer in the extracellular milieu, creating two central channels that preferentially bind T3. Circulating complexes can transiently associate with extracellular partners that mediate tissue uptake. A plausible route is handoff at the choroid plexus: the tetramer may engage endocytic clearance/uptake receptors such as LRP2/megalin to traverse epithelial barriers. Complex formation with retinol-binding protein 4 (RBP4) is also expected for transthyretin-family carriers, stabilizing vitamin A transport while modulating hormone carriage. At target cells, proximity to the T3 transporter MCT8 (SLC16A2) could facilitate hormone delivery after local release. Albumin in plasma acts as a competing, lower-affinity binder that can exchange hormone with the tetramer. Together, the domain-defined tetramerization (GO:0042802) and hormone-binding channel (GO:0070324) cause extracellular transport (GO:0070327) in the extracellular region (GO:0005576), enabling targeted T3 distribution to the brain in the American bullfrog.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A secreted homotetrameric carrier that builds a central hormone-binding channel to capture and stabilize triiodothyronine in extracellular fluids, enabling efficient delivery of thyroid hormone to target tissues such as the brain. The tetramer forms via self-association of the transthyretin fold, and hormone is likely handed off at epithelial interfaces and near thyroid hormone transporters to drive precise endocrine signaling during development.

row.go_bp_leaf: GO:0042060 wound healing
row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by RNA-focused modules arranged in a canonical splicing-factor layout. A CCCH-type zinc finger is present at residues 165–192, captured both by IPR000571 (Zinc finger, CCCH-type) and its superfamily IPR036855 (Zinc finger, CCCH-type superfamily). Immediately downstream lies an RNA recognition motif from 197–304 identified by IPR000504 (RNA recognition motif domain), whose fold is further supported by IPR012677 (Nucleotide-binding alpha-beta plait domain superfamily, 197–303) and IPR035979 (RNA-binding domain superfamily, 235–306). The ensemble from 158–313 is classified by IPR009145 (U2 auxiliary factor small subunit family), which is a hallmark of the U2AF small subunit found in metazoans. This ordered layout—CCCH zinc finger directly preceding an RRM—defines a module that reads RNA sequence features at the 3′ splice site while forming a stable heterodimer with the U2AF large subunit.

The CCCH zinc finger (165–192) coordinates Zn2+ through cysteine and histidine ligands. This metal coordination stiffens the local structure and creates a compact nucleic-acid/protein-contact surface that specializes in recognizing short RNA motifs, notably the AG dinucleotide at the 3′ splice site of introns. The adjacent RRM (197–304), built on an alpha–beta plait core, provides an RNA-binding scaffold and interfaces for protein partners. Together, this zinc finger–RRM tandem causes selective pre-mRNA recognition at exon–intron junctions and stabilizes assembly of the early spliceosome. The family assignment to the U2 auxiliary factor small subunit (158–313) specifies that this protein forms a heterodimer with the U2AF large subunit, creating an adaptor that couples 3′ splice-site recognition to recruitment of the U2 snRNP to the branchpoint region. Mechanistically, the small subunit’s zinc finger captures the 3′ AG, while the large subunit (via its own RRMs) binds the polypyrimidine tract; the small subunit’s RRM-like core supports these contacts and engages additional splicing factors.

From these domain-level causes, the molecular functions follow. The RRM and the RNA-binding superfamily signatures directly entail RNA binding. The CCCH zinc finger requires and confers zinc ion binding, and the U2AF family role implies a protein heterodimerization activity necessary to assemble the U2AF complex. The zinc finger’s selective recognition of the 3′ splice-site AG further refines the function to pre-mRNA 3′ splice site binding. These molecular activities converge to drive the biological process of mRNA splicing via the spliceosome, and, given the regulatory positioning of U2AF at splice-site choice, they also support regulation of alternative mRNA splicing via the spliceosome.

Splicing occurs cotranscriptionally in the nucleus, frequently within or near nuclear speckles that concentrate spliceosomal components. The absence of transmembrane features and the strong spliceosomal signature argue for a nuclear, nucleoplasmic localization with dynamic residence in the spliceosomal complex. Through splicing control of gene sets that govern cell migration, cytoskeletal remodeling, and inflammation, this protein’s activity plausibly contributes to organismal wound healing. In Drosophila melanogaster, tissue repair requires rapid reprogramming of transcripts; by enabling efficient recognition of 3′ splice sites and promoting U2 snRNP recruitment, this factor would modulate isoform production needed for wound closure.

A coherent mechanistic hypothesis is that the protein forms the small subunit of the U2AF heterodimer, binding Zn2+ via its CCCH motif to stabilize contacts with the 3′ splice-site AG while its RRM-like core engages RNA and protein partners. It likely dimerizes with the U2AF large subunit (U2AF65/U2af50), bridges to Splicing Factor 1 (SF1) at the branchpoint, and hands off to U2 snRNP components (e.g., SF3A/SF3B subunits), operating within nuclear speckles and the spliceosomal complex. These assemblies adjust splicing patterns of wound-response transcripts, thereby influencing wound healing outcomes.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: An RNA-focused splicing factor in fruit fly that uses a CCCH zinc finger adjacent to an RNA-recognition module to read the 3′ splice site of pre-mRNAs and assemble with a larger partner into the U2 auxiliary factor. By stabilizing early spliceosome formation at exon–intron junctions in the nucleus and nuclear speckles, it promotes accurate and regulated splicing of transcripts, thereby shaping gene-expression programs that support tissue repair and wound closure.

- IPR046348: SIS domain superfamily (homologous_superfamily) [45-207]

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is compact and enzyme-like. IPR017552 (3-hexulose-6-phosphate isomerase family) spans essentially the full polypeptide from residues 1–208, indicating a single-function enzyme whose core chemistry matches that family. Nested within this, IPR001347 (SIS domain, residues 44–195) sits inside IPR046348 (SIS domain superfamily, residues 45–207). This layered arrangement shows that the protein is built around one catalytic SIS domain, with no obvious auxiliary modules. SIS domains form a conserved α/β fold that grips phosphorylated sugars; conserved basic residues coordinate the phosphate while a catalytic lysine/serine pair promotes ring opening and proton transfer through a cis-enediol(ate) intermediate. When this chemistry is embedded in the IPR017552 family context across nearly the entire sequence, the active site geometry is tuned for the reversible isomerization of 3-hexulose-6-phosphate to fructose-6-phosphate.

The absence of transmembrane segments or signal peptides and the small size of the single-domain enzyme argue for solubility in the cytosol. SIS-family isomerases typically act as soluble dimers or higher-order oligomers in the cytoplasm. That placement is consistent with GO:0005829 cytosol and with a role feeding central carbon metabolism in Arabidopsis thaliana, where fructose-6-phosphate is a key glycolytic and hexose-phosphate hub.

From this architecture, the molecular function follows directly: it catalyzes an intramolecular redox isomerization between an aldose and a ketose within a phosphorylated hexose, most specifically the 3-hexulose-6-phosphate isomerization characteristic of this family. The immediate biochemical outcome is production/consumption of fructose-6-phosphate, linking the enzyme to hexose phosphate metabolic process and broader carbohydrate metabolism. In organisms that generate 3-hexulose-6-phosphate via condensation of formaldehyde with ribulose-5-phosphate, this isomerase closes the entry point of a ribulose monophosphate-type route by converting that adduct into fructose-6-phosphate; in plants, such chemistry would contribute to detoxification/assimilation of formaldehyde and to balancing cytosolic sugar-phosphate pools.

Mechanistically, the SIS catalytic core binds a hexose phosphate; a catalytic base facilitates ring opening, proton transfer via a cis-enediolate intermediate interconverts aldose and ketose, and the phosphate-binding pocket ensures specificity for phosphorylated substrates. The soluble location and the product fructose-6-phosphate imply functional coupling to cytosolic carbohydrate enzymes. The most plausible partners are the enzyme that generates the substrate (a 3-hexulose-6-phosphate synthase, if present) and downstream cytosolic nodes that receive fructose-6-phosphate, such as phosphoglucose isomerase and phosphofructokinase. Because the substrate derives from ribulose-5-phosphate, association with pentose phosphate pathway enzymes (ribose-5-phosphate isomerase, ribulose-phosphate 3-epimerase, and transketolase) would facilitate metabolite channeling. If the physiological role includes formaldehyde handling, transient coupling to glutathione-dependent formaldehyde dehydrogenase could integrate detoxification with carbon salvage in the cytosol.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A small cytosolic sugar‑phosphate isomerase in Arabidopsis that uses a single SIS catalytic domain to interconvert a hexulose‑6‑phosphate and fructose‑6‑phosphate through a cis‑enediol intermediate. By producing fructose‑6‑phosphate in the cytosol, it feeds central carbon metabolism and likely participates in a carbon‑salvage/detoxification route that processes formaldehyde‑derived hexulose‑phosphate adducts, functioning as a soluble enzyme that couples to pentose‑phosphate and glycolytic nodes.

row.reasoning:
The sequence is dominated by two related InterPro signatures arranged in a compact architecture. A family-level signature, IPR044281 (U3 snoRNP protein/Ribosome production factor 1 family) spans residues 8–295, indicating a protein that is specialized for early, nucleolar steps of ribosome assembly. Nested within it, IPR007109 (Brix domain, residues 93–276) forms the conserved structural core. The Brix fold is a basic, RNA-interacting module used by large-subunit assembly factors to recognize structured segments of nascent pre-rRNA and simultaneously present surfaces for partner proteins. The placement of the Brix domain centrally within the family envelope suggests that the N- and C-terminal flanks tune interactions with neighboring assembly factors on the maturing particle rather than adding catalytic activity. The absence of enzymatic motifs implies a scaffolding and RNA-binding role rather than RNA cleavage or modification.

This architecture compels two molecular functions. First, the Brix domain supplies an RNA-binding surface suited to engage the long, structured rRNA primary transcript as it emerges in the nucleolus, consistent with GO:0042134 rRNA primary transcript binding. Second, the Brix fold characteristically creates composite binding grooves for ribosome biogenesis factors, accounting for GO:0005515 protein binding. Together, these capabilities enable the protein to dock onto early large subunit precursors and nucleate multicomponent assemblies.

Anchoring to the primary rRNA transcript places the factor at the juncture where 5.8S rRNA and LSU rRNA mature from the tricistronic precursor. By stabilizing and positioning helices around the internal transcribed spacer 2 (ITS2) region and adjacent domains of 25S rRNA, the Brix core provides the geometric cues that permit stepwise processing. This directly drives GO:0000466 maturation of 5.8S rRNA from the tricistronic transcript and GO:0000463 maturation of LSU-rRNA from the same transcript. Completion of these steps licenses construction of an export-competent pre-60S particle; by organizing these early assembly events, the factor thereby enables GO:0000055 ribosomal large subunit export from nucleus. The essential nature of the protein follows from this positioning: failure to scaffold these early pre-60S transactions stalls the entire 60S pathway.

The cellular context follows from the chemistry. Engagement with the unprocessed rRNA primary transcript occurs in the nucleolus, where rDNA is transcribed and early ribosome assembly takes place. The factor therefore resides within GO:0005730 nucleolus and associates specifically with GO:0030687 preribosome, large subunit precursor. The family signature’s link to U3 snoRNP-regulated early assembly further supports a nucleolar stage of action rather than cytoplasmic maturation.

A coherent mechanistic picture emerges. The Brix domain binds a structured pocket of the 35S pre-rRNA encompassing the future 25S and 5.8S neighborhoods and recruits a defined set of LSU assembly factors to remodel and stabilize this region. ATP-dependent RNA helicase HAS1 supplies the energy to unwind or reposition local helices, enabling factor handover. RLP7 and rRNA-processing protein EBP2 occupy ITS2-proximal rRNA, cooperating with the Brix surface to clamp RNA conformations. Pescadillo homolog and Protein MAK16 help build the architectural scaffold of the forming 60S subunit, while Nucleolar complex protein 2 integrates this module with broader nucleolar assembly pathways. Proteasome-interacting protein CIC1 (Nsa3) and Ribosome biogenesis protein 15/Ribosomal RNA-processing protein 15 likely strengthen the pre-60S interface and coordinate turnover or exchange of factors. Direct contact with 25S rRNA rationalizes substrate recognition and explains how precise stabilization of rRNA helical elements enables both maturation of 5.8S and 25S rRNA and the transition to an export-competent pre-60S particle. Through this RNA-centric scaffold-and-remodel mechanism in the nucleolus, the protein performs an indispensable, early-acting role in 60S ribosomal subunit biogenesis and nuclear export readiness.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: An essential nucleolar assembly factor that uses a Brix RNA-binding core to anchor onto nascent pre-rRNA within early large subunit precursors, scaffolding the recruitment of remodeling and stabilization factors. By shaping RNA structure around the junction that yields 5.8S and 25S rRNAs and coordinating ATP-dependent remodeling, it promotes maturation of these rRNAs and builds an export-competent pre-60S particle for delivery from the nucleus.

row.reasoning:
A single N-to-C architecture emerges from the domain signatures. The polypeptide core is defined by IPR053050 (Acetolactate synthase regulatory subunit, family, residues 7–309), marking the sequence as the regulatory small subunit that partners with the catalytic acetolactate synthase. Nested within this, multiple overlapping ACT-fold signatures—IPR054480 (Acetolactate synthase small subunit-like, ACT domain, residues 80–146), IPR002912 (ACT domain, residues 79–159), IPR039557 (AHAS, ACT domain, residues 78–148), and the broad IPR045865 (ACT-like domain, homologous_superfamily, residues 77–282)—define a central ligand-sensing module. Downstream, the C-terminal half carries IPR027271 (Acetolactate synthase/Transcription factor NikR, C-terminal, homologous_superfamily, residues 156–280), which supplies the regulatory C-terminal ACT-like extension that typically mediates dimerization and signaling to partner enzymes, and the more specific IPR019455 (Acetolactate synthase, small subunit, C-terminal, residues 219–280), which specializes the interface for productive docking onto the catalytic subunit. Concordance with IPR004789 (Acetolactate synthase, small subunit, family, residues 76–282) further anchors the identity and span of the regulatory core. The ordered layout—an N-terminal regulatory-subunit scaffold feeding into a central ACT sensor and a tailored C-terminal coupling interface—creates a dedicated allosteric controller for the catalytic partner.

The ACT fold is a classic small-molecule sensor for amino acids. In this architecture, ligand occupancy in the ACT pocket stabilizes specific conformational states of the small-subunit dimer, and those states are transmitted through the C-terminal interface (IPR027271/IPR019455) to the catalytic subunit’s thiamine diphosphate (ThDP) active site. This causally explains enzyme regulator activity (GO:0030234): the protein modulates the catalytic partner’s kinetics rather than carrying out chemistry itself. Effector logic follows from the sensor: binding of branched-chain amino acids, notably valine, would favor an inhibitory conformation, whereas ATP binding at or near the intersubunit interface biases the active state. The consequence at the holoenzyme level is acetolactate synthase activity (GO:0003984), i.e., ThDP-dependent condensation of pyruvate (or 2-ketobutyrate) to acetolactate (or acetohydroxybutyrate). The regulatory subunit’s architecture explains the observed seven- to tenfold stimulation of the catalytic subunit’s turnover by stabilizing the active conformation and organizing the catalytic subunit’s cofactors for optimal chemistry, while also conferring valine sensitivity and ATP activation through the ACT-centered allosteric network.

This reaction initiates flux into the branched-chain amino acid biosynthetic process (GO:0009082). By placing an allosteric sensor at the gatekeeper step, the cell couples pathway entry to the current metabolic state: valine excess triggers feedback inhibition to limit further synthesis, while ATP abundance signals energetic capacity to drive biosynthesis, thereby activating flux. The presence of a dedicated regulatory subunit causes this flux control to be both sensitive and sharply transduced.

Localization follows from both targeting logic and pathway topology. The regulatory subunit family context (IPR053050/IPR004789) commonly includes an N-terminal, cleavable presequence for import, and the entire branched-chain amino acid pathway in yeast is organized in the mitochondrial matrix. The protein therefore resides in and assembles the acetolactate synthase complex (GO:0005948) within mitochondria. The C-terminal interaction platforms can also engage matrix scaffolds associated with mitochondrial nucleoids (GO:0042645), positioning the enzyme near DNA-anchored metabolic hubs. Such placement would create a metabolically privileged microdomain, allowing tight coordination of precursor supply and local energy/redox status with biosynthetic demand.

A mechanistic model emerges: the small subunit forms a dimer via the ACT-like core, presenting two regulatory surfaces that clamp onto catalytic subunits. Valine binds the ACT pocket to stabilize an inhibited conformation that propagates through the C-terminal interface to partially close the catalytic active site, while ATP binding at the interface stabilizes an open, catalytically competent state. The assembled complex draws substrates from proximal providers—threonine dehydratase (for 2-ketobutyrate) and central pyruvate pools—and hands off products to ketol-acid reductoisomerase and dihydroxy-acid dehydratase, with branch-specific association to the leucine enzymes (2-isopropylmalate synthase isoforms, 3-isopropylmalate dehydratase, 3-isopropylmalate dehydrogenase). Transient contacts with 2-hydroxyacyl-CoA lyase could integrate ketoacid balance. This arrangement rationalizes the hypothesized partners: the acetolactate synthase catalytic subunit, ketol-acid reductoisomerase, threonine dehydratase, dihydroxy-acid dehydratase, 2-isopropylmalate synthase and its mitochondrial isoform, 3-isopropylmalate dehydratase, 3-isopropylmalate dehydrogenase, and 2-hydroxyacyl-CoA lyase. Together they form a mitochondrial matrix metabolon anchored near nucleoids, enabling efficient, feedback-controlled synthesis of branched-chain amino acids.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A mitochondrial regulatory protein that dimerizes through an ACT-fold core and couples to a thiamine-dependent decarboxylase to form the enzyme that commits pyruvate or 2-ketobutyrate to branched‑chain amino acid synthesis. Binding of valine to the ACT pocket enforces an inhibited state, whereas ATP biases the active conformation, resulting in strong stimulation of catalytic output and feedback control of pathway entry. The complex operates in the mitochondrial matrix and likely associates with nucleoid-linked scaffolds, organizing a metabolon with downstream enzymes to channel intermediates efficiently.

row.protein_function: Functions in nuclear protein import as nuclear transport receptor. Serves as receptor for nuclear localization signals (NLS) in cargo substrates. Is thought to mediate docking of the importin/substrate complex to the nuclear pore complex (NPC) through binding to nucleoporin and the complex is subsequently translocated through the pore by an energy requiring, Ran-dependent mechanism. At the nucleoplasmic side of the NPC, Ran binds to the importin, the importin/substrate complex dissociates and importin is re-exported from the nucleus to the cytoplasm where GTP hydrolysis releases Ran. The directionality of nuclear import is thought to be conferred by an asymmetric distribution of the GTP- and GDP-bound forms of Ran between the cytoplasm and nucleus (By similarity). Mediates the nuclear import of UBC9, the RBM8A/MAGOH complex, PAX6 and probably other members of the paired homeobox family. Also mediates nuclear export of eIF-1A, and the cytoplasmic release of eIF-1A is triggered by the loading of import substrates onto IPO13 (By similarity).

row.reasoning:
A continuous helical solenoid dominates the sequence from residues 18–963, captured by IPR011989 (Armadillo-like helical homologous superfamily) and reinforced by IPR016024 (Armadillo-type fold, residues 25–805). Membership in IPR051345 (Importin beta-like nuclear transport receptors family, residues 20–962) establishes this molecule as a karyopherin built from stacked HEAT/ARM-like repeats that create a flexible, concave binding groove for short, basic, and non-classical nuclear localization signals and for Ran. The N-terminus contains IPR001494 (Importin-beta, N-terminal domain, residues 45–111) followed by IPR013598 (Exportin-1/Importin-beta-like domain, residues 117–262). This N-terminal clamp is the hallmark Ran-GTP docking module of importin-β-like receptors, and its conformational coupling to the helical superstructure controls cargo affinity and release. Embedded within the central and C-terminal body are Importin-13–specific repeat modules: IPR057941 (Transportin-3/Importin-13, second TPR domain, residues 301–398), IPR040709 (Importin 13, repeat 1, residues 402–441), IPR057942 (Transportin-3/Importin-13, third TPR domain, residues 503–562), IPR040944 (Importin 13 repeat 2, residues 584–672), and IPR040520 (Importin 13 repeat, residues 826–899). These helical hairpins sculpt cargo-selective grooves and FG-nucleoporin-binding surfaces, conferring the recognition profile characteristic of Importin-13.

This architecture causes a Ran-regulated transport cycle: in the cytoplasm, the Ran-free N-terminal clamp leaves the mid-body Importin-13 repeats accessible, promoting high-affinity capture of NLS-bearing or non-classical cargos such as UBC9 and the RBM8A/MAGOH complex, as well as paired-homeobox factors like PAX6. The HEAT/ARM groove also affords direct contacts with nuclear receptors; thus, the same structural logic supports nuclear glucocorticoid receptor binding (the concept formalized as GO:0035259), with the receptor’s basic hinge/LBD segments docking along the concave groove. Once cargo is bound, the solenoid exposes hydrophobic stripes that engage FG-repeat nucleoporins, docking the import complex at the nuclear pore and threading it through the channel. In the nucleus, Ran-GTP binds the IPR001494–IPR013598 N-terminal module, driving an allosteric closure of the groove that ejects import cargo. The receptor then returns to the cytoplasm as a Ran-GTP complex; cytosolic RanGAP-catalyzed hydrolysis resets the receptor for another round. This Ran-gradient–driven directionality and cargo capture/release cycle instantiate protein import into nucleus (GO:0006606).

Importin-13 is unusual in that the same solenoid can also assemble a Ran-GTP–dependent export complex with eIF1A inside the nucleus; upon reaching the cytoplasm, loading of new import substrates onto the mid-body repeats allosterically triggers eIF1A release. This bidirectional coupling is a direct consequence of the modular HEAT/ARM repeats and N-terminal Ran switch.

The cycle inherently shuttles the receptor between the cytoplasm (GO:0005737) and nucleus (GO:0005634), consistent with the absence of transmembrane segments and with the solenoid’s need to contact FG-nucleoporins at the pore. Interaction logic follows from the mechanism: GTP-binding nuclear protein Ran binds the N-terminal clamp in the nucleus; Importin subunit beta-1 and Exportin-5, as fellow karyopherins, compete for FG-repeat sites and share Ran-GTP, creating regulatory crosstalk; Importin subunit alpha isoforms (including alpha-5) may transiently engage the receptor or its cargos during handoff of classical NLS clients; SUMO-conjugating enzyme UBC9 and RNA-binding protein 8A (RBM8A) bind the Importin-13–specific repeat grooves as direct import cargos; and Snurportin-1, an import adaptor for m3G-capped RNPs, likely interfaces at the pore or in cargo exchange hubs. Together, these interactions fit a transport receptor whose HEAT/ARM architecture determines nuclear cargo binding, Ran-dependent directionality, and shuttling between cytoplasm and nucleus.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A shuttling nuclear transport receptor that uses a flexible HEAT/ARM solenoid to capture cargo proteins in the cytoplasm, dock to FG-repeat nucleoporins, and deliver cargo into the nucleus under control of a Ran-GTP switch. It binds nuclear receptors such as the glucocorticoid receptor and imports cargos including UBC9 and the RBM8A/MAGOH complex, while also exporting eIF1A from the nucleus. Directionality arises from the Ran-GTP/GDP gradient, with cargo release and receptor recycling occurring through allosteric changes at the N-terminal Ran-binding clamp. The protein cycles between the cytoplasm and nucleus as it couples import and export rounds.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture centers on three coherent InterPro signatures that define a soluble P-loop NTPase specialized for RNA-associated remodeling. A broad family envelope, IPR051451 (PhoH2-like, residues 4–325), spans almost the entire polypeptide, indicating ancestry to the bacterial PhoH/PhoH2 clade. Nested within this, the catalytic core is specified twice: IPR027417 (P-loop containing nucleoside triphosphate hydrolase homologous superfamily, residues 115–321) and IPR003714 (PhoH-like protein domain, residues 118–321). The overlap of IPR027417 and IPR003714 in the 115–321 region places the Walker A/B P-loop NTP-binding/hydrolysis machinery directly within the PhoH-like fold, a fold known to bias catalysis toward nucleic acid-associated NTP hydrolysis rather than generic phosphorylation chemistry. The N-terminus (approximately residues 1–114) lies outside the annotated P-loop core but inside the family shell, consistent with a low-complexity or helical region that often serves as an adapter surface for partner proteins.

This domain ordering causally establishes the molecular functions. The P-loop region requires nucleotide engagement and hydrolysis, so ATP binding and ATP hydrolysis activity follow from the Walker A/B architecture. The extended family envelope and N-terminal segment create a platform for recruiting cofactors, supporting a general protein binding capacity (GO:0005515). The PhoH-like core has been associated with RNA-dependent activation in related proteins; this points to an NTPase whose activity is stimulated by RNA or RNA-protein complexes rather than by free nucleotides alone.

The biological role becomes clearer when this catalytic framework is placed alongside the observed partner spectrum, which is rich in RNA quality-control factors. Endoribonuclease YbeY is a central 16S rRNA maturation and ribosome quality-control nuclease. Ribonuclease R is a 3′–5′ exoribonuclease that degrades highly structured RNAs. RNA-binding protein Hfq is an RNA chaperone that organizes sRNA–mRNA pairs and hands them to ribonucleases for decay. UPF0250 protein YbeD often associates genetically and physically with small-subunit RNA maturation modules. These contacts collectively require the exposure and remodeling of RNA-protein assemblies to permit endonucleolytic and exonucleolytic access. An RNA-activated P-loop ATPase such as a PhoH-like enzyme can provide exactly this remodeling energy: ATP binding at residues 115–321 loads the core, and hydrolysis drives conformational changes that dissociate chaperones (e.g., Hfq) or reshape structured RNAs, thereby licensing cleavage by YbeY and processive degradation by RNase R. The tRNA-2-methylthio-N–linked enzyme suggests an additional interface with tRNA modification pathways; an NTPase that remodels RNPs could help surveil or triage hypomodified tRNAs by exposing them to exonucleases. The association with ATP-dependent Clp protease subunit ClpX further integrates protein homeostasis: following ribosome/RNA quality control, misassembled ribosomal proteins can be passed to ClpXP for proteolysis. Contacts with Apolipoprotein N-acyltransferase and the magnesium/cobalt efflux protein CorC are consistent with stress-coupled crosstalk: when envelope or ion-stress programs are active, the cytosolic NTPase can tune RNA turnover to reprogram translation toward stress-appropriate targets.

The absence of transmembrane segments and the soluble P-loop fold endorse a cytosolic location in Escherichia coli K-12. This aligns with GO:0005829 cytosol and the generic bacterial “cytoplasm” annotation, placing the enzyme where ribosomes, Hfq-sRNA complexes, and RNA decay machineries operate.

Mechanistically, the protein most plausibly functions as an RNA-activated ATPase that binds RNA-protein complexes via its N-terminal adapter surface (family region, residues 4–114) and engages nucleotides within the PhoH/P-loop core (residues 115–321). ATP hydrolysis drives local strand separation or factor displacement, exposing RNA substrates to YbeY and RNase R for cleavage and decay, while coordination with Hfq manages substrate selection. In parallel, handoffs to ClpX help couple RNA quality control to protein quality control. This energy-coupled remodeling model accounts for the observed interaction network and explains how a PhoH-like NTPase advances RNA catabolic processes and rRNA processing from a cytosolic position.

From these causal steps: molecular functions include ATP binding and ATP hydrolysis alongside protein binding (GO:0005515); biological processes center on RNA catabolic process and rRNA processing; the cellular component is the cytosol (GO:0005829).
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A soluble bacterial P-loop NTPase that uses ATP hydrolysis to remodel RNA–protein assemblies in the cytosol, thereby exposing structured RNAs to endo- and exonucleases for maturation or decay. Its N-terminal adapter surface likely recruits RNA-processing factors, while the PhoH-like catalytic core provides the energy to displace RNA chaperones and unwind local structures, coordinating with ribosome and tRNA quality-control pathways and linking RNA surveillance to proteostasis under stress.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is anchored by IPR052600 (Nuclear receptor coactivator/corepressor family) spanning residues 1–536, covering essentially the full polypeptide. Family membership defines a transcriptional coregulator scaffold that engages nuclear receptors via short linear motifs and recruits chromatin-modifying enzymes and the transcription machinery. These proteins typically lack catalytic domains; instead, their modular surfaces mediate protein–protein and nucleic-acid interactions that tune transcriptional outputs in response to ligands.

Embedded within this scaffold, residues 167–283 form IPR036621 (Anticodon-binding domain superfamily). Although classically found in aminoacyl‑tRNA synthetases, this superfamily confers a compact nucleic-acid–binding fold with basic patches that can engage structured nucleic acids. In a nuclear receptor coregulator context, such a fold can provide direct nucleic-acid affinity, allowing the protein to grip DNA near receptor response elements or contact nascent RNA to stabilize transcriptional assemblies. This architecture causally explains DNA association and supports the molecular function formalized as GO:0003677 DNA binding. Given the defining family assignment, the same architecture also produces transcription coregulator activity by physically bridging nuclear receptors with enzymatic coactivators or corepressors.

Because nuclear receptors control RNA polymerase II promoters and enhancers, a coregulator that binds both receptors and DNA necessarily participates in regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II. The family label further ties its role to hormone- and lipid‑responsive nuclear receptor signaling. Thus, the most fitting biological processes are regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II and participation in nuclear receptor signaling pathways.

DNA engagement and receptor docking require nuclear residence. Coregulators of this type accumulate in the nucleoplasm at hormone response elements, associate with chromatin, and transiently occupy transcription regulator complexes at active or repressed loci. Therefore, the cellular component is best inferred as nucleus, with association to chromatin and transcription regulator complexes.

A mechanistic model follows from this arrangement. In Atlantic salmon, ligand-activated nuclear receptors (for example, estrogen, glucocorticoid, or PPAR receptors) bind their DNA response elements. This coregulator docks to the receptor activation surface through family-typical motifs, while its anticodon-binding–like module stabilizes the assembly on nucleic acid. In the activated state, it recruits histone acetyltransferases (CBP/EP300), Mediator subunits (such as MED1), and the RNA polymerase II preinitiation complex to stimulate transcription. Under unliganded or antagonist conditions, the same scaffold can shift to a repressive configuration by engaging corepressor machinery (NCOR1/HDAC3), compacting chromatin and reducing initiation. This dual-mode, nucleic-acid–anchored assembly explains both DNA binding and coregulator function, and predicts transient interactions with nuclear receptors and chromatin-modifying complexes.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A nuclear transcriptional coregulator in Atlantic salmon that assembles on hormone response elements by docking to ligand-regulated nuclear receptors and directly contacting nucleic acids via a compact binding fold. It alternates between coactivator and corepressor modes, recruiting chromatin-modifying enzymes and Mediator to modulate RNA polymerase II initiation and enhancer activity in response to endocrine cues.

row.reasoning:
A single, continuous beta-propeller scaffold is implied by the stacked WD40 signatures spanning essentially the entire polypeptide: IPR001680 (WD40 repeat, residues 1–302) establishes the repeating blade architecture; IPR020472 (PAC1/LIS1-like, WD-40 repeat, residues 27–301) refines the repeat subtype; and two superfamily annotations, IPR015943 (WD40/YVTN repeat-like-containing domain superfamily, residues 3–314) and IPR036322 (WD40-repeat-containing domain superfamily, residues 13–305), confirm a toroidal, solvent-exposed propeller surface optimized for multivalent protein interfaces rather than catalysis. IPR037363 (Sec13/Seh1 family, residues 8–316) places this propeller within the clade of coat-like WD40 proteins that create curvature and rigid edges in macromolecular assemblies such as the nuclear pore outer ring and nutrient-sensing complexes. The ordered architecture—an N-to-C array of WD repeats composing a compact propeller within the Sec13/Seh1 family—causes high-affinity, geometry-specific docking of partner subunits and equips the protein to serve as a structural module in large complexes.

This domain architecture dictates molecular function as a protein-interface scaffold. At the nuclear pore, Sec13/Seh1-family propellers act as structural constituents that brace the outer ring; thus the propeller here confers structural constituent of the nuclear pore. Because WD40 blades are interaction platforms, the molecule necessarily exhibits broad protein binding, using multiple blades to capture different partners and to stabilize composite edges within assemblies.

From those molecular activities, two biological programs become direct consequences. First, as a stable element of the nuclear pore coat, the propeller supports envelope integrity and basket anchoring. It maintains the localization of the large coiled-coil nucleoporin Mtor at the nuclear envelope in early meiotic female germ cells, not by initiating NPC recruitment of Mtor, Nup107, Nup153, or FG-nucleoporins, but by preserving the outer-ring geometry to which the nuclear basket attaches. This structural maintenance role, acting during oogenesis, impacts germarium-derived egg chamber formation and the regulation of meiotic and mitotic cell cycles by securing nucleo-cytoplasmic transport capacity as cells grow and divide.

Second, the Sec13/Seh1 family annotation also explains participation in the GATOR2 complex, a nutrient-sensing scaffold that sits at the lysosomal membrane to control the Rag-TORC1 axis. As a WD40 propeller within GATOR2, the protein stabilizes subunit contacts (for example with Wdr59 and Mio) to potentiate positive regulation of TORC1 signaling. In nutrient-replete conditions, this promotes anabolic growth, linking the scaffold to positive regulation of cell size and to cell-cycle progression. Under amino acid starvation, the same platform engages the cellular response to amino acid starvation, tuning TORC1 down or modulating feedback pathways so that autophagic flux can be either restrained or enabled as needed. The dual annotations for negative regulation of autophagy and positive regulation of autophagy reflect context-dependent control around the autolysosome: when TORC1 is active the scaffold suppresses autophagy; when TORC1 is curtailed during starvation, the scaffolded complex can facilitate the autophagic program’s execution and resolution.

These activities place the protein in two cellular venues demanded by its architecture and partners. As a nuclear pore structural constituent, it concentrates at the nuclear envelope, especially in nurse cells, oocytes, and syncytial embryos where transport capacity must scale with rapid growth. As a GATOR2 subunit, it associates with the cytosolic face of the lysosome and becomes detectable within autolysosomes in egg chambers, aligning with its role in nutrient sensing and autophagy control.

A coherent interaction network follows from this model. The propeller’s blade edges engage outer-ring nucleoporins such as Nup160, Nup133, Nup75, Nup37, and the small Nup43, and interface with the dynamic Nup98–Nup96 pair to stabilize pore architecture that anchors Mtor. Within the nutrient-sensing arm, the same scaffolded surfaces bind GATOR2 partners Wdr59 and Mio, while positioning near GATOR1 component NPRL2 to modulate Rag signaling into TORC1. The Sec13/Seh1 family relationship further rationalizes association with Protein SEC13 homolog: the two propeller paralogs often form analogous edge elements across coat complexes. Together, this WD40 propeller toggles between two assemblies—NPC and GATOR2—to couple nuclear transport stability with lysosome-centered growth control, thereby coordinating cell size, cell-cycle progression, and autophagy during oogenesis and starvation responses.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A WD40 beta-propeller scaffold that serves as a structural element of the nuclear pore coat while also functioning within the lysosome-associated GATOR2 complex to regulate nutrient signaling. At the nuclear envelope it stabilizes the outer ring to maintain the nuclear basket nucleoporin Mtor during early female meiosis, supporting transport capacity needed for egg chamber formation and orderly cell-cycle transitions. At the lysosome–autolysosome interface it assembles GATOR2 to promote TORC1 activity in nutrient-replete conditions and modulate autophagy and starvation responses, thereby influencing cell size and proliferation.

row.reasoning:
The sequence is dominated by peptidase C13 signatures: IPR001096 (Peptidase C13, legumain family, residues 6–438) and IPR043577 (Asparaginyl endopeptidase family, residues 11–438) span essentially the full length of the polypeptide, defining a cysteine protease core that uses a catalytic Cys–His dyad to hydrolyze peptide bonds. Within this framework, the C-terminal region contains an autoinhibitory prodomain: IPR046427 (Legumain prodomain superfamily, residues 312–437) and IPR048501 (Legumain, prodomain, residues 314–434). This architecture places the inhibitory LSAM-like prodomain after the catalytic module, a hallmark of legumain zymogens. Such prodomains occlude the active site until exposure to acidic conditions triggers autoactivation via specific cleavage events, thereby gating proteolysis to the lumen of acidic organelles.

This layout specifies the molecular function. The C13 fold and the family-level asparaginyl endopeptidase signatures impose strict P1 specificity for Asn residues, so the enzyme catalyzes peptide-bond hydrolysis after asparagine. Because the chemistry is executed by the nucleophilic cysteine within the conserved legumain active site, the appropriate molecular functions are cysteine-type endopeptidase activity and, more specifically, asparaginyl endopeptidase activity. The prodomain-regulated, acid-triggered activation further implies that activity is tuned to the endolysosomal pH range; at near-neutral pH the same active site can favor transpeptidation/ligation, but in vivo the dominant role is endopeptidic cleavage in acidic compartments.

From molecular function, a role in broader biology follows. Endolysosomal cysteine proteases drive protein turnover, matrix remodeling, and activation of latent precursors. During tissue regeneration, these activities clear damaged components and unmask or activate growth and guidance cues, aligning with tissue regeneration (GO:0042246). In the nervous system, targeted cleavage of inhibitory extracellular matrix and processing of pro-forms of neurotrophic factors or receptors can reduce barriers to axon extension and reestablish permissive environments, aligning with axon regeneration (GO:0031103). These processes are natural consequences of a legumain-type protease that is selectively active where damage-induced endocytosis, phagolysosomal processing, and extracellular matrix turnover are intense.

The prodomain-locked zymogen design that activates under acid strongly points to the endolysosomal system as the primary location. Legumain-family proteases are synthesized into the secretory pathway and sorted to lysosomes, where the acidic lumen promotes prodomain removal and stable maturation. Therefore, the cellular component is best placed in lysosome; given its biosynthetic route and activation, it is also reasonable to expect presence in late endosomes that fuse with lysosomes. A fraction may be secreted or released during injury, but the controlling feature of the architecture is to confine catalysis to acidic compartments.

Mechanistically, the protein is produced as a zymogen whose C-terminal prodomain masks the active site. Delivery to an acidic compartment allows autoproteolysis at defined Asn sites, releasing the prodomain and generating the active enzyme. The active legumain then cleaves substrates after Asn, contributing to matrix remodeling and activation of proenzymes and growth factors essential for regeneration. Likely interaction partners therefore include luminal trafficking and maturation factors (such as cation-independent mannose-6-phosphate receptor for sorting and LAMP1/2 as lysosomal scaffolds), fellow lysosomal proteases whose activation cascades it can influence (cathepsin L and cathepsin B), and endogenous inhibitors that titrate its activity (cystatin family members). Through these associations, the enzyme can couple endolysosomal proteolysis to the cellular programs that enable tissue and axon regrowth.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: An acid-activated cysteine protease that is synthesized as a zymogen with a C‑terminal autoinhibitory segment and becomes active upon prodomain removal in acidic compartments. Once matured, it cleaves peptide bonds specifically after asparagine residues, driving endolysosomal proteolysis that clears damaged material, remodels extracellular matrices, and activates latent factors needed for repair, thereby supporting tissue and axon regeneration. Its activity is likely concentrated in lysosomes and late endosomes, with potential transient presence along the secretory–endolysosomal route during trafficking and maturation.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by a single small signaling module: a WW domain precisely mapped by IPR001202 (WW domain, residues 46–80) and encompassed by the broader IPR036020 (WW domain superfamily, residues 45–81). A WW domain is a compact, ~35–40 residue β-sheet module specialized for recognizing short proline-rich motifs (notably PPxY/PY motifs) and, in some WW classes, phospho-Ser/Thr–Pro dipeptides. This compact recognition fold lacks catalytic residues and instead functions as a protein–protein interaction adaptor. The tight overlap of the specific WW domain (IPR001202) within the superfamily annotation (IPR036020) indicates that the core binding module is intact and positioned near the N-terminus, enabling it to act as an interaction hook that recruits partners bearing PY or related motifs.

Because the WW domain enforces motif-specific docking, the primary molecular function resolves to protein binding via modular motif recognition rather than enzymatic chemistry. The module’s binding mode is compatible with engaging low-complexity regions, including polyproline- and glutamine-enriched stretches frequently found in regulatory proteins. This binding logic supports GO:0005515 protein binding as the central molecular function.

A WW-mediated adaptor commonly organizes assemblies that couple transcriptional regulators and RNA processing factors. The stated subcellular residency in nuclear speckles and cytoplasmic granules provides key biological context. Nuclear speckles are reservoirs and assembly sites for pre-mRNA splicing and mRNP maturation factors; thus, a WW-domain scaffold concentrated there would help choreograph spliceosomal regulators and auxiliary factors, driving steps encompassed by GO:0006397 mRNA processing and GO:0008380 RNA splicing. The presence in cytoplasmic granules implies an additional role in post-transcriptional RNP remodeling or storage, consistent with shuttling or triaging mRNPs between nuclear assembly zones and cytoplasmic RNA granule environments.

The cellular component can therefore be assigned to nuclear specks (GO:0016607), consistent with the observed speckled nuclear distribution, and extended to cytoplasmic ribonucleoprotein granules where modular adaptors often participate in mRNP condensation and turnover. The absence of transmembrane segments and enzymatic domains further supports a soluble, scaffold-like role that relies on docking multiple partners rather than catalysis.

Mechanistically, the protein likely uses its WW domain to capture PY-motif–containing splicing regulators and speckle scaffolds in the nucleus, stabilizing assemblies that promote splice site selection or mRNP maturation. In the cytoplasm, the same interaction logic would recruit RNA granule scaffolds and RNA-binding proteins, modulating the condensation state and fate of specific mRNPs. Candidate partners include speckle-resident splicing factors (e.g., SR proteins and U2/U5-associated factors) and cytoplasmic granule scaffolds (e.g., stress granule nucleators). This adaptor capacity also rationalizes an ability to engage low-complexity segments such as glutamine-rich tracts in regulatory proteins, which would favor partitioning into speckles and granules via multivalent, sequence-context–dependent contacts.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A small human adaptor protein built around a single WW interaction module that binds short proline-rich and related low-complexity motifs to assemble ribonucleoprotein complexes. By concentrating in nuclear speckles, it likely scaffolds factors that execute pre-mRNA processing and splicing. Its presence in cytoplasmic granules suggests a complementary role in organizing or remodeling mRNPs during post-transcriptional control. The mechanism centers on motif-directed docking via the WW domain to nucleate dynamic protein assemblies rather than catalyzing reactions.

row.interpro_formatted: - IPR006373: Variant surface antigen Rifin (family) [1-350]
row.go_bp_leaf: GO:0020033 antigenic variation
row.reasoning:
The sequence is entirely encompassed by a single InterPro family signature, IPR006373 (Variant surface antigen Rifin, residues 1–350). A family-wide signature spanning essentially the full length indicates that this polypeptide adopts the canonical RIFIN architecture: an N-terminal secretory signal followed by an export motif, a large variable luminal/extracellular region, and a C-terminal transmembrane anchor. This layout is optimized for display on the surface of the host erythrocyte rather than for catalysis; the small size and absence of independent catalytic domains argue that its primary role is to present a variable binding surface to external receptors.

A single-pass membrane anchor coupled to a hypervariable ectodomain causally enables multivalent binding to host molecules. Such a surface-exposed variable region is designed to engage receptors on immune cells or on neighboring erythrocytes, which defines molecular activities in the space of GO:0005515 protein binding and, more specifically, GO:0005102 receptor binding. The binding activity is not an incidental property; it is the central mechanism by which RIFINs exert function—by contacting host inhibitory or adhesion receptors to alter immune sensing and cell–cell interactions.

Switchable expression of many rifin family members generates alternating ectodomain sequences at the erythrocyte surface. This expression switching produces changing epitopes over time, which is the operational basis of GO:0020033 antigenic variation. By repeatedly remodeling the exposed binding surface, the parasite evades adaptive immune recognition while retaining the capacity to bind alternative host receptors as needed during infection.

The export-competent signal architecture implies trafficking from the parasite endomembrane system across the parasitophorous vacuole via the translocon and delivery to host-cell membranous structures before final insertion into the erythrocyte membrane. Consequently, the functional site of action is the host interface, consistent with GO:0020002 host cell plasma membrane. At this location, the variable ectodomain can contact leukocyte inhibitory receptors or erythrocyte surface glycoconjugates to modulate immunity and promote cytoadhesion or rosetting.

A mechanistic model follows from this architecture: in Plasmodium falciparum (isolate 3D7), the protein is synthesized in the parasite, exported through the PTEX machinery, trafficked via parasite-induced secretory organelles to the host cell plasma membrane, and anchored by its C-terminal transmembrane segment. The exposed ectodomain then binds host receptors (e.g., inhibitory immune receptors) to dampen host responses while contributing to antigenic diversification. Parasite chaperones and trafficking factors likely shepherd the protein through export and membrane insertion, while co-display with other variant antigens organizes higher-order adhesive assemblies. Probable partners therefore include the parasite’s export/translocon components for trafficking, cytoskeletal/knob organizers for membrane presentation, co-variant antigens for cooperative display, and host receptors on leukocytes and erythrocytes for functional binding.

By integrating the full-length IPR006373 signature, the inferred single-pass surface architecture, and the requirement for antigenic switching, the most parsimonious functional assignment is a host-facing, receptor-binding variant surface antigen that drives antigenic variation at the infected erythrocyte surface and modulates host–parasite interactions at the host cell plasma membrane.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A parasite-exported, single-pass surface antigen displayed on infected erythrocytes that presents a variable extracellular region to bind host receptors. Through this binding, it modulates host immunity and promotes phenotypic switching at the cell surface, enabling immune evasion via alternating antigenic forms. The protein is inserted into the host cell plasma membrane after parasite-directed export and trafficking, where its receptor interactions drive the essential biology of antigenic variation.

row.go_bp_leaf: GO:0016480 negative regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase III
row.go_mf_leaf: GO:0000994 RNA polymerase III core binding
row.reasoning:
The sequence shows a compact regulatory architecture anchored by two Maf1-specific signatures. IPR015257 (Repressor of RNA polymerase III transcription Maf1 family, residues 1–389) spans essentially the full polypeptide, indicating a single-chain regulator built around a conserved core and flexible terminal regions rather than a modular enzyme. Nested within it, IPR038564 (Maf1 superfamily, residues 192–339) marks the structurally conserved core that forms the principal interaction surface. The absence of catalytic motifs and the dominance of a lineage-specific regulatory core argue that the protein acts through high-affinity protein–protein interactions and conformational control rather than chemistry.

A Maf1-family core positioned mid-chain typically presents a negatively charged, evolutionarily conserved surface that docks directly onto the central body of the small-RNA transcription polymerase. This architecture causally supports RNA polymerase III core binding, formalized as GO:0000994. The flanking segments likely tune avidity and regulation (e.g., by phosphorylation) while the core domain engages the polymerase’s clamp/cleft surfaces. Occupation of that interface sterically and allosterically blocks productive initiation, which in turn prevents the basal initiation factor from assembling on the promoter. Consequently, the same architecture enforces negative regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase III (GO:0016480). Mechanistically, when the core is bound to the polymerase, the preinitiation complex cannot form because de novo loading of the TFIIIB complex onto DNA is inhibited.

Cellular geography follows from this regulatory logic. The repressor must access the transcription machinery at rDNA/tRNA gene clusters, so it transits to the nucleus and concentrates at the nucleolus, consistent with GO:0005730 nucleolus. Yet the presence of extensive regulatory segments implies signal-responsive sequestration away from chromatin; phosphorylation-dependent masking of nuclear targeting or polymerase-binding interfaces would retain the protein in the cytoplasm (GO:0005737 cytoplasm) when repression is not needed. The stated subcellular distribution between cytoplasm and nucleus reflects this regulated shuttling.

The interaction network emerges naturally. Direct docking to the polymerase core implicates contacts with the largest and second-largest core subunits (RPC1 and RPC2), while additional surfaces across the polymerase periphery (RPC3, RPC4, RPC5, RPC6, RPC7 and the shared RPAC1) provide auxiliary binding sites that stabilize an inhibitory clamp over the active-center cleft. Because the repressor prevents de novo TFIIIB assembly onto DNA, it is poised to contact the 70 kDa TFIIIB subunit during preinitiation complex formation, either displacing it or preventing its productive orientation. Upstream modulation by a serine/threonine kinase such as SCH9 provides the signal-integration layer: phosphorylation weakens polymerase binding and promotes cytoplasmic retention, whereas dephosphorylation allows nuclear/nucleolar accumulation and potent repression. In sum, a conserved Maf1 core domain binds the polymerase to suppress transcription of tRNA and other small RNA genes by preventing TFIIIB assembly.

This chain of inference incorporates the domain architecture (IPR015257 [1–389], IPR038564 [192–339]) to deduce RNA polymerase III core binding (GO:0000994) as the molecular function, links that binding to negative regulation of polymerase III transcription (GO:0016480) as the biological process, and explains the observed cytoplasmic and nucleolar localizations (GO:0005737, GO:0005730) via phosphorylation-controlled nucleocytoplasmic shuttling that tunes repression strength. It naturally predicts physical and regulatory interactions with polymerase III subunits (RPC1, RPC2, RPC3, RPC4, RPC5, RPC6, RPC7, RPAC1), the TFIIIB 70 kDa subunit at promoters, and the kinase SCH9 as an upstream controller of activity state.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A conserved, phosphorylation-regulated repressor that binds the core of the polymerase dedicated to tRNA and other small noncoding RNA genes, blocking assembly of the basal initiation factor at promoters and thereby shutting down transcription. Its activity is tuned by nutrient/stress signaling that controls nucleocytoplasmic shuttling: when phosphorylated it is retained in the cytoplasm, and when dephosphorylated it accumulates at nucleolar transcription sites to clamp the polymerase in a non-initiating state.

row.reasoning:
The sequence encodes a classic cytochrome P450 monooxygenase based on a tightly nested architecture of P450 signatures. The fold-level scaffold appears as IPR036396 (Cytochrome P450 superfamily, residues 18–492), within which sits the canonical P450 family core IPR001128 (Cytochrome P450, residues 70–482) and IPR050196 (Cytochrome P450 Monooxygenases, residues 11–489). The catalytic region characteristic of eukaryotic microsomal P450s is further specified by IPR002401 (Cytochrome P450, E-class, group I, residues 288–459), and the heme-thiolate catalytic motif is pinpointed by IPR017972 (Cytochrome P450, conserved site, residues 429–438). This composition and ordering establishes an enzyme that binds a heme cofactor via the conserved cysteine ligand, activates O2, and inserts one oxygen atom into a hydrophobic substrate while reducing the second atom to water. The E-class, group I signature indicates the electron delivery route typically used by microsomal P450s, whereby electrons flow from NADPH to the heme via a diflavin reductase.

These features causally define the molecular function: the heme-binding conserved site (IPR017972) entails GO:0020037 heme binding, while the P450 monooxygenase fold (IPR001128, IPR050196) entails GO:0004497 monooxygenase activity. The chemistry is an O2-dependent hydroxylation requiring two electrons, matching GO:0016705 oxidoreductase activity, acting on paired donors, with incorporation or reduction of molecular oxygen.

The substrate class most consistent with this fold in Caenorhabditis is lipids. P450 monooxygenases commonly hydroxylate fatty acids, very-long-chain lipids, sterols, and related hydrophobic metabolites. The interaction set reinforces this: NADPH–cytochrome P450 reductase is the canonical redox partner supplying electrons to microsomal P450s; fatty acid synthase partners (present twice, implying strong or repeated capture of this association) provide nascent acyl chains that can be directly hydroxylated; and a nematode cuticle collagen N-terminal domain-containing protein places the enzyme at a biosynthetic hub feeding extracellular matrix and surface structures. Lipid hydroxylation creates functionalized building blocks for structural lipids and signaling molecules. Such products plausibly underlie GO:0008610 lipid biosynthetic process and mechanistically connect to developmental outcomes. Hydroxylated lipids and sterol derivatives shape membrane properties and signaling gradients that guide GO:0048598 embryonic morphogenesis and GO:0008356 asymmetric cell division. During oocyte maturation and meiosis, localized membrane remodeling and signaling lipids are essential for GO:0040038 polar body extrusion after meiotic divisions. In the embryo, specialized lipid crosslinking and barrier formation contribute to GO:0030703 eggshell formation; P450-catalyzed modifications can supply oxidized lipid precursors for this impermeable layer.

The E-class, group I architecture and reliance on NADPH–cytochrome P450 reductase set the cellular location. Microsomal P450s are monotopically embedded in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) membrane via an N-terminal signal-anchor segment that positions the catalytic domain cytosolically. Although the transmembrane helix is not explicitly annotated here, the domain context and obligate reductase partner causally place the enzyme as an integral component of membrane, specifically the endoplasmic reticulum membrane.

Mechanistically, the protein most likely forms a metabolon on the ER with NADPH–cytochrome P450 reductase for electron transfer and with fatty acid synthase to intercept elongating acyl chains for regio- and stereo-selective hydroxylation. The oxidized products then feed into pathways producing eggshell and cuticle lipids and into lipid signals that bias spindle positioning and cortical flows during asymmetric cell division and support membrane scission events during polar body extrusion. Contacts with the nematode cuticle collagen N-terminal domain-containing protein suggest channeling of modified lipids into collagen-associated extracellular matrix assembly during embryonic morphogenesis and eggshell/cuticle maturation.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A membrane-associated heme–thiolate monooxygenase in C. elegans that uses electrons from a diflavin reductase to activate oxygen and hydroxylate hydrophobic lipid substrates. By decorating fatty acid and related lipid intermediates on the endoplasmic reticulum surface, it supplies functionalized building blocks and signals required for lipid biogenesis, eggshell and cuticle-associated matrix assembly, and developmental events such as asymmetric division, morphogenesis, and polar body extrusion.

row.reasoning:
The sequence architecture is dominated by keratin/intermediate-filament signatures. A broad family assignment, IPR002957 (Keratin, type I family) spans residues 9–478, indicating an acidic type I keratin that obligately pairs with a basic type II keratin to assemble 10-nm intermediate filaments. Nested within this, IPR039008 (Intermediate filament, rod domain) from residues 118–433 defines the central coiled-coil rod. This heptad-repeat rod domain enforces parallel coiled-coil heterodimerization with a type II partner and aligns dimers into antiparallel tetramers that laterally and longitudinally anneal into mature filaments. Near the C-terminal end of the rod, IPR018039 (Intermediate filament protein, conserved site) at residues 419–427 marks the conserved IF consensus motif that stabilizes end-to-end association and governs docking to cytolinker complexes. The architecture lacks transmembrane segments or secretion signals, fixing the protein as a cytosolic structural element rather than a membrane or secreted factor.

This domain layout causes two direct molecular activities. First, the extensive coiled-coil rod plus the IF conserved site provide structural constituent activity for the cytoskeleton: once polymerized, the keratin lattice bears tensile load and resists shear. Second, the type I family identity dictates protein heterodimerization activity, because assembly initiates by specific pairing with a type II keratin through the rod domain.

These molecular activities translate into epithelial-level morphogenesis. Keratin filaments crosslink to desmosomes and hemidesmosomes via cytolinkers, distributing mechanical forces across epithelial sheets. In the corneal epithelium, this load-bearing network preserves tissue architecture under constant blinking and environmental stress, thereby driving morphogenesis of an epithelium (GO:0002009) and enabling cornea development in the camera-type eye (GO:0061303). The conserved IF motif (419–427) predicts robust integration into junctional complexes, which is essential for maintaining the corneal epithelial barrier and layered organization.

The absence of targeting signals and the presence of IF-defining motifs constrain cellular location to the intermediate filament system. The protein resides within keratin filaments (GO:0045095), a subset of the intermediate filament cytoskeleton (GO:0005882) in the cytoplasm (GO:0005737).

Mechanistically, the protein forms type I–type II keratin heterodimers that assemble into filaments tethered to desmosomal and hemidesmosomal plaques via adapters such as plectin and desmoplakin. In corneal epithelium, a plausible type II partner is a cornea-enriched keratin (e.g., K3). Through integrin α6β4, BPAG1/dystonin, and collagen XVII/BP180, this network anchors to the basement membrane. Although the stromal proteoglycan keratocan is extracellular, the keratin network can indirectly couple to it through epithelial–basement membrane–stroma linkages, aligning epithelial mechanics with stromal matrix organization. Thus, predicted interaction partners include a type II keratin, junctional cytolinkers, hemidesmosomal integrins, and matrix-anchoring proteins, with keratocan participating indirectly in the same tissue module to coordinate corneal architecture.
row.final_answer:
- Functional Summary: A corneal epithelial intermediate-filament subunit in mouse that assembles as a type I keratin into coiled-coil heterodimers with a type II partner and polymerizes into keratin filaments. These filaments form a cytoplasmic load-bearing network tethered to desmosomes and hemidesmosomes, distributing mechanical forces to maintain epithelial shape, barrier integrity, and tissue layering during corneal development and remodeling.

row.protein_function: UDP-glucuronosyltransferase (UGT) that catalyzes phase II biotransformation reactions in which lipophilic substrates are conjugated with glucuronic acid to increase the metabolite's water solubility, thereby facilitating excretion into either the urine or bile. Essential for the elimination and detoxification of drugs, xenobiotics and endogenous compounds. Catalyzes the glucuronidation of endogenous steroid hormones such as androgens (androsterone, 3alpha-androstanediol) and estrogens (estradiol, estrone). Catalyzes the glucuronidation of bile acid substrates, which are natural detergents for dietary lipids absorption. Displays glucuronidation activity toward the phenolic compounds eugenol.
